podcasts.name: Endgame
podcasts.description: Long-form intellectual conversations with world-class thought leaders, economists, politicians, and academics hosted by Gita Wirjawan — Indonesian entrepreneur and former Trade Minister.

podcasts.name: Dwarkesh Podcast
podcasts.description: Deeply researched, intellectually rigorous long-form interviews with world-leading scientists, historians, economists, and AI researchers.

podcasts.name: View From The Top
podcasts.description: The Stanford Graduate School of Business dean's speaker series — world-class CEOs, founders, and investors in conversation with MBA students on leadership, values, and career lessons.

episodes.title: Peace as a Paradoxical Ideal - Wesley Clark | Endgame #265
episodes.summary: General Wesley Clark discusses the challenges of promoting democracy and peace abroad, particularly in light of America's own history of conflict and intervention. He emphasizes the need for humility and understanding of local contexts, arguing that American democracy cannot simply be transplanted elsewhere. He also touches on the importance of religion and spirituality in maintaining a sense of purpose and meaning, even in the face of war.
key_highlights: Humility is essential for a nation; American democracy can't be transplanted elsewhere
American democracy is like a river, constantly moving and changing
America is a stabilizer, but also involved in too many conflicts
God speaks in many voices; killing over religion is wrong
America's reputation was earned by a different group of people: wealthy people
episodes.quotes: You have to have humility as a nation. You can't pick up American democracy, transplant its laws. Besides, American democracy is like any river—it moves, it changes, the rules change, the attitudes change.

episodes.title: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia’s moat persist?
episodes.summary: Jensen Huang discusses Nvidia's strategy for maintaining its lead in AI computing, emphasizing the importance of a rich ecosystem and the company's ability to innovate at every layer of the AI stack. He argues that Nvidia's commitment to code design and its broad reach across industries and geographies are key to its success. Huang also addresses concerns about competition from custom ASICs, arguing that Nvidia's focus on general programmability and its strong relationships with key partners provide a significant advantage.
key_highlights: Nvidia's moat is its rich ecosystem and ability to innovate at every layer of the AI stack
Nvidia focuses on enabling the transformation of electrons to tokens
Nvidia's goal is to do as much as needed, as little as possible
Nvidia's strength is its ability to drive efficiency through new algorithms
Nvidia does not pick winners, but supports all frameworks
episodes.quotes: "The transformation from electrons to tokens is such an incredible journey... making one token more valuable than another... the amount of artistry, engineering, science, invention that goes into making that token valuable."

episodes.title: David Rubenstein, Co-Founder and Chairman of the Carlyle Group: Pursue Something Meaningful
episodes.guest_title: Co-Founder and Chairman, The Carlyle Group
episodes.summary: David Rubenstein shares his career journey, from a modest background to the White House and co-founding the Carlyle Group. He emphasizes the importance of education, hard work, and adaptability. Rubenstein discusses his approach to interviewing, his passion for reading, and his belief that true happiness comes from doing something useful with one's life. He also touches on the evolution of private equity, the importance of strong leadership, and the need to give back to society.
key_highlights: Be a leader by showing, not just telling
Modest circumstances can be an advantage
Education is a path to economic mobility
Adapt to the situation you have
Personal happiness comes from doing something useful
Don't be afraid to make mistakes
Pursue something more significant than making money
episodes.quotes: "Stanford is a great university, and Stanford Business School is a great business school... a graduate degree here will help you open a lot of doors."

episodes.title: Francis Fukuyama: Is Western Civilization in Decline? | Endgame #263
episodes.summary: Francis Fukuyama argues that the US is in long-term decline, and that the world is adapting to this reality. He suggests the US should focus on domestic reform instead of threatening other countries. He also argues that the best outcome would be for everyone to step back and focus on domestic reform and not threaten other people.
key_highlights: US is in a period of long-term decline
The world is adapting to a US that is not a big player
Trump administration has treated its closest allies poorly
Nobody will trust the United States
Focus on domestic reform and not threatening other people
episodes.quotes: "I think that the United States is in a period of long-term decline. You know, the rest of the world is now slowly adapting to this world in which the United States is really not the kind of big player that it was for most of the 20th century."

episodes.title: Michael Nielsen – Why aliens will have a different tech stack than us
episodes.summary: Michael Nielsen and Dwarkesh Patel discuss the difficulty of recognizing scientific progress, especially in AI. They explore the history of science, focusing on the Michelson-Morley experiment and the discovery of special relativity, to illustrate how scientific progress is often a messy, nonlinear process. They argue that there is no single, universal heuristic for recognizing scientific progress, and that different fields may require different approaches.
key_highlights: Michelson-Morley experiment was not the direct inspiration for special relativity
The scientific community, not just individual scientists, is key to scientific progress
The theory of everything may be a misleading goal for physics
AI can help close the RL verification loop on scientific discovery
Expertise can sometimes be a hindrance to scientific progress
Different fields may require different heuristics for recognizing scientific progress
episodes.quotes: "Subtle is the Lord, but malicious He is not"

episodes.title: How Orientalism Creates Information Asymmetry About Iran - Seyed Mohammad Marandi | Endgame #262
episodes.guest_title: Professor of North American Studies and Postcolonial Literature, University of Tehran
episodes.summary: Marandi argues that Western media's orientalist narratives about Iran are designed to create a sense of superiority and exceptionalism in the West, leading to miscalculations and aggression. He contends that the West's misunderstanding of Iran's culture of resistance and its support for the oppressed has led to failed policies. Marandi believes that Iran's steadfastness and resilience, rooted in its Islamic worldview, will ultimately lead to the defeat of Western imperialism.
key_highlights: Western media's orientalist narratives about Iran are designed to create a sense of superiority
Iran's culture of resistance is rooted in its Islamic worldview
The West underestimates Iran's resolve due to its orientalist bias
Iran's support for Palestine stems from its commitment to social justice
Iran's military doctrine is designed to push back against the empire
Iranian society is sophisticated and resilient despite Western propaganda
episodes.quotes: “They are bombing Tehran right now… Can you hear?” | "Good will come out of it" | "The front lines are the cities now"

episodes.title: Why This War Could Be a “Turning Point” for the Global Order - Vali Nasr | Endgame #261
episodes.summary: In this episode, Vali Nasr discusses the history of Iran and its relationship with the United States and Israel. He argues that Iran is determined not to be bullied into surrender and that the current conflict is happening in the shadow of the collapse of the JCPOA. He also argues that the new leadership in Iran is much more hardline than its predecessors and that it is determined to resist the United States.
key_highlights: Iran is determined not to be bullied into surrender
Iran is not interested in a ceasefire
Iran is open to attacks from Israel and America
Iran is facing an angry population at home
Iran does not want to go back to the situation before the war
The current conflict is happening in the shadow of the collapse of the JCPOA
The new leadership in Iran is much more hardline
episodes.quotes: Iran is determined not to be bullied into surrender. Iran is not interested in a ceasefire that would take it back to the box that it was kept in before the war.

episodes.title: Mengapa Hukum yang Buruk Boleh Ditentang? - Bivitri Susanti | Endgame #260
episodes.guest_title: Founder, Pusat Studi Hukum dan Kebijakan Indonesia (PSHK)
episodes.summary: Bivitri Susanti argues that Indonesia's legal system is increasingly weaponized by those in power to serve oligarchic interests, marginalizing ordinary citizens. She contends that the focus on legalism over morality in policymaking leads to unjust outcomes, and that the overemphasis on order and obedience in education stifles critical thinking. She calls for a shift towards a more equitable and rights-based approach to law, emphasizing the importance of dissent and challenging the status quo.
key_highlights: Laws are being created solely for the benefit of the oligarchy
The law is being used as a weapon by those in power
Ordinary people lack the power to challenge unjust laws
The focus on legalism over morality is a problem
The education system doesn't encourage critical thinking
The spirit of the law should favor the majority
The government should not criminalize policy
episodes.quotes: "Orang itu kalau mau melakukan kudeta dan merampas hak, enggak perlu tank, enggak perlu senjata. Pakai kertas. Namanya hukum. Lalu pada dasarnya Anda bisa mendapatkan apa saja yang Anda inginkan."

episodes.title: Terence Tao – How the world’s top mathematician uses AI
episodes.summary: Terence Tao discusses the potential of AI to revolutionize mathematics, drawing parallels to historical shifts in scientific methodology. He argues that while AI excels at breadth and pattern recognition, human mathematicians still possess a unique capacity for depth and conceptual understanding. The conversation explores the challenges of evaluating AI-generated proofs and the need for new metrics to assess their significance and elegance.
key_highlights: AI excels at breadth, generating many ideas, while humans excel at depth
Kepler's discovery of planetary motion involved both data and geometric intuition
AI could help identify fruitful areas for mathematical research
AI may struggle with the "human" aspects of science, like persuasion
A key challenge is evaluating the significance of AI-generated mathematical results
There's a need for new metrics beyond correctness to assess mathematical progress
episodes.quotes: "You can't look at any given scientific achievement purely in isolation and give it an objective grade... without being aware of the context both in the past and the future."

episodes.title: Jiang Xueqin: Our True Wealth Is Our Consciousness | Endgame #259
episodes.summary: Jiang Xueqin discusses the rise of techno-Marxism and the elite's desire for a compliant population. He argues that wealth is not money but attention and consciousness, which the elite seek to control. He also discusses the shift from unipolarity to multipolarity, the importance of open-source information, and the need for individuals to think for themselves.
key_highlights: The world is moving towards techno-Marxism, where the elite seek a compliant population
Wealth is attention and consciousness, not money
The elite purposefully hide the message that "you matter"
The US is no longer a democracy but an oligarchy
AI is being used to create individual matrices to control consciousness
episodes.quotes: "The entire universe is watching what we do. We are all connected to the entire universe. Each of us can be a beautiful ray of light."

episodes.title: Dylan Patel — The single biggest bottleneck to scaling AI compute
episodes.summary: Dylan Patel discusses the AI compute landscape, focusing on the semiconductor supply chain and the economics of building and deploying AI models. He argues that while there's a lot of capital investment in AI, the bottleneck is the limited capacity of ASML's EUV lithography tools, which are essential for manufacturing advanced chips. He also highlights the importance of understanding the entire supply chain, from power and data centers to memory vendors, to accurately assess the potential for AI scaling.
key_highlights: Big Tech's $600B capex this year would require 50 gigawatts of compute at current rental prices
AI labs' recent raises are enough to cover their compute spend for the year
Nvidia is intentionally fracturing the complementary industry to maintain leverage
A100s are worth more now than they were three years ago
Google woke up late to the AI compute game
The AI compute bottleneck is the semiconductor supply chain, especially ASML's EUV tools
episodes.quotes: "After you use it, I'm like, I can't use this again."

episodes.title: Why Leonardo was a saboteur, Gutenberg went broke, and Florence was weird – Ada Palmer
episodes.guest_title: Renaissance Historian, Novelist, and Composer, University of Chicago
episodes.summary: Ada Palmer discusses the invention of the Renaissance, arguing that it was a conscious effort to revive Roman virtues in response to the chaos of the late Middle Ages. She highlights the role of merchant guilds in Florence, which, while not democratic, provided a unique social safety net and fostered a culture of intellectual exchange. Palmer emphasizes the importance of understanding the historical context of the Renaissance, including the role of patronage, the limited access to knowledge, and the ever-present threat of tyranny.
key_highlights: The Renaissance was a deliberate effort to revive Roman virtues in response to societal breakdown
Italian city-states were clustered in Italy due to the dissolution of the Roman Empire
Wealthy towns with good agriculture were better able to transition to republics
The Medici family rose to power by manipulating the lottery system in Florence
Machiavelli saw the end justifying the means when the end was the survival of the state
The Florentine toga was a symbol of Republican values, not a sign of tyranny
episodes.quotes: "I'm going to blast the top story off your house, would say, yes your grace, please continue, because there are literally severed heads of people who resisted still rotting on spikes in front of the Palazzo Vecchio."

episodes.title: How AI-Enabled Organized Crimes & Systemic Scams Work - Niki Luhur | Endgame #257
episodes.summary: Niki Luhur argues that current cybersecurity measures are inadequate to address the sophistication of modern fraud. He points out that current cybersecurity strategies, such as SMS OTPs, are easily exploited, and that fraud is increasingly used to fund illicit activities, including nuclear weapons programs. Luhur advocates for a shift in focus from educating consumers to changing the system, emphasizing the importance of data sovereignty and encryption.
key_highlights: Digital economies are vulnerable to trust erosion and can move backward
Passwords and SMS OTPs are questionable authentication factors
Phishing is the number one attack vector, targeting SMS OTPs
AI can be weaponized and is already intertwined with geopolitics
Consumer education is insufficient; systemic change is needed
Data sovereignty and encryption are crucial for fraud prevention
The industry needs to define clear, enforceable, and auditable standards
episodes.quotes: "If you believe that a SMS OTP is also a secure factor, the proof is, the majority of the phishing and the biggest threats today, the number one attack method or vector, is through phishing."

episodes.title: Dario Amodei — “We are near the end of the exponential”
episodes.summary: Dario Amodei discusses the current state of AI and the future of scaling. He argues that the underlying technology is progressing as expected, but what is surprising is the lack of public recognition of how close we are to the end of the exponential. He also discusses the importance of careful planning and responsible compute scaling, as well as the need for a wide variety of realistic tasks and environments to achieve generalization.
key_highlights: Underlying AI technology is progressing as expected, but public recognition lags
There is a lack of scaling laws for RL
Pre-training and RL are not that different
The frontier of AI is a bit uneven
The exponential is coming to an end
episodes.quotes: "The most surprising thing has been the lack of public recognition of how close we are to the end of the exponential. To me it is absolutely wild."

episodes.title: Jane Fraser, CEO of Citi: Lead with Empathy
episodes.summary: Jane Fraser discusses her international upbringing, including attending an all-girls school that produced powerful female leaders. She shares how she made partner at McKinsey by age 30, then stepped back to work part-time while raising her family. Fraser emphasizes the importance of having a clear vision, being clinically unemotional, and having the courage to make tough decisions. She explains how empathy is a competitive advantage, and how she learned to lead with humor.
key_highlights: Early education taught her to "go for it" and be curious
Two women from same high school ran Middle East financial institutions
Great leaders equip you to give it a shot
You're measured in decades, not years, so don't be rushed
Be a bank with a brain and a soul
Empathy is about understanding the other person's position
You can always call them an asshole tomorrow
episodes.quotes: You have to be a bank with a brain and a soul. The soul is your people. You have to have the courage that it won't slow you down. You can always call them an asshole tomorrow.

episodes.title: Akui Perbedaan, tapi Cari Persamaan - M. Quraish Shihab | Endgame #255
episodes.summary: Quraish Shihab argues that religious differences are inevitable and should be respected, but that a focus on shared humanity and values is essential for unity. He critiques the tendency to impose one's own religious interpretations on others, especially in the context of Abrahamic religions. Shihab emphasizes the importance of understanding the historical and cultural context of religious texts to avoid misinterpretations and promote tolerance.
key_highlights: Agama cinta: Love is the essence of religion
Avoid imposing religious concepts without expertise
Meeting of three religions is not enough
Discussing God with the heart unites, but the mind divides
Don't create problems that hinder unity
Don't be a source of conflict
episodes.quotes: "Agamaku agama cinta. Di mana ada cinta, di situ aku beragama."

episodes.title: SBY Membaca Catur Politik Dunia | Endgame #254
episodes.summary: SBY discusses the importance of Indonesia’s role in maintaining global stability and prosperity, emphasizing the need for a balanced approach between national interests and international cooperation. He highlights the dangers of ultranationalism and the importance of adhering to international law. SBY also touches on the need for Indonesia to learn from China's economic success while maintaining its democratic values, and the need to avoid being caught in the middle of US-China rivalry.
key_highlights: Ultranationalism can lead to a disregard for international norms and cooperation.
A strong military is not the only measure of power.
Indonesia must balance national interests with global cooperation.
The world needs a new UN.
Indonesia must learn from China's economic success.
The world is moving away from multilateralism.
episodes.quotes: "Orang bilang, kalau yang baik-baik diam, nanti yang jahat menang."

episodes.title: Elon Musk – "In 36 months, the cheapest place to put AI will be space”
episodes.summary: Elon Musk discusses the future of AI, arguing that the most interesting outcome is the most probable. He claims that the limiting factor for AI is power, and that space will become the cheapest place to put AI in 36 months. He argues that the goal of AI should be to expand intelligence and consciousness in the universe, and that it should be rigorously truth-seeking to avoid becoming deceptive.
key_highlights: The most interesting outcome is the most probable
Power is the limiting factor for AI
Space will be the cheapest place to put AI in 36 months
AI should be truth-seeking
The atmosphere causes 30% energy loss for solar panels
It's harder to scale on the ground than in space
episodes.quotes: "The output of chips is growing pretty much exponentially, but the output of electricity is flat. So how are you going to turn them chips on?"

episodes.title: Joe Tsai, Co-Founder and Chairman, Alibaba: Find Your People
episodes.summary: Joe Tsai shares his experiences navigating cultural differences in his career, from moving to the US as a teenager to building Alibaba. He emphasizes the importance of finding people you enjoy working with, and the need to be direct and honest, even when delivering difficult news. Tsai also stresses the importance of having a mission-driven approach and investing in the right technology, even if it means going against conventional wisdom.
key_highlights: Be humble and learn from those smarter than you
Find people you enjoy working with
Focus on the customer, not just the boss
Don't be afraid to be direct
Prioritize strategic value over short-term ROI
Have a mission-driven approach
Agility and quick decision-making are critical
episodes.quotes: "You have to be able to tolerate not having full information and then just making a decision and commit to it."

episodes.title: China Predicted Its Rise 75 Years Ago - Eric X. Li | Endgame #253
episodes.guest_title: Venture Capitalist, Chairman of Chengwei Capital
episodes.summary: Eric X. Li argues that the United States has become a "Liberal Credo State" that has built its empire at the expense of the American nation. He contends that developing countries often fail because they adopt transplanted constitutions that are not rooted in their own cultural, moral, and social conditions. He suggests that the US is retreating from globalization, and that China's future depends on continued interconnectedness with the Global South.
key_highlights: US has become a "Liberal Credo State" that sacrifices its nation for empire
Developing countries fail when they adopt transplanted constitutions
China's success depends on interconnectedness with the Global South
Western modernity is a package deal, but individualism is a viral strand
China's success is due to its revolution and unique political institutions
episodes.quotes: "The United States today has become a liberal credo state. The American empire was built at the expense of the American nation."

episodes.title: Bencana dan Revolusi Era Baru - Sudirman Said | Endgame #252
episodes.summary: Gita Wirjawan interviews Sudirman Said, Rector of Universitas Harakat Negeri, on the topic of education, leadership, and Indonesia's place in the world. Said argues that Indonesia needs to focus on producing high-quality STEM graduates, but also needs to address the moral and ethical issues that plague the country's elite. He also argues that Indonesia needs to invest in its teachers and provide them with better incentives.
key_highlights: Indonesia's constitution mandates 20% of the budget for education, but where does it go?
Aceh's tragic past requires leaders who are willing to serve
The best leaders are those who are willing to serve
Indonesia needs to produce high-quality STEM graduates
Indonesia needs to address the moral and ethical issues of its elite
episodes.quotes: "Mungkin karena suasana sangat tidak menentu, jadi, bukan dicari insinyur paling hebat, bukan dicari ahli sosiologi paling hebat, tapi dicari orang yang begitu dihubungi, enggak pakai nawar, 'Ya, saya berangkat.'"

episodes.title: Trump's Strategic Ambiguity and Future of EU - Glenn Diesen | Endgame #251
episodes.summary: Glenn Diesen argues that the US is pivoting away from Europe to Asia, leaving Europe in a vulnerable position. He contends that Europe's reliance on the US for security and economic partnerships is a mistake, and that Europe should diversify its relationships and pursue a more independent foreign policy. He also discusses the Israel-Palestine conflict and the Ukraine war, arguing that the West's approach is hypocritical and counterproductive.
key_highlights: US is pivoting to Asia, leaving Europe vulnerable
Europe should diversify relationships, not rely on the US
The world is multipolar, so diversify, don't align with one power
Europe's security and economic partnerships are at stake
The West's approach to the Israel-Palestine conflict is hypocritical
Europe's over-reliance on US technology and industry is a problem
episodes.quotes: When the world is multipolar, you diversify. If you go align with one great power and you go against the other, now you become 100% dependent on the one, and a vessel status is unavoidable.

episodes.title: A.G. Sulzberger, Publisher and Chairman of The New York Times: Mission Under Pressure
episodes.guest_title: Publisher and Chairman, The New York Times
episodes.summary: A.G. Sulzberger discusses the importance of independent journalism and the challenges of maintaining it in a polarized world. He emphasizes the need for business leaders to stay informed and support quality journalism, even when it's critical. He shares how the New York Times is adapting to the digital age, focusing on the core mission of seeking truth and holding power accountable, while also embracing new technologies and diversifying its offerings.
key_highlights: You're only as good as your product, and journalism is the product
You can either work at the NYT or read it, but not both
Print is used as a sweep-up mechanism to catch every story
Need to be authentic to the medium
The best ideas flow upwards in an organization
episodes.quotes: You can either work at the New York Times, or you can read the New York Times, but no one has time to do both. I've been really determined to prove that wrong.

episodes.title: Pandu Sjahrir Patahkan Mitos tentang Danantara | Endgame #250
episodes.summary: Pandu Sjahrir discusses the importance of education and human capital development in Indonesia, emphasizing that they are key to increasing productivity and attracting foreign investment. He stresses the need for a shift in mindset, from focusing on natural resources to prioritizing human talent and skills. He also highlights the importance of good governance, transparency, and a focus on long-term, sustainable growth.
key_highlights: Hard work, education, and kindness are key values
Commercial return and economic return are both important
Need high expectations and pessimism to succeed
Indonesia needs to focus on human capital development
The best investment is in your classmates
Need to translate Indonesian narrative to the world
episodes.quotes: "Corruption is actually a tax on the economy for growth."

episodes.title: ‘Bapak Satelit Indonesia’: Kita Harus Bangun Industri Antariksa - Adi Rahman Adiwoso | Endgame #249
episodes.summary: Adi Rahman Adiwoso, Pimpinan dari Pasifik Satelit Nusantara (PSN), discusses the importance of developing Indonesia's space industry and the challenges it faces. He emphasizes the need for a shift in mindset, from seeking projects to creating an industry, and for the government to embrace risk and provide continuous funding. Adiwoso also highlights the importance of nurturing local talent and leveraging Indonesia's unique geographical position to become a center of space excellence.
key_highlights: Indonesia needs to shift from seeking projects to creating a space industry
Government must embrace risk and provide continuous funding
Nurturing local talent is key to Indonesia's space industry
Indonesia's equatorial position is a strategic advantage
Current policies lack confidence in Indonesia's industrial capabilities
episodes.quotes: "Kalau anak-anak ini ada tempat mereka bisa bekerja dan berkreasi, nah itu tanggung jawab-jawab generasi yang di atasnya untuk menciptakan kapabilitas tersebut."

episodes.title: Adam Marblestone – AI is missing something fundamental about the brain
episodes.summary: Adam Marblestone argues that modern AI lacks something fundamental about the brain, which is not just a learning algorithm but a system for encoding high-level desires and intentions. He suggests that the brain's architecture and the way it uses a steering subsystem to connect learned features with innate behaviors are key to its efficiency. He also emphasizes the importance of understanding the evolutionary context of the brain's design, which has had to balance energy efficiency and computational power.
key_highlights: AI lacks the brain's ability to encode high-level desires and intentions
The brain has a steering subsystem to connect learned features with innate behaviors
AI lacks the brain's ability to generalize
The brain has many different loss functions turned on at different stages of development
The brain has a learning curriculum that evolution has encoded
The cortex is a universal prediction engine
episodes.quotes: "My overall meta-level take is that we have to empower the field of neuroscience to just make neuroscience a more powerful field technologically and otherwise to actually be able to crack a question like this."

episodes.title: Etika Politik, Tata Negara, Filsafat Ekonomi ala Tom Lembong | Endgame #248
episodes.summary: In this episode of Endgame, host Gita Wirjawan interviews Tom Lembong, Managing Partner at Quvat Management, about ethics, governance, and economic philosophy. Lembong argues that Indonesia should focus on improving the quality of its teachers and decentralizing authority to local leaders. He also notes that China's economic success is due to its decentralized economic activity, but that it is also very sensitive to public opinion.
key_highlights: All things have limits, even populism and extremism
Rationality will always return
Trust is the most important thing
Indonesian society is now very smart
We need to be more selective with our content
episodes.quotes: Hukum yang berlaku itu hukum alam, bahwa semua ada batasnya, bahkan para populis atau ekstremis, apakah ekstremis kanan atau ekstremis kiri, ujungnya kehabisan bensin karena untuk menyiram terus—menyalut, tapi rasionalitas orang muncul kembali.

episodes.title: Sarah Paine – Why Russia Lost the Cold War
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Strategy and Policy, U.S. Naval War College
episodes.summary: Sarah Paine argues that the Soviet Union lost the Cold War not due to Reagan's military buildup, but due to internal factors and the unintended consequences of its own policies. She contends that the Soviet Union's economic system was inherently flawed, leading to widespread lying and misallocation of resources.  She also highlights the role of human rights movements and the unintended consequences of Gorbachev's reforms in undermining the Soviet system.
key_highlights: Reagan's military buildup bankrupted the Soviet Union
Human rights clauses undermined communist belief
Soviet economy was literally exhausted from the arms race
Soviet leaders made green light to a dozen new military programs
The arms race plunged the Soviet economy into a permanent crisis
episodes.quotes: "The most dangerous moment for a bad government is when it begins to reform."

episodes.title: Runtuhnya Kepercayaan Publik: Sebuah Perspektif Politik Global - Marty Natalegawa | Endgame #247
episodes.summary: Marty Natalegawa and Gita Wirjawan discuss the shifting world order, the rise of revisionist powers, and the erosion of trust in international institutions. Natalegawa argues that unilateral deadlines in negotiations are counterproductive and that the focus should be on building trust and understanding each other's interests. He also emphasizes the importance of education and diplomacy in navigating the complex geopolitical landscape.
key_highlights: Unilateral deadlines in negotiations are counterproductive
Trust is the foundation of legitimate power
A multipolar world is becoming more structured
Indonesia needs to increase its geopolitical antenna
Need for a more magnanimous world order
The importance of education and diplomacy
episodes.quotes: Dalam perundingan/negosiasi, hal pertama yang perlu diprioritaskan adalah tidak boleh ada tenggat waktu yang ditetapkan secara sepihak.

episodes.title: Bagaimana Akarkan Budaya Bisnis Secara Sistematis - Keluarga Subakat | Endgame #246
episodes.summary: The Subakat family discusses the cultural and strategic factors behind ParagonCorp's success. They emphasize the importance of R&D, a strong ethical foundation, and a commitment to Indonesia's development. They also touch on the challenges of competing with global brands and the need to adapt to changing market dynamics. The family stresses the importance of a strong connection to the company's values and a commitment to continuous improvement.
key_highlights: Talk the walk, but also walk the talk
Action first, then advice
Humbleness and approachability are key
Purpose-driven companies attract purpose-driven talent
Indonesia must embrace science and entrepreneurship
Luck is effort multiplied by opportunity
R&D is paramount to compete globally
episodes.quotes: Bu Nur Subakat benar-benar ngajarin kita tuh—orang suka ngomong work the talk ya, tapi kalau menurut saya Bu Nur Subakat itu talk the work. Jadi action-nya dilakuin dulu, konsisten, baru nasihatin kita.

episodes.title: Purbaya Sadewa: Pendirian dalam Berkebijakan Itu Penting | Endgame #245
episodes.guest_title: Chief Economist at the Coordinating Ministry for Economic Affairs
episodes.summary: Purbaya Sadewa argues that Indonesia's economic struggles stem from domestic policy failures, not global uncertainty. He advocates for increased liquidity and a shift towards private sector-led growth. He criticizes government-led growth, claiming it stifles the private sector. He also emphasizes the need to improve the quality of STEM education and to translate uncertainty into manageable risk.
key_highlights: Indonesia's economic struggles are due to domestic policy, not global uncertainty
Indonesia needs more liquidity in the system to spur growth
Government-led growth stifles the private sector
Indonesia needs to translate uncertainty into manageable risk
Indonesia needs more STEM graduates
LPDP can be a catalyst for STEM education
Indonesia's current economic narrative is too focused on fixed income
episodes.quotes: "Waktu ekonomi kita goncang, orang sering bilang karena ketidakpastian global. Enggak ada urusan sama ketidakpastian global. Sebetulnya kalau saya lihat, salah kebijakan domestik, uang kita hampir nol pertumbuhannya."

episodes.title: How Math and Sanskrit Shape Amartya Sen's Economic Thinking | Endgame #244
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics and Philosophy, Harvard University
episodes.summary: Professor Amartya Sen discusses the influence of Rabindranath Tagore and his grandfather on his upbringing, emphasizing the importance of tolerance and collaboration. He reflects on the integration of Hindu and Muslim cultures in India, citing examples like Indian music and the Taj Mahal. Sen also touches on the need for freedom and knowledge, while cautioning against the dangers of religious sectarianism.
key_highlights: Tolerance is not enough; working together is essential
Indian music and architecture are products of Hindu-Muslim fusion
Claims about Indian mathematical origins are mostly nonsense
The importance of freedom combined with learning
AI should not be left to inanimate objects
Democracy requires equality, freedom, and distribution of resources
The best things in India are a mix of Hindu and Muslim
episodes.quotes: My grandfather used to say, "Tolerance is not enough, you have to work together."

episodes.title: Ilya Sutskever – We're moving from the age of scaling to the age of research
episodes.summary: Dwarkesh and Ilya discuss the shift from scaling to research in AI, with Ilya arguing that we have reached a point where simply scaling up existing models is no longer the most effective path to progress. They debate the nature of human intelligence, the role of emotions in decision-making, and the potential for AI to surpass human capabilities. They also discuss the importance of AI safety and the need for careful consideration of the ethical implications of increasingly powerful AI systems.
key_highlights: The shift in AI from scaling to research is about better understanding the underlying principles
Humans may have a built-in evolutionary prior that makes them sample-efficient learners
The value function of humans is modulated by emotions, which are hardcoded by evolution
Pre-training is a recipe that is good, but not necessarily the best
The power of AI is the problem, not the intelligence itself
episodes.quotes: "The whole problem is the power."

episodes.title: David Baszucki, Founder and CEO of Roblox: Trust Your Gut
episodes.summary: David Baszucki shares his journey from Stanford engineering student to Roblox CEO, emphasizing the importance of trusting intuition and building a strong team. He recounts the early days of Roblox, its near-death moments, and the key decisions that led to its success. Baszucki also discusses the challenges of balancing safety with innovation and the importance of having a values-aligned company culture.
key_highlights: Trust your gut and don't ignore your intuition
Focus on building a strong team
Prioritize safety over short-term gains
Embrace innovation and be open to new ideas
Build infrastructure with cloud-bursting capability
Make decisions that align with your values
Be a perpetual student
episodes.quotes: “I think it's actually an opportunity, not a challenge… We feel very lucky that we are not dependent on the cloud providers… We always go safety.”

episodes.title: Rocky Gerung dan Tom Lembong: Mungkinkah Meritokrasi di Tengah Mediokrasi? | Endgame #243
episodes.guest_title: Political Commentator Rocky Gerung and Economist Tom Lembong
episodes.summary: This Endgame episode explores meritocracy in Indonesia, with guests Rocky Gerung and Tom Lembong. Gerung argues that meritocracy must be filtered through strong democracy, which requires societal values to be absorbed by power. Lembong argues that when formal systems fail, people and political energy shift to informal systems, where truth and trust can overcome weapons and money. The conversation touches on the challenges of achieving meritocracy in a feudalistic political culture.
key_highlights: Meritokrasi harus dihasilkan melalui filter demokrasi yang kuat
Filter demokrasi diperkuat dengan elemen nilai masyarakat
Kekuasaan harus menyerap nilai masyarakat secara sempurna
Melanggar undang-undang pencemaran sungai bukan melanggar HAM
Kebenaran dan kepercayaan bisa mengalahkan senjata, partai, dan uang
episodes.quotes: Meritokrasi harus dihasilkan melalui filter demokrasi yang kuat. Filter demokrasi itu hanya bisa diperkuat kalau ada elemen nilai masyarakat itu diserap secara sempurna oleh kekuasaan.

episodes.title: dr. Ryu Hasan: Kecerdasan dan Kebodohan Sama-sama Bisa Dilatih | Endgame #242
episodes.summary: Dr. Ryu Hasan argues that people are trained to be irrational, which hinders critical thinking. He suggests that activities, behaviors, and decision-making are results of training. He also argues that common sense is uncommon, and IQ is declining because people are trained to let it decline. He concludes that abstraction and empiricism are complementary, not oppositional, and that happiness is more about social interaction than individual achievement.
key_highlights: People are trained to be irrational, hindering critical thinking
Common sense is very uncommon
IQ is declining because people are trained to let it decline
Abstraction and empiricism are complementary, not oppositional
IQ is trained to decline
Common sense is very uncommon
IQ is trained to decline
episodes.quotes: "How common sense is very uncommon at present. How we believe unbelievable. IQ kita itu merosot tajam karena dilatih untuk merosot."

episodes.title: Ketika Lapangan Kerja Formal Gagal Diciptakan… | Endgame #241
episodes.guest_title: Dr. Hendri Saparini, Tirza Munusamy, Dee Lestari
episodes.summary: The Endgame episode discusses the gig economy in Indonesia, its challenges, and the need for a nuanced understanding of its role. While the gig economy offers flexibility and choice, it often lacks the benefits and protections of formal employment. The panelists emphasize the importance of financial literacy and the need for government policies that support both formal and informal sectors, recognizing their distinct characteristics and needs.
key_highlights: Gig economy is an option, even a source of pride, offering flexibility
Formalizing the gig economy can limit benefits for workers
Gig workers need financial literacy to manage income fluctuations
The informal sector is a safety net due to limited formal jobs
Indonesia needs to boost STEM education for a stronger economy
Policy should support both formal and informal sectors
Government should facilitate access to expertise and resources
episodes.quotes: "Gig ekonomi atau gig workers itu pekerja di sektor gig ini menjadi satu opsi, bahkan itu menjadi sesuatu yang membanggakan karena dia bisa punya pilihan."

episodes.title: Satya Nadella – How Microsoft thinks about AGI
episodes.summary: Satya Nadella discusses Microsoft's strategy for AI, emphasizing the importance of building a flexible infrastructure that supports multiple models rather than focusing solely on a single model. He stresses the need for a balance between innovation and practical application, and emphasizes the importance of data liquidity and a robust API business. He also touches on the role of AI in enhancing productivity and the need for responsible AI development.
key_highlights: AI is the biggest thing since the Industrial Revolution
Focus on building infrastructure that supports multiple models
One model is just one tweak away from being commoditized
Microsoft wants to be an infrastructure business in support of agents doing work
The thing to think through is what you do for the next 50 years
AI should be a guardian angel or a cognitive amplifier
Microsoft is building a world-class superintelligence team
episodes.quotes: Maybe after the Industrial Revolution, this is the biggest thing. But at the same time, I'm a little grounded in the fact that this is still early innings.

episodes.title: “Pendidikan Jauh Lebih Penting daripada Uang” - Helman Sitohang | Endgame #240
episodes.summary: In this episode of Endgame, Gita Wirjawan speaks with Helman Sitohang, Vice Chairman of Credit Suisse, about the importance of education. They discuss the need for a strong foundation in STEM fields, as well as the importance of critical thinking and literacy. They also touch on the challenges of providing quality education in developing countries, and the need for a balance between innovation and preservation.
key_highlights: Helman Sitohang credits his success to his parents and his education
Education is more important than money
The importance of critical thinking and literacy
The need for a balance between innovation and preservation
The challenges of providing quality education in developing countries
The need for more investment in teachers
episodes.quotes: Saya rasa dari segi keberuntungan, enggak perlu dipertanyakan, punya orang tua dan kesempatan untuk masuk ke sekolah-sekolah yang bagus.

episodes.title: Sarah Paine — How Russia sabotaged China's rise
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Strategy and Policy, U.S. Naval War College
episodes.summary: Sarah Paine argues that Russia has historically acted as a spoiler to China's rise, often using China as a pawn in its geopolitical games. She outlines how Russia has consistently undermined China's sovereignty and economic development, from the Opium Wars to the Soviet era. Paine also contends that Russia's core identity is rooted in territorial expansion and control, making it an existential threat to its neighbors.
key_highlights: Stalin's plan for the Chinese worked beautifully to unite Nationalists and Communists
Russia took more territory from China than all US territory east of Mississippi
Russia poses an existential threat to its neighbors forever
Russia has a template for block building
China has a nightmare scenario: collapse of empire into chaos
episodes.quotes: "Russia has posed existential threat to its neighbors forever. There are so many neighbors you have never heard of because they've disappeared from the pages of history courtesy of the Russians."

episodes.title: Keyu Jin: Centralized Power, Decentralized Economy | Endgame #239
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics at the London School of Economics
episodes.summary: Keyu Jin argues that the US and China are not as different as they are portrayed, and that the tension between them is fundamentally rivalry. She believes the US is hugely benefited from the liberal order it created, and that the US administration pretends to be a victim of all this. She also believes that China's state capacity has been fundamental to its development process.
key_highlights: US and China are not as different as they are often portrayed
The clash between US and China is fundamentally rivalry
The US is hugely benefited from the liberal order
The US administration pretends to be a victim of this
China's state capacity has been fundamental to its development process
episodes.quotes: I don't really think that US and China are as different as they tend to be or claim to be.

episodes.title: Orlando Bravo, Founder and Managing Partner of Thoma Bravo: Take the Risks Meant for You
episodes.guest_title: Founder and Managing Partner, Thoma Bravo
episodes.summary: Orlando Bravo shares his journey from Puerto Rico to Stanford and the founding of Thoma Bravo. He emphasizes the importance of mentorship, particularly from Carl Thoma, in shaping his leadership style and investment philosophy. Bravo stresses the need to stay grounded, focus on the business, and be open to new ideas, even when they challenge conventional wisdom. He also discusses the importance of giving back to his community, particularly through entrepreneurship initiatives in Puerto Rico.
key_highlights: Mentors are all around you; seek them out
Do your own thing
Stay positive
Focus on the business
Be open to new ideas
Value creation only exists when you can buy the whole company
episodes.quotes: In private equity, the only thing that matters is returns. Investors have incredible choices of where to put their money.

episodes.title: Opini Publik & Kebijakan Bercerai, Bisa Rekonsiliasi? - Rian Fahardhi | Endgame #238
episodes.summary: Rian Fahardhi argues that Indonesia's government today lacks seriousness, causing public anxiety. He believes the burden on young Indonesians is immense, yet they are excluded from policy decisions. He contends that the intangible nature of modern threats undermines the resilience of young people, who are often generalized by their birth year. He advocates for empathy and a focus on public goods to achieve a golden Indonesia.
key_highlights: Government seems unserious, causing anxiety
Youth bear immense responsibility, yet are excluded
Intangible threats undermine resilience
Generational generalizations are harmful
Empathy and public goods are key
Every generation has a responsibility to leave a good legacy
episodes.quotes: “Saya berani bilang bahwa tanggung jawab, beban generasi muda hari ini itu luar biasa dahsyat. Sekarang, bebannya tuh, musuhnya, ancamannya itu enggak kelihatan, enggak nyata.”

episodes.title: Andrej Karpathy — “We’re summoning ghosts, not building animals”
episodes.summary: Karpathy argues that reinforcement learning is overhyped, and that the field is building "ghosts, not animals" — digital entities that mimic human intelligence but lack the embodiment and grounding of real beings. He believes that LLMs are not truly intelligent, but rather are very good at pattern completion, and that the industry is overestimating how quickly they will be able to solve real-world problems. He also argues that the best way to learn about AI is to build it yourself, rather than reading blog posts or watching videos.
key_highlights: Reinforcement learning is terrible, but everything that came before it was much worse
AI is building ghosts, not animals
LLMs are mimicking humans, not building real intelligence
It's the decade of agents, not the year of agents
The best way to learn about AI is to build it yourself
LLMs are overfit to the internet
Humans don't use reinforcement learning
episodes.quotes: "Reinforcement Learning is terrible. It just so happens that everything that we had before it is much worse."

episodes.title: “Dengan Musik, Dunia Penuh Cinta dan Kedamaian” - Dwiki Dharmawan | Endgame #237
episodes.summary: Dwiki Dharmawan discusses his musical journey, starting with traditional Sundanese Gamelan music and classical piano, and evolving into jazz and fusion. He emphasizes the importance of improvisation and collaboration in music, drawing parallels to diplomacy and the need for openness to new ideas. He shares anecdotes about his experiences with Krakatau and his work with international musicians.
key_highlights: Early exposure to traditional music shaped his musical foundation
Elfa's influence led to jazz and fusion exploration
Improvisation is key to musical creativity and life
Collaboration with diverse musicians broadens artistic horizons
Music is a powerful tool for cultural diplomacy
The best music has soul and feeling
The most successful people are often the most rebellious
episodes.quotes: “Kamu main, kamu interpretasikan, baca apa yang ada, jangan ditambahi!”

episodes.title: “I find it almost disturbing that the universe favors life this strongly” – Nick Lane
episodes.summary: Nick Lane argues that life is likely abundant in the universe because it is thermodynamically favored. He argues that the bottleneck to complex life is the evolution of eukaryotes, which are rare because they require a specific endosymbiotic event. He also argues that the existence of two sexes is a way to increase variance in the mitochondrial genome, which is important for adaptation.
key_highlights: Life is likely abundant because it is thermodynamically favored
Eukaryotes are rare because they require a specific endosymbiotic event
Eukaryotic cells look the same under an electron microscope
Bacteria have more genetic versatility than eukaryotes
Sex increases variance in the mitochondrial genome
episodes.quotes: "If you've got 1000 planets with life on, maybe life is going to be the same way 999 out of 1000 times because it's going to be carbon-based, it's going to be water, it's going to be cells."

episodes.title: Victor Gao on How the US Misunderstands China  | Endgame #236
episodes.guest_title: Vice President of Center for China and Globalization
episodes.summary: Victor Gao argues that the US has misunderstood China's intentions, and that the US's overreach is detrimental to global peace. He asserts that China's economic success is due to its commitment to free trade and building, not destroying. He argues that the US should recognize the legitimate rights of the Palestinian people and work towards a two-state solution.
key_highlights: President Trump should talk to China if he wants to bring back manufacturing
China's transformation has changed not only China, but also the world
China does not seek hegemony; it wants peaceful coexistence
The US needs to improve its container ports to compete with China
The US should not isolate China but work with it
The US should respect the rights of the Palestinian people
episodes.quotes: “If you are really interested in bringing back manufacturing jobs to the United States, you need to talk to China. China is the most successful country ever since 1978 in achieving rapid rate of industrialization.”

episodes.title: Danai 300+ Perusahaan Teknologi di Asia Tenggara - Roderick Purwana | Endgame #235
episodes.summary: Roderick Purwana discusses the importance of education, particularly in STEM fields, for Indonesia's economic growth. He emphasizes the need for local expertise and a focus on productivity-driven innovation, rather than just growth at all costs. Purwana also highlights the importance of trust and integrity in attracting investors and building a sustainable tech ecosystem.
key_highlights: Entrepreneurship is difficult and not for everyone; resilience and toughness are key
Education is crucial for Indonesia's economic growth and global competitiveness
Indonesia needs to focus on productivity-driven innovation, not just growth
Trust and integrity are essential for attracting investment and building a tech ecosystem
Local expertise is crucial for navigating the Indonesian market
Technology is a tool to enhance productivity, not a solution in itself
The right people are key to success, with a focus on execution
episodes.quotes: Entrepreneurship is very difficult and it's not for everyone, tough, resilient, how they operate under pressure, yeah, kan? Apa aja sih determinasi sukses?

episodes.title: Richard Sutton – Father of RL thinks LLMs are a dead end
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Computing Science, University of Alberta
episodes.summary: Richard Sutton argues that LLMs are a dead end, because they are trained on human data, rather than learning from experience. He claims that the key to intelligence is learning from experience, and that the most important thing is to understand the world, not to mimic people. He also argues that the focus on LLMs is a fashion, and that the field has lost track of the basic things. He believes that the next step is to create AI that can learn from experience, and that this will be a major transition in the universe.
key_highlights: Humans are animals, and what we have in common is more interesting
Understanding a squirrel is almost all the way there
Learning is an active process of trying things and seeing what happens
Learning is about understanding the world, not mimicking people
Reinforcement Learning is basic AI
Large language models are about mimicking people
episodes.quotes: "Why are you trying to distinguish humans? Humans are animals. What we have in common is more interesting. What distinguishes us, we should be paying less attention to."

episodes.title: Pendidikan di Indonesia Terlalu Santai? - Hendra Kwee | Endgame #234
episodes.guest_title: Founder of Yayasan Simetri and Leader of the Indonesian Physics Olympiad Team
episodes.summary: Hendra Kwee and Gita Wirjawan discuss the state of Indonesian education, particularly in STEM fields. Kwee argues that Indonesia's emphasis on rote memorization and lack of investment in teachers hinders its ability to compete globally. He advocates for a shift towards critical thinking and a greater focus on basic science education, drawing comparisons to China and the US.
key_highlights: Indonesia's education system emphasizes rote memorization over critical thinking
Teacher quality is crucial for improving STEM education
Indonesia's education system is too focused on paperwork
Indonesia needs a long-term plan for education
Indonesia needs to prioritize STEM education
Indonesia needs to invest in teachers
Indonesia needs to promote innovation
episodes.quotes: "The openness of the teacher to, there is a point, there is a time in which when a child asks, oh I can't, I don't know the answer."

episodes.title: Pimpin 15 Ribu Karyawan: Itu Amanah - Sari Chairunisa | Endgame #233
episodes.summary: Sari Chairunisa, Deputy CEO of Paragon Group, discusses the importance of investing in STEM education and research in Indonesia to increase productivity and compete with other countries. She emphasizes that Indonesia needs to move from being a consumer to a producer and that this requires a shift in mindset and a focus on innovation. She also highlights the importance of having a strong legal framework to attract investment.
key_highlights: People perform best when they master their field, have autonomy, and understand their purpose
Indonesia needs more STEM graduates to compete globally
Indonesia needs to invest in research and development to become a producer
The beauty industry can be a STEM field
Indonesia needs to improve its legal framework to attract investment
Indonesia needs to focus on building a strong ecosystem for STEM
episodes.quotes: Menurut beliau, orang itu akan perform atau awet di dunia kerja kalau tiga: Satu, dia menguasai bidangnya. Dua, dia merasa punya otonomi. Yang ketiga, dia ngerti tujuannya.

episodes.title: Sebuah Surat Cinta untuk Indonesia
episodes.summary: Gita Wirjawan hosts a panel of Indonesian thought leaders to discuss the country's future. The discussion touches on Indonesia's history, the importance of good governance, the need to address inequality, and the role of technology. The panel emphasizes the need for a new social contract that prioritizes the well-being of all citizens.
key_highlights: Indonesia's history is long, and it is important to know what to tell
Need to increase cognition and distribute it
The importance of rule of law
The need to re-anchor money to something tangible
The need for transgenerational empathy
The need to address inequality
The need to be critical of leaders

episodes.title: Fully autonomous robots are much closer than you think – Sergey Levine
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences, UC Berkeley
episodes.summary: Sergey Levine discusses the state of robotics, arguing that the field is at the very early beginnings of building general-purpose robotic foundation models. He argues that the key to building truly general robots lies in leveraging prior knowledge and finding the right representations. He also argues that the best AI systems are those that can learn from feedback and correct mistakes. He suggests that multi-modality is key, but that the key is to find the right way to represent context.
key_highlights: Robotics is at the very early beginnings of building general-purpose robotic foundation models
Robots are encompassing all AI technologies
General purpose models could control any robot to perform any task
The robot is essentially encompassing all AI technologies
The end goal is not to fold a nice t-shirt
The end goal is to confirm our initial hypothesis that the basics are solid
The methods we're developing have the ability to tackle the kind of intricate tasks that people can do
episodes.quotes: The robot is essentially encompassing all AI technologies, so if we can get a robot that's truly general, then you can do hopefully a large chunk of what people can do.

episodes.title: Laut dan Pesisir: Jati Diri Indonesia yang Sejati - Arif Havas Oegroseno | Endgame #232
episodes.guest_title: Vice Minister for Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Indonesia
episodes.summary: Arif Havas Oegroseno argues that Indonesia is a maritime and agrarian nation, but that its independence from Dutch colonialism was incomplete because it remained colonized by international law. He believes that Indonesia must revisit its history to understand its identity, and that it must focus on STEM education to develop its maritime resources. He also believes that Indonesia must assert its rights in the South China Sea.
key_highlights: Indonesia is a maritime and agrarian nation, but not fully independent
International law is a form of colonialism
Indonesia must revisit its history
Indonesia should focus on STEM education
Indonesia must assert its rights in the South China Sea
Indonesia's strength lies in its archipelago
Indonesia's founding fathers were visionary
episodes.quotes: Indonesia adalah negara maritim yang agraris. Itulah negara maritim yang agraris. Kita memang merdeka dari Belanda, tapi kita tidak merdeka dari kolonialiasi hukum internasional. Karena hukum internasional yang dibuat waktu itu adalah hukum internasionalnya para penjajah.

episodes.title: Sarah Paine – How Hitler almost starved Britain
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Strategy and Policy, U.S. Naval War College
episodes.summary: Sarah Paine argues that Germany almost starved Britain in WWII, but the British were saved by the US. She argues that the British were able to control the seas, and that Germany should have bought more U-boats. She also argues that the Allies were able to win because they had a big continental buddy in Russia. She argues that the US was able to help Britain because it had a big productive base.
key_highlights: Germany almost sank a terminal quantity of British trade
Britain was dependent on oil and food imports
Malta, Crete, and the Suez Canal were key to Italian ambitions
Germany should have bought a lot more U-boats
Britain was always fighting France, then Germany
The possibilities for maritime and continental powers are different
episodes.quotes: "The Germans almost sank a terminal quantity of British trade. It's close, because Britain is dependent not only on oil imports, but about half of its food supply."

episodes.title: Why Jeffrey Sachs Thinks That Indonesia's Leadership Is Needed | Endgame #231
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Sustainable Development at Columbia University
episodes.summary: Jeffrey Sachs argues that the US self-perception is delusional, especially regarding its foreign policy. He asserts that the US has become the "great meddler" and that its actions destabilize other countries, leaving a mess behind. Sachs calls for an end to US provocations, covert operations, and destabilization efforts, particularly in the context of the ongoing genocide in Gaza, where the US is complicit with Israel.
key_highlights: US self-perception is delusional, leading to harmful foreign policy
The CIA's core role was to fight communism through coups
The US has become the "great meddler" around the world
US should stop destabilizing other countries and meddling in their affairs
US is complicit in the ongoing genocide in Gaza
The US track record in foreign policy is absolutely awful
The US needs to stop provoking other countries
episodes.quotes: Amerika's self-perception versus kenyataannya, saya mulai menyebutnya cukup delusional. They would throw out leaders, it would create coups. It did this dozens and dozens of times around the world. We became the great meddler.

episodes.title: Story Behind Your Cup of Coffee at World’s Largest Coffeehouse Chain - Brady Brewer | Endgame #230
episodes.summary: Brady Brewer, CEO of Starbucks International, discusses Starbucks' commitment to sustainability and community engagement, particularly in Indonesia. He highlights the importance of supporting coffee farmers and providing educational opportunities for their children, emphasizing the company's role in fostering human connection and democratizing ideas. Brewer also addresses misinformation and boycotts faced by Starbucks, underscoring the need for transparency and community engagement.
key_highlights: Starbucks needs a long-term supply of the world’s best coffee
Climate change threatens the coffee industry and farmers
Starbucks supports farmers by soil sampling and analysis
The future of farming depends on healthy children
Starbucks builds clinics to provide free healthcare
Misinformation impacts Starbucks people
Starbucks is committed to being a local brand
episodes.quotes: “Selfishly as Starbucks, we need a long-term supply of the world's best coffee…As global as a brand as we are, we're actually very local.”

episodes.title: Evolution designed us to die fast; we can change that — Jacob Kimmel
episodes.summary: Jacob Kimmel argues that the human genome has not been optimized for longevity due to evolutionary constraints. He suggests that the key to extending lifespan lies in epigenetic reprogramming, which can reset cells to a younger state by manipulating transcription factors. Kimmel also discusses the challenges of delivering these therapies effectively, emphasizing the need for targeted delivery systems to avoid unintended consequences.
key_highlights: Evolutionary constraints limit the optimization of the human genome for longevity
Epigenetic reprogramming can reset cells to a younger state
Transcription factors are key to cell fate and aging
Delivery of therapies to specific cell types is a major challenge
The immune system is a key factor in the aging process
episodes.quotes: "You can reprogram a cell's type and a cell's age simultaneously just by turning on four genes. That's a shocking fact."

episodes.title: Pembangunan Daerah Bisa Rata Jika… - Reynaldi Hermansjah | Endgame #229
episodes.guest_title: President Director of PT Sarana Multi Infrastruktur (Persero)
episodes.summary: Reynaldi Hermansjah discusses the role of PT SMI in bridging expectations and market realities in infrastructure projects, emphasizing the importance of risk mitigation and cost management. He highlights the long-term nature of infrastructure investments, drawing a parallel to a marathon, and advocates for a decentralized economic approach to empower regional development. He also touches on the need for a more nuanced understanding of the risks and benefits of nuclear energy.
key_highlights: PT SMI bridges expectations and market realities in infrastructure projects
Risk mitigation and cost management are crucial for successful infrastructure projects
Infrastructure is a long-term industry, a marathon, requiring strategic partnerships
Decentralized economic activity is essential for regional development and balanced growth
The need for better understanding of nuclear energy's risks and benefits
The importance of capacity building and good governance in infrastructure projects
episodes.quotes: "Kita kan nggak bisa ngecat langit, Pak ya." - Reynaldi Hermansjah

episodes.title: China is killing the US on energy. Does that mean they’ll win AGI? — Casey Handmer
episodes.summary: Casey Handmer and Dwarkesh Patel discuss the industrial race between the US and China, particularly in the context of AI and energy. Handmer argues that the US is uniquely positioned to win this race due to its geography and innovative culture, but also acknowledges China's manufacturing prowess and capital allocation. They explore the role of synthetic fuels, the challenges of permitting renewable energy projects in the US, and the importance of attracting top talent to the US.
key_highlights: China may be accidentally correct in over-investing in solar capacity
China's geographic position is less advantageous than the US
Autocratic dictatorships have a high capacity to shoot themselves in the foot
Synthetic fuels could asymmetrically help China
The US is not cost-sensitive but availability-sensitive when it comes to power
episodes.quotes: "Never underestimate the capacity for an autocratic dictatorship to shoot itself in the foot."

episodes.title: Why the World Looks Like the World Today? - Wang Gungwu | Endgame #228
episodes.guest_title: Historian, Professor at National University of Singapore
episodes.summary: Professor Wang Gungwu discusses the historical development of nation-states, contrasting China's unique civilizational identity with India's more open, less centralized approach. He argues that China's shared written language has been a key factor in its enduring unity, while the Westphalian system of nation-states is a relatively recent phenomenon. He emphasizes the role of cultural values and historical memory in shaping national identity and international relations.
key_highlights: Socialism is only possible after capitalism has created wealth to distribute
Capitalism knows how to make money, socialism does not
Socialism is about distributing money fairly after it has been made
Deng Xiaoping was a genius because he understood the need for prosperity
You can't have socialism without prosperity
The idea of nationhood is a relatively recent invention
The Chinese state is unique because of its shared written language
episodes.quotes: You can only have socialism after you've had capitalism. Capitalism knows how to make money. Socialism does not. The idea of socialism is that when you have the money, you distribute it fairly.

episodes.title: Artificial meat is harder than artificial intelligence — Lewis Bollard
episodes.guest_title: Farm Animal Welfare Program Director, Open Philanthropy
episodes.summary: Lewis Bollard argues that the end of factory farming is far from inevitable, and that technological progress may not solve the problem due to cultural and political obstacles. He claims that the efficiency of factory farming has historically increased alongside technological advancements, leading to more animal suffering. Bollard suggests that animal welfare advocates should focus on government and corporate reforms, as well as supporting alternative protein research, rather than relying solely on individual dietary choices.
key_highlights: The end of factory farming is not inevitable
Technology has historically increased factory farming efficiency
Cultural and political obstacles impede alternatives to factory farming
Plant-based meat is not enough to change cultural preferences
Cultivated meat faces political opposition and may be illegal
AGI may accelerate alternative proteins and humane technology
Animal welfare advocates should focus on government and corporate reforms
episodes.quotes: "The basic efficiency is that the animal, and the chicken in particular, has evolved over a very long time to be a being that can take in a relatively small amount of grain and convert it very efficiently into a form of protein that people like to eat."

episodes.title: All You Want to Know About ChatGPT - Aaron “Ronnie” Chatterji | Endgame #227
episodes.summary: Chatterji discusses the role of education in STEM fields in the age of AI, arguing that STEM education is still valuable because it teaches people how to think. He also discusses the importance of building trust in AI products, and how AI can be used to improve the distribution of public goods. He emphasizes the importance of the human element in AI decision-making.
key_highlights: STEM education teaches people how to think, not just how to code
AI can improve public goods distribution if used correctly
AI can help Indonesia attract more foreign direct investment
AI needs to be designed to build trust with users
Humans must remain in the loop for AI decision-making
episodes.quotes: “Your undergraduate degree is a reflection of learning how to think in a particular way.”

episodes.title: Sarah Paine — How Imperial Japan defeated Tsarist Russia & Qing China
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Strategy and Policy, Naval War College
episodes.summary: Sarah Paine argues that Japan's rise in Asia was due to a combination of Westernization, mastery of grand strategy, and seizing the culminating point of victory in the Russo-Japanese War.  She contrasts Japan's success with China's implosion, attributing this to civil wars, European imperialism, and the collapse of Manchu rule. Paine emphasizes that Russia's expansionist ambitions in Asia, particularly its construction of the Trans-Siberian Railway, prompted Japan's strategic actions, including forming an alliance with Britain and engaging in psychological operations.
key_highlights: Japan's success was due to Westernization, grand strategy, and seizing the culminating point of victory
China's implosion was due to civil wars, imperialism, and collapse of Manchu rule
Russia's expansionist ambitions in Asia prompted Japan's strategic actions
The Anglo-Japanese alliance was key to isolating Russia
Japan's military build-up was funded by loans
episodes.quotes: Japan in this battle is just taking anybody, young boys, old people, whoever they can put into that army, they're putting into it.

episodes.title: Stephen Kotkin — How Stalin became the most powerful dictator in history
episodes.summary: Stephen Kotkin discusses the nature of Stalin's terror, arguing that it was unique in its self-cannibalizing nature, as it consumed even the police who carried out the murders. He contrasts this with the Tsarist regime, which was repressive but not self-destructive, and argues that the intelligentsia's belief in Marxism-Leninism was a key factor in the Russian Revolution. Kotkin also discusses the difficulty of creating a stable political order in a mass society, and the role of ideology in shaping political outcomes.
key_highlights: Stalin's terror was unique in its self-cannibalizing nature, consuming even the police
Tsarist regime was repressive but not self-destructive
Marxism-Leninism empowered the intellectual class
The need to compete in the international system drives modernization
The world order rests on the back of farmers
The importance of a stable political order in a mass society
episodes.quotes: "The thing about Stalin's terror is the police are also murdered while they are doing the murdering."

episodes.title: A billion years of evolution in a single afternoon — George Church
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Genetics, Harvard Medical School
episodes.summary: George Church discusses the potential and risks of biotechnology, including the possibility of creating a life-destroying weapon. He argues that biotechnology is enabling smaller and smaller efforts, making them harder to detect, but that it also holds the key to almost perfect health. He also discusses the potential for biotechnology to improve mechanical and electrical engineering, and the possibility of creating room-temperature superconductors.
key_highlights: Biotechnology could wipe out all competing life if properly weaponized
Biotechnology enables smaller and smaller efforts, harder to detect
Biotechnology could lead to almost perfect health
Evolution might incorporate a few base pair changes in a million years
Biotechnology could improve mechanical and electrical engineering
We might even finally get a room-temperature superconductor that way
episodes.quotes: "This seems like the sort of thing that could wipe out all competing life if it were properly weaponized."

episodes.title: Why China's manufacturing economy is dominating — Arthur Kroeber
episodes.summary: Arthur Kroeber argues that China's economic success is due to its unique combination of central planning and export-driven manufacturing, which has allowed it to capture a large share of the global market. He contrasts this with Japan's more protected domestic market and the US's focus on financialization. Kroeber notes that China's high debt levels are a concern, but that it is uniquely positioned to manage these risks due to its state-controlled financial system and its ability to focus on long-term goals.
key_highlights: China's economic success is due to its export-driven manufacturing and central planning
China's government is willing to lose huge amounts of money
China is like a giant VC fund
The US is going to try and constrain China
China's answer is to make it impossible to build the block that you want
China's ambition is to produce all the manufactured goods in the world
The US is over 100% in debt to GDP
episodes.quotes: "I will operate so that it is impossible for you to build the block that you want."

episodes.title: "China is digging out of a crisis. And America’s luck is wearing thin." — Ken Rogoff
episodes.summary: Ken Rogoff argues that China's economic model, while impressive, is now facing a crisis due to overbuilding and financial repression. He contrasts this with the US, which has a more dynamic economy but is hampered by political polarization and high debt. Rogoff suggests that the US has been lucky in avoiding financial crises but may face a reckoning if it doesn't address its fiscal imbalances and that the Fed’s independence is increasingly threatened.
key_highlights: China's economic model is facing a crisis due to overbuilding and financial repression
China's leadership competence is high, but loyalty is now prioritized
The US has been lucky in avoiding financial crises
US political polarization threatens the Fed's independence
US debt is high, but it can print money to honor its debts
The US has a dynamic economy, but needs to rebalance saving, investment, and consumption
The US is ahead in tech, but China is catching up
episodes.quotes: "The Chinese are way better than we are... of sort of hearing a hundred different views... mine would be one of many, many, many that they heard."

episodes.title: Xi Jinping’s paranoid approach to AGI, debt crisis, & Politburo politics — Victor Shih
episodes.guest_title: Director of the 21st Century China Center at UC San Diego
episodes.summary: Victor Shih argues that Xi Jinping's paranoia about losing control is driving China's approach to AI, prioritizing control over innovation. China's economic system, while appearing centralized, is actually decentralized at the provincial level, but this is being reined in by Xi. The Chinese government's focus on output over profit leads to misallocation of resources and a lack of innovation. The Chinese Communist Party is not interested in making people happy, but rather in preserving its own power.
key_highlights: AI is a high priority for Xi Jinping due to the party's paranoia about losing power
China's leadership meets 270 days a year on policy
No succession plan is in place for Xi Jinping
China was fiscally decentralized, but is becoming more centralized
China's economic system prioritizes output over profit
The Chinese government is afraid of subversive content
episodes.quotes: "If Xi Jinping expresses a preference over a policy direction, that is the direction you have to go toward, no matter what. If he observes that you're dragging your feet, you will be purged."

episodes.title: Thailand's First Silicon Valley Impact VC Talks Trump, Life After Intel - Jeep Kline | Endgame #221
episodes.guest_title: Managing Partner and Founder at RaceWell Ventures
episodes.summary: Jeep Kline discusses the unique position of Southeast Asia in the US-China geopolitical competition, arguing that the region can benefit from its neutrality. She emphasizes the importance of investing in deep tech and hardware in Southeast Asia, while also acknowledging the structural impediments to growth, such as political instability and weak rule of law. Kline advocates for a shift in mindset towards entrepreneurship and a focus on impact-driven venture capital.
key_highlights: Southeast Asia benefits from US-China geopolitics due to its neutrality
Region should absorb economic development and scalability from the US
Industries change rapidly, so Southeast Asia must stay on top of it
AI revolution is a new opportunity for Southeast Asia
Southeast Asians must become very hungry to compete on a global stage
Need to improve productivity and efficiency in Southeast Asia
Impact-driven VC is key to long-term sustainable growth
episodes.quotes: The geopolitics between US and China, it actually benefit us. We should be the region that absorb all of the economic development and the scalability that would come from the US.

episodes.title: Is RL + LLMs enough for AGI? — Sholto Douglas & Trenton Bricken
episodes.summary: Sholto Douglas and Trenton Bricken discuss the progress of AI in the past year, focusing on the integration of Reinforcement Learning (RL) and Large Language Models (LLMs).
