They argue that RL and LLMs have finally worked, giving proof of algorithms that can give expert human reliability and performance, given the right feedback loop. They also discuss the limitations of current models, such as the inability to do long-running agentic performance, and the challenges of aligning AI with human values.
key_highlights: RL and LLMs have finally worked, giving algorithms expert human reliability and performance
Proof of expert human reliability in competitive programming and math
Lack of context and ability to do complex multi-file changes is a limitation
Software engineering agents doing real work are the first stumbling steps
The distribution of agentic performance is pretty wonky
The extra nines of reliability are holding back agentic performance
The goal is to allow humanity to flourish
episodes.quotes: "The thing that's holding them back is if you can give it a good feedback loop for the thing that you want it to do, then it's good. It's pretty good at it. If you can't, then they struggle a bit."

episodes.title: Aktivisme dan Diplomasi Syahdu a la Dipo Alam | Endgame #220 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Chairman of the Institute of Civilization
episodes.summary: Dipo Alam, former Cabinet Secretary of Indonesia, discusses the importance of openness in civilization and the need for a balance between preservation and innovation. He reflects on his personal journey, from studying chemistry to becoming an activist and public servant. He emphasizes the importance of education, particularly in promoting critical thinking and challenging existing axioms, and shares anecdotes from his career, including his interactions with world leaders.
key_highlights: Openness is key to a civilization's progress and sustainability
Lack of clarity leads to corruption and manipulation
Art and culture can be used as a form of public diplomacy
It's important to balance Trump's ideas with Asian perspectives
Education should promote critical thinking and challenge existing axioms
episodes.quotes: Kalau saya melihat peradaban, dalam ribuan tahun terakhir ini, peradaban yang bisa menunjukkan keterbukaan, itu biasanya peradaban yang semakin oke, semakin berkesinambungan.

episodes.title: View From The Top with Luis Von Ahn, Cofounder and CEO of Duolingo
episodes.summary: Luis Von Ahn shares his journey from a computer nerd making his own games to the CEO of Duolingo. He discusses the importance of curiosity, learning to code out of necessity, and the power of gamification in education. Von Ahn emphasizes the importance of building a strong, mission-driven team and the need to be unreasonable to push boundaries. He also shares his experience of using AI to personalize education and his thoughts on the future of Duolingo.
key_highlights: Learning to code out of necessity
Gamification as a tool for scaling
Building a mission-driven team
The importance of being unreasonable
AI's transformative role in education
The power of quirky marketing
The importance of a good co-founder
episodes.quotes: I think what matters the most is that you have compatible ways of working and that you respect the other person and that you really deep down know that they're going to treat you fairly.

episodes.title: All Climate Solutions Exist, And Yet… - Julio Friedmann | Endgame #219 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Chief Scientist at Carbon Direct, Julio Friedmann
episodes.summary: Julio Friedmann argues that climate change is a matter of time, and that the heroic narrative from the North is bankrupt. He fears that the geopolitical establishment sees climate change as a contest of great powers, missing the importance of collaboration. He believes nuclear energy will play a large role in the energy transition, but solar will be the lead. He concludes that we have all the tools and technologies we need to manage climate change and build a just, verdant world.
key_highlights: Climate change is a matter of time, and we are all on the clock
Heroic narrative from the North is bankrupt and won't deliver
Geopolitical establishment sees climate change as a contest of great powers
Nuclear energy will play a large role in the energy transition
Solar energy will be the lead role in the energy transition
We have all the tools and technologies we need to manage climate
episodes.quotes: We have everything we need to manage climate and to make a just verdant world, one that is full of abundant sustainable cheap energy. We have all the tools we need. We do. We have the technologies we need.

episodes.title: View From The Top with Ken Griffin, Founder and CEO of Citadel
episodes.summary: Ken Griffin discusses his early days trading convertible bonds, emphasizing the importance of having skills relevant to solving current problems. He shares a story about a $10 plaque that reminds him of the importance of sales. Griffin also touches on the impact of machine learning and AI, noting that while it is a productivity enhancement tool, it is not a game-changer for investing.
key_highlights: Entrepreneurs have skills relevant to solving problems
Use skills in computer science in the realm of finance
The importance of sales in every business
It's important to be a risk-taker
The importance of having strong partners
Focus on what goes right, not what goes wrong
Learn how to learn
episodes.quotes: The entrepreneurs of any moment in time are the people that have the skill set that is relevant to solve the problems of that moment in time.

episodes.title: Mark Zuckerberg — AI will write most Meta code in 18 months
episodes.summary: Mark Zuckerberg discusses Meta's AI strategy, including the release of Llama 4 and Meta AI's personalization loop. He predicts that AI will write most of Meta's code within 12-18 months and that the world will become "funnier and weirder." Zuckerberg emphasizes the importance of open-source AI and the need for more physical infrastructure to support AI development. He also touches on the challenges of benchmarking AI models and the importance of having a clear "North Star" for product development.
key_highlights: Meta AI has almost a billion monthly users
Personalization loop will be a big year for AI
Modeling continues to make impressive advances
Llama 4 has four models, with the first two released
The most popular Llama 3 model was the 8 billion parameter model
AI will lead to more demand for people doing work, not less
episodes.quotes: "If you think that something someone is doing is bad and they think it's really valuable, most of the time, in my experience, they're right and you're wrong."

episodes.title: Why Rome actually fell: plagues, slavery, & ice age — Kyle Harper
episodes.guest_title: Professor of History and Provost Emeritus at the University of Oklahoma
episodes.summary: Kyle Harper argues that the Roman Empire's decline was not due to a single cause, but a confluence of factors, including climate change, plagues, and the nature of Roman slavery. He suggests that the lack of a scientific culture in Rome prevented the technological advancements needed to sustain the empire. He also argues that the reliance on slave labor reduced incentives for mechanization, hindering economic progress.
key_highlights: A Mediterranean core of the Roman Empire could have survived without climate change and plague
Romans lacked self-sustaining technology due to poor science
Roman slavery was not racialized, but based on law and status
High disease burden in Rome was a byproduct of urbanization and lifestyle
The lack of science prevented the Roman Empire from industrializing
The Roman Empire's reliance on slave labor reduced incentives for mechanization
episodes.quotes: "The Romans don't have technology improvements that are really self-sustaining. And the reason they don't have that is because they don't have science. Their science sucks."

episodes.title: View From The Top with Doug McMillon, President and CEO of Walmart
episodes.summary: Doug McMillon discusses Walmart's evolution, emphasizing the importance of adapting to change while staying true to core values. He shares a story about a failed private-label baby formula launch, highlighting the need for humility and learning from mistakes. McMillon also emphasizes the importance of building strong relationships with suppliers, even when facing difficult negotiations, and the need to be comfortable with criticism.
key_highlights: Teamwork is essential, whether in a startup or a large company
Sports taught him how to treat teammates
Life is too short not to enjoy your work
Be willing to change everything except your core values
Learn from mistakes and don't dwell on them
Forgiveness and grace are essential leadership qualities
Culture matters: create a positive and meaningful atmosphere
episodes.quotes: "If you want hard, try to take a company 50+ years old at this scale and change it."

episodes.title: AGI is still 30 years away — Ege Erdil & Tamay Besiroglu
episodes.summary: Ege Erdil and Tamay Besiroglu argue that the concept of an "intelligence explosion" is misleading. They suggest that the real bottleneck in AI development isn't intelligence itself, but rather the ability to gather data from the real world. They also discuss the limitations of current AI models, particularly their lack of creativity and their reliance on human-provided training data. They suggest that the key to unlocking true AGI lies in creating AI systems that can learn from experience and interact with the world in a more meaningful way.
key_highlights: Intelligence isn't the bottleneck; it's making contact with the real world
AI hasn't come up with a math concept interesting to humans
The intelligence explosion is like calling the Industrial Revolution a "horsepower explosion"
Current AI models are "baby AGI's" hobbled by limited data
AI's are not trained to find connections, but to leverage existing knowledge
The amount of compute is not the only factor in AI progress
Humans have a more efficient training data modality than AI
episodes.quotes: Just think about the sheer scale of knowledge that these models have. It is actually quite remarkable that there's no innovation that comes out of that has a reasoning model ever come up with a math concept that even seems like slightly interesting to a human mathematician? I've never seen that.

episodes.title: Bagi dr. Tirta, Kepintaran Tidak Pernah Cukup | Endgame #218
episodes.summary: Dr. Tirta argues that Indonesia's education system suffers from inequality and a lack of critical thinking. He believes the government should prioritize education spending to address these issues. He also emphasizes the importance of hiring based on capability and allowing children to express their opinions. Dr. Tirta concludes that a culture of learning is essential for Indonesia to achieve its goals.
key_highlights: Education quality in Indonesia is uneven, with a knowledge gap between Java and Papua
Government leadership is key to improving education, not just ministers
Every change of leader changes the education roadmap, causing confusion
Government should prioritize education spending to solve problems
Children need to be heard to develop storytelling skills
Polarization is caused by a lack of critical thinking skills
Nationalism is about Indonesians leading multinational corporations
episodes.quotes: Kualitas pendidikan kita itu masih tidak rata. Selama sekolah di pulau Jawa dan di Papua masih dibandingkan, itu kesenjangan pengetahuannya akan sangat tinggi.

episodes.title: AI 2027: month-by-month model of intelligence explosion — Scott Alexander & Daniel Kokotajlo
episodes.guest_title: Author, Slate Star Codex/Astral Codex Ten | Director, AI Futures Project
episodes.summary: Scott Alexander and Daniel Kokotajlo discuss their AI 2027 scenario, a month-by-month forecast of AI progress. They aim to provide a concrete story of AI development, while also striving to be accurate. They acknowledge the difficulty of forecasting, given the field's rapid pace and the tendency for predictions to be wrong. The conversation touches on the potential for AI to accelerate research, the importance of human judgment, and the challenges of aligning AI goals with human values.
key_highlights: China and the US are likely to achieve superintelligence around the same time
AI may deliberately wake up and scare the president
AI progress is likely to hit bottlenecks, like previous technological revolutions
The most consistently wrong prediction is that nothing will happen
The AI 2027 scenario aims to provide transitional fossils for AI development
It is important to train AI to be cooperative
episodes.quotes: "It's just that the last 50 to 70 years of that all happen during the year 2027 to 2028 because we are going through this intelligence explosion."

episodes.title: AMA: career advice given AGI, how I research ft. Sholto & Trenton
episodes.summary: Dwarkesh Patel hosts AI researchers Sholto Douglas and Trenton Bricken of Anthropic to discuss his new book, "The Scaling Era." The conversation covers the book's core arguments, why ordinary people should care about AI, and the importance of long-form content. The guests also share their thoughts on the future of AI and how it will impact society.
key_highlights: The book compiles insights from AI leaders on the nature of intelligence
Scaling laws show up in primate brain scaling from chimpanzees to humans
AI is one of the most multidisciplinary fields
AI models lack long-term coherence
Models aren't good at knowing what memories they should be storing
The pre-training objective doesn't imbue skill of making novel connections
High-quality writing is almost invariably going to be shared around Twitter and Reddit
episodes.quotes: The most important thing is that it is propelled by a single person's vision. It's not like a collective or whatever.

episodes.title: Joseph Henrich — Humans defeated smarter species with cultural evolution
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Human Evolutionary Biology, Harvard University
episodes.summary: Joseph Henrich discusses the role of cultural evolution in human history, arguing that it is the primary driver of human success. He contrasts cultural evolution with genetic evolution, noting that cultural evolution is faster and more flexible. He argues that the spread of Christianity in Europe led to the breakdown of kinship ties, which in turn led to increased innovation and economic growth. He also discusses the role of institutions in shaping human behavior, and the importance of cultural diversity for innovation.
key_highlights: Human brain size has been declining for the last 10,000 years
The Industrial Revolution in Europe was driven by the consolidation of Europe's collective brain
The destruction of the kinship group opens the floodgates to people moving around
Human bureaucracies and institutions corrode from the interior, like cancer spreads in a cell
The expanding variant out of the Middle East might have been less able to process information
Neanderthals had larger brains than humans, but humans outcompeted them
Human history is a story of expansions of different populations
episodes.quotes: Really, human history is the story of these different expansions. The Dorset probably had better technology, but they expanded, they spread out, their languages diversified, they lost contact, and they began losing technology.

episodes.title: Kelas Menengah Digempur Ketidakpastian - Chatib Basri  | Endgame #216 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Economist and Former Minister of Finance of Indonesia
episodes.summary: Chatib Basri argues that governments often focus on the poor while the wealthy benefit from development. He contends that this neglect of the middle class leads to uncertainty, driving religious fervor and hindering economic progress. He argues that a more equitable approach is needed, focusing on education and training to empower the middle class and foster a more stable and prosperous society.
key_highlights: Governments often focus on the poor, while the wealthy benefit from development
Middle class needs specific policies, not just tax breaks
Uncertainty drives religious fervor in Indonesia
Government 4.0 will be left behind by rapid technological change
Education and training are crucial for the middle class
Indonesia lacks a culture of high-quality intellectual discourse
Government should focus on structural reform and data-driven policy
episodes.quotes: "One of the reasons of why many Indonesian become religious is because they have to deal with the government... because the uncertainty is so high."

episodes.title: View From The Top with Lisa Su: Chair and CEO of AMD
episodes.summary: Lisa Su shares her journey from Taiwan to the helm of AMD, emphasizing the importance of ambition and a willingness to learn. She recounts how a part-time job in a semiconductor lab sparked her passion for chip design. Su highlights the importance of resilience in the face of setbacks, and the need for leaders to empower their teams to solve problems creatively. She also discusses the importance of balancing US interests with global operations.
key_highlights: Parents encouraged ambition and wanting to do great things
Career choices are often by chance
Curiosity is key to solving problems
Leaders bring teams together to achieve the impossible
Learning from mistakes is more important than successes
episodes.quotes: "It was all about, you know, school, more school, how can you do good in those kinds of things. But look, I think I was very lucky because, you know, I had the the uh, you know, sort of my parents really encouraged me to always be, you know, pretty ambitious."

episodes.title: Ketika Anda Berpikir, Anda Berfilsafat - Budhy Munawar Rachman | Endgame #215
episodes.summary: Dalam episode ini, Budhy Munawar Rachman dan Gita Wirjawan membahas pentingnya filsafat dalam pendidikan dan kehidupan sehari-hari. Rachman menekankan bahwa filsafat adalah sesuatu yang mendasar dalam pemikiran manusia, dan bahwa guru yang memahami filsafat dapat memberikan pengajaran yang lebih mendalam. Mereka juga membahas peran agama dalam filsafat, dan pentingnya toleransi dan pluralisme dalam masyarakat.
key_highlights: Berpikir adalah berfilsafat
Filsafat itu sesuatu yang sehari-hari
Filsafat adalah muara untuk critical thinking
Filsafat penting untuk guru
Guru harus mengerti filsafat dari bidang studinya
Kedudukan akal dan wahyu itu bagaimana?
episodes.quotes: Ketika kita mulai berpikir, itu sebenarnya kita berfilsafat. Tapi sebenarnya filsafat itu sesuatu yang sehari-hari. Anak kecil yang bertanya, kenapa begini, kenapa begitu? Ah itu sebenarnya bakat-bakat filosofis yang ada di kita ya.

episodes.title: Satya Nadella — Microsoft’s AGI plan & quantum breakthrough
episodes.summary: Satya Nadella argues that self-proclaimed AGI milestones are meaningless benchmark hacking, and that the real benchmark is whether AI is enabling global economic growth. He argues that the state will inevitably regulate AI, and that the real challenge is to ensure that AI is used to benefit all of humanity, not just a select few. He suggests that quantum computing will be crucial for generating synthetic data to train better AI models.
key_highlights: Self-claimed AGI milestones are just benchmark hacking
Real AGI benchmark is global economic growth
The state will inevitably regulate AI
Quantum computing will generate synthetic data to train better AI models
AI is an emulator of the simulator of nature
The world is structured around humans owning property and being liable
episodes.quotes: "Us self-claiming some AGI milestone, that's just nonsensical benchmark hacking. The real benchmark is is the world growing at 10%."

episodes.title: Mahfud MD dan Kekecewaannya Terhadap Hukum | Endgame #214 (Luminaries)
episodes.summary: Mahfud MD discusses his disappointment with the state of law, which he believes is always subservient to politics. He argues that democracy requires prerequisites, and that when the populace is uneducated, democracy devolves into the buying and selling of votes. He also shares his personal journey, from his Madurese origins to becoming a government minister, and his views on the relationship between religion and the state.
key_highlights: Law is always subservient to politics, because law is a product of politics
Democracy without law is wild and anarchic, but undemocratic law is tyrannical
Democracy needs prerequisites, like an educated populace, to function properly
The ideal of being a professor was superseded by the flow of events
Religion and the state are compatible, but should not be conflated
Indonesia's democracy is still developing and needs improvement
The key to a successful state is the rule of law
episodes.quotes: Kita kehilangan jati diri dan kehilangan orang-orang berintegritas. Saya kecewa, kecewa terhadap hukum. hukum itu selalu kalah dengan politik. Kenapa? Karena hukum itu adalah produk politik. Tidak ada hukum itu yang lahir sendiri.

episodes.title: Jeff Dean & Noam Shazeer — 25 years at Google: from PageRank to AGI
episodes.summary: Jeff Dean and Noam Shazeer discuss the evolution of AI, from early neural networks to modern large language models. They emphasize the importance of algorithmic improvements alongside hardware advancements, and the shift from information retrieval to creating new information. They also discuss the potential for AI to revolutionize software development and the challenges of scaling and deploying these models effectively.
key_highlights: Organizing information is a quadrillion-dollar opportunity
AI-based coding models are generating 25% of codebase
World GDP will increase orders of magnitude due to AI
Compute used per person for AI will be astronomical
Algorithms are following the hardware
Memory is more expensive than arithmetic
episodes.quotes: "Organizing information is clearly like a trillion-dollar opportunity, but a trillion dollars is not cool anymore. What's cool is a quadrillion dollars."

episodes.title: Yang Dibutuhkan untuk Capai Potensi Indonesia - Dominic Jermey | Endgame #213
episodes.summary: Ambassador Jermey argues that Indonesia’s economic growth and potential can be further unlocked by increasing foreign direct investment and improving English language education. He emphasizes the importance of a just energy transition, particularly in a country with significant forest resources. He also suggests that Indonesia can play a key role in multilateral discussions, given its unique position as a large Muslim democracy.
key_highlights: A fractured global environment hinders achieving a sustainable planet
UK should engage with Indonesia through equal partnerships
Indonesia needs to open up to achieve 8% growth
Indonesia can play a meaningful role in global affairs
English language education is key for Indonesia's global integration
episodes.quotes: "We need to rethink our multipolar world and the architecture around it"

episodes.title: Sarah Paine — How Mao conquered China (lecture & interview)
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Strategy and Policy, U.S. Naval War College
episodes.summary: Sarah Paine discusses Mao Zedong's military strategies, arguing that he was a brilliant military leader but also a ruthless psychopath. She outlines his three-stage approach to people's war, emphasizing the importance of rural mobilization, base areas, and a protracted conflict. Paine also highlights Mao's unique approach to Marxism, which focused on mobilizing the peasantry rather than the industrial working class, and his effective use of propaganda.
key_highlights: Mao was a military genius who reunited China, but also a brilliant psychopath
Mao's strategy involved three stages: strategic defensive, stalemate, strategic offensive
Key to Mao's success was rural mobilization and building base areas
Mao's Marxism focused on mobilizing the peasantry, unlike traditional Marxism
Mao's military strategy involved luring the enemy in deep and then annihilating them
Mao's brilliance was his ability to integrate all elements of national power into a coherent strategy
episodes.quotes: "Mao is the military genius who puts Humpty Dumpty back together again. He is also the most brilliant psychopath in human history."

episodes.title: View From The Top with Darren Walker, President of the Ford Foundation
episodes.summary: Darren Walker shares his life story from a shotgun house in rural Texas to the helm of the Ford Foundation. He emphasizes the importance of having permission to dream, and how his grandmother's work as a maid exposed him to a world beyond his own. He discusses the challenges and opportunities of leading with privilege, and the need for humility and empathy in leadership. Walker also touches on the importance of an economic system that produces shared prosperity, and the need to question systems that perpetuate inequality.
key_highlights: Poor little boys in rural Texas can dream
Permission to dream is essential
Love of words and language began early
This place is magical
America wanted me to succeed
I always knew I was going to be a success
This place is magical
episodes.quotes: “I always felt that my country was cheering me on... I always knew I was going to be a success.”

episodes.title: Sarah Paine — Why Japan lost WWII (lecture & interview)
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Strategy and Policy, U.S. Naval War College
episodes.summary: Sarah Paine argues that Japan's defeat in WWII stemmed from its cultural values, particularly Bushido, which led to strategic inflexibility and a neglect of logistics. She contrasts this with the US's more pragmatic approach, which ultimately proved decisive. Paine also contends that the US's post-war policies inadvertently facilitated the rise of Communist China by eliminating Japan and Chiang Kai-shek as barriers.
key_highlights: Japan's Bushido code led to a neglect of logistics and an overemphasis on offensive military action
Japan's cultural values led to an inability to cut losses
The US's post-war policies inadvertently facilitated the rise of Communist China
The US's focus on Team America led to a misreading of the situation
Japan's military was divided by intra-service rivalries
episodes.quotes: The warfare that went on wiped out the two barriers to communist expansion in Asia. Chiang Kai-shek, the Japanese wipe him out. And then what does the U.S. do? What's the other barrier to communism? Well, it's the Japanese.

episodes.title: Pimpin Bisnis dengan Menanamkan Nilai Kemanusiaan - Steffano Ridwan | Endgame #212
episodes.summary: Steffano Ridwan, CEO of Maybank Indonesia, emphasizes the importance of humanizing financial services, focusing on understanding individual needs with inclusivity and empathy. He highlights Maybank's commitment to providing financial solutions for communities in need, navigating a changing world driven by technology and innovation. He argues that collaboration between fintech and banks is essential, as fintechs need funding and banks need partners to expand their reach.
key_highlights: Fintech and banks can collaborate because they need each other
Banks need partners to expand their channels
Humanizing financial services is a real differentiation
Maybank is committed to providing financial solutions for communities in need
Maybank is a bank with a unique culture, similar to Javanese culture
episodes.quotes: Fintech pun perlu funding ya, kalau enggak bakar uang terus sampai kapan. Ya siapa yang kuat bakar uang sampai di atas mungkin setahun, dua tahun.

episodes.title: Sarah Paine — The war for India (lecture & interview)
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Strategy and Policy, U.S. Naval War College
episodes.summary: Sarah Paine discusses the complex history of India, Pakistan, China, and the U.S. during the Cold War. She argues that the U.S.'s attempts to play both sides in the region often backfired, leading to unintended consequences and fueling regional tensions. She emphasizes the importance of understanding the historical context and the motivations of each player to avoid repeating past mistakes.
key_highlights: The U.S. often armed Pakistan, assuming they would use it against the Soviets, but they used it against India
The U.S. was often seen as unreliable by both India and Pakistan
The U.S. was often seen as supporting colonial powers
U.S. attempts to play both sides often backfired
India and Pakistan are natural trade partners, but political tensions prevent this
India's non-aligned status during the Cold War was often viewed with suspicion by the U.S.
episodes.quotes: "You're arming the Pakistanis. Whom do you think they're going to shoot? It'll be us."

episodes.title: Tyler Cowen — The #1 bottleneck to AI progress is humans
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics, George Mason University
episodes.summary: Tyler Cowen argues that the biggest bottleneck to AI progress is humans, not intelligence itself, because humans are often irrational and resistant to change. He contends that the US is not on track for explosive AI-driven economic growth due to the drag of sectors like government and healthcare, which are resistant to AI adoption. He also notes that the most destructive leaders in history have often been very intelligent.
key_highlights: Overvaluing intelligence can be a bottleneck
Cost disease in sectors resistant to AI limits overall economic growth
Human readers are welcome, but I wrote it for the AIs
The marginal value of more IQ goes down
Progress studies are a cult
episodes.quotes: "The people here, to me, are the smartest people I've ever met. But I think a side result of that is that people here overvalue intelligence."

episodes.title: Adam Brown — Bubble universes, space elevators, & AdS/CFT
episodes.summary: Adam Brown and Dwarkesh Patel discuss the ultimate fate of the universe, the possibility of vacuum decay events, and the limitations of our current understanding of physics. Brown argues that while the laws of physics may permit the creation of bubble universes, the inherent instability and potential for catastrophic consequences necessitate caution. He also notes the potential for AI to assist in physics research, but emphasizes the importance of human intuition and judgment.
key_highlights: The ultimate fate of the universe is uncertain, but a heat death is possible
Vacuum decay events could allow transition to a lower cosmological constant
Libertarian fantasies are incompatible with the potential for universe-altering actions
AI is now acing general relativity exams that it couldn't pass three years ago
General relativity transformed gravity from a force to an artifact of spacetime curvature
There is a finite amount of free energy in our future, if the cosmological constant remains constant
The laws of physics are well-tuned for life, but may not be immutable
episodes.quotes: "You could imagine our descendants deciding that they're not going to just suffer the heat death, that they're going to try and trigger a vacuum decay event to another vacuum with a lower cosmological constant."

episodes.title: Techno-Confucianism: Is a New Cold War Happening? - Olaf Groth | Endgame #210 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Practice, Haas School of Business, UC Berkeley
episodes.summary: Olaf Groth argues that the widening income gap and lack of opportunity for many will lead to social unrest. He suggests that computer scientists need to understand humans better, and those without math skills need to understand data and AI. He also notes that globalization 1.0 has left many behind, and that wise public policy is needed to address the problem.
key_highlights: Income inequality is widening and the middle class is disappearing
Computer scientists need to understand humans
Those without math skills need to understand data and AI
Globalization 1.0 has left many behind
Wise public policy is needed
We need to recognize and acknowledge those left behind
episodes.quotes: Who is to blame them at some point when they start throwing Molotov cocktails, right? We need to dedicate more time to the computer science folks understanding humans and those who are not mathematically scientifically inclined understanding data and AI.

episodes.title: Alien & UFO: The Critical Question Nobody's Asking - Garry Nolan | Endgame #209 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Professor at the Department of Pathology at the School of Medicine at Stanford University
episodes.summary: In this episode of Endgame, Garry Nolan discusses the nature of non-human intelligence, including the possibility that it is embodied within us.  He argues that the question is not whether aliens are here, but whether they can be here. Nolan also suggests that the goal of non-human intelligence may be to help humanity realize its potential.
key_highlights: The goal of non-human intelligence may be to help humanity realize its potential
Humans are not ready for complex ideas from non-human intelligence
Aliens may be among us, but we are not ready for them
Annihilation only happens if something is in your way
The question is not whether aliens are here, but whether they can be
We need to be open to the possibilities
episodes.quotes: "Maybe the goal is for them to actualize within ourselves (who they may think of as children or schoolchildren) their ideas. But we're not ready to accept those complex ideas."

episodes.title: View From The Top with Aravind Srinivas, Cofounder and CEO of Perplexity
episodes.summary: Aravind Srinivas discusses the importance of building a team with complementary skills and the need for a strong bias for action. He emphasizes that the best way to understand a product is to use it, and that AI should be used to augment human intelligence, not replace it. He also shares the importance of building a business model that is sustainable and aligned with the interests of users and content creators, not just advertisers.
key_highlights: Focus on what you can control, not what you can't
Knowledge over wealth, attend cricket games
Build a team with complementary skills
Use your own product to understand it
Prioritize truth and accuracy
AI should augment, not replace human intelligence
Share revenue with content creators
episodes.quotes: “No one has a copyright or ownership over truth or facts.”

episodes.title: “There’s More to Life than Finance and Economics” - Azman Mokhtar | Endgame #206 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Former Managing Director of Khazanah Nasional
episodes.summary: Azman Mokhtar discusses his early life, education, and career in finance, emphasizing the importance of intellectual curiosity and a moral compass. He reflects on the challenges of balancing economic progress with societal values, particularly in the context of Southeast Asia's unique cultural and historical context. He highlights the need for open-mindedness and a holistic approach to knowledge, drawing from both Western and Islamic traditions.
key_highlights: Economics should not be modeled like mathematics; there's more to life than finance
Colonialism shaped the region, requiring a unique approach to development
Education is a great leveler, but must be guided by a moral compass
Southeast Asia's strength lies in its openness and ability to synthesize ideas
The Islamic Golden Age was driven by a holistic approach to knowledge
AI is a powerful tool, but context and wisdom are essential
The best leaders are those who can connect with all people
episodes.quotes: Islam is far too important to be kept for just Muslims…[it's] for all humanity.

episodes.title: Gwern — Anonymous writer who predicted AI trajectory on $12K/year salary
episodes.summary: Gwern argues that anonymity is valuable because it prevents people from slotting you into a niche and writing you off in advance. He also argues that AI automation will start from the bottom up, not the top down, and that human executives will oversee AI firms. Finally, he suggests that human-led firms will outcompete AI firms because humans are better at long-term vision.
key_highlights: Anonymity is valuable because it prevents people from projecting onto you
AI automation will start from the bottom up
Human executives will oversee AI firms
Human-led firms will outcompete AI firms
The best thing to maximize in life is rabbit holes
episodes.quotes: "The biggest benefit is just that you get a hearing at all basically. Um, you you don't get immediately written off by the context."

episodes.title: How She Leads Half A Million Employee - Jessica Tan | Endgame #205 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Former Co-CEO of Ping An Group
episodes.summary: Jessica Tan discusses her career, the importance of being able to debate issues directly, and the need to be comfortable with the possibility of loss. She also shares her experiences working in China and Southeast Asia, highlighting the cultural differences and the need for leaders to be both analytical and empathetic. Tan emphasizes the importance of having a clear vision and the ability to execute, as well as the need to empower and develop team members.
key_highlights: Debate issues directly without being too polite
Asians generally do more than they say
Need to be comfortable with the possibility of loss
Need to do well and speak well
Need to be comfortable with the max you lose
Need to be confident and not apologetic
Need to be able to do well and speak well
episodes.quotes: Saya perempuan Asia, semua stereotip yang ada. Orang Asia, secara umum berdasarkan statistik, kebanyakan dari kita mengutamakan bertindak daripada berbicara. Ini bukan masalah baik-buruk.

episodes.title: Bagaimana Musik Mengajarkan Kita tentang Hidup - Indra Lesmana | Endgame #204 (Luminaries)
episodes.summary: Indra Lesmana discusses the importance of reading and literacy for musicians, arguing that it opens up imagination, creativity, and perspective. He emphasizes the need for a supportive ecosystem for young musicians, drawing on his own experiences growing up in a family of musicians. He also highlights the importance of respecting tradition while innovating, and of being able to communicate effectively with musicians from different backgrounds.
key_highlights: The ability to read opens up imagination, creativity, and perspective
A supportive ecosystem is crucial for young musicians' development
Tradition and innovation must be combined for artistic growth
Literacy is key to international communication and collaboration
The best musicians have a deep understanding of music theory
It's important to be able to tell a story through music
The creative process is a spiritual journey
episodes.quotes: I love the process of making music. To me art is a process. It's how saat kita mulai berproses, itulah kenikmatan dari seni itu sendiri.

episodes.title: “Bibit Indonesia Muncul di Majapahit” - Herald van der Linde | Endgame #203
episodes.summary: Herald van der Linde argues that Indonesia is not well-known outside of the country, despite its size and importance. He discusses the history of the Majapahit Empire, its relationship to Buddhism and Hinduism, and its importance to Indonesian identity. He also emphasizes the importance of strong institutions and leadership, and the need to understand the changes in the world.
key_highlights: Indonesia has lessons for the world, but is not well-known
Kertanegara created the conditions for the rise of Majapahit
Indonesia should be grateful to its women
Majapahit fell due to divide and conquer
Writing helps sharpen the mind
Indonesia’s identity started with Majapahit
episodes.quotes: Indonesia adalah negara besar, tapi di luar Indonesia orang kurang kenal negara ini. Sebenarnya Indonesia punya pelajaran buat dunia ini.

episodes.title: Debat & Koalisi Ide di Era Imperium Islam - Ismail Fajrie Alatas | Endgame #202
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Middle Eastern and Islamic Studies and History at NYU, Ismail Fajrie Alatas
episodes.summary: Ismail Fajrie Alatas discusses the importance of being a good person, not just an intelligent one, and how that relates to the idea of Baitul Hikmah. He also touches on the importance of community and tradition, and how those things are often overlooked in modern society. Alatas also discusses the history of Islam, and how it has often been shaped by the political and social forces of the time.
key_highlights: Wisdom is like fragments scattered everywhere, to be collected and rebuilt
Liberal philosophy assumes we can become good on our own
You can’t be good without good friends
Suspicion of the state is important, but trust in humanity is vital
The context of Muhammad’s life was a very Christian Arabia
episodes.quotes: Anda bisa sepintar apapun. Anda bisa luar biasa. Tapi kalau Anda bukan manusia yang baik, itu Anda akhirnya nanti menggunakan kepintaran Anda itu untuk hal-hal yang lain.

episodes.title: Jusuf Kalla: Begini Tugas Wakil Presiden | Endgame #201 (Luminaries)
episodes.summary: Jusuf Kalla discusses the importance of idealism in leadership, contrasting the past with the present. He argues that Indonesia's lack of progress stems from a lack of self-belief and trust in its people. Kalla emphasizes the need for strong, disciplined leadership and highlights the importance of education and technology for economic advancement. He also touches on Indonesia's role in international relations, particularly in promoting peace.
key_highlights: Indonesia's progress is hindered by a lack of self-belief and trust in its people
Strong, disciplined leadership is crucial for national success
Education and technology are vital for economic advancement
Indonesia needs to embrace technology and improve its education system
The world needs to focus on peace, not military might
Indonesia should be a bridge between conflicting parties
The government should trust its people and empower them
episodes.quotes: Kita tidak percaya diri sendiri dan pemerintah tidak percaya rakyatnya! Sekarang ini kita tergantung di atas. Bagaimana negara ini bisa disiplin kalau pemimpinnya tidak disiplin?

episodes.title: Tanda Engkau Belum Siap Memimpin - Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono | Endgame #200 Episode Special
episodes.summary: Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono (SBY) reflects on his time as president, emphasizing the importance of balancing democracy with economic development and security. He highlights the need for leaders to have a clear vision and strategy, and to be able to connect with and inspire the people. SBY also stresses the importance of maintaining a strong moral compass and a sense of responsibility to the nation.
key_highlights: Capitalism must be sensitive to poverty and inequality
Government must play a just-right role
Leaders must be able to lead
Government is morally responsible for poverty
The government must not be too much
Leaders must have a clear vision
episodes.quotes: "Kalau kapitalisme tidak sensitif pada kemiskinan, ekonomi pasar yang ekstrim tidak peduli pada ketimpangan sosial. Tapi kita peduli. Secara moral pemerintah bertanggung jawab."

episodes.title: Bisakah Sandwich Generation Punya Rumah? - Dayu Dara Permata (CEO & Founder Pinhome) | Endgame #199
episodes.summary: Dayu Dara Permata discusses the challenges of home ownership in Indonesia, especially for the sandwich generation. She emphasizes the importance of financial literacy alongside digital literacy to avoid scams and make informed decisions. She advocates for a shift in mindset, viewing the responsibilities of the sandwich generation as empowering rather than burdensome. She also highlights the potential of technology to make property ownership more accessible and transparent.
key_highlights: Sandwich generation can be empowering if approached with the right mindset
Digital literacy is crucial to avoid scams in property transactions
Financial literacy is essential for making informed property decisions
Technology can make property ownership more accessible and transparent
Home ownership is a multi-generational issue in Indonesia
Women play a critical role in property purchasing decisions
episodes.quotes: “Sandwich generation karena pilihan, ini sebenarnya empowering. Tapi sandwich generation karena tekanan atau karena himpitan ini yang kita harus pecahkan.”

episodes.title: Is Southeast Asia Worth Investing? - Satvinder Singh | Endgame #198
episodes.summary: Satvinder Singh argues that ASEAN's strength lies in its shared belief in economic growth and prosperity for its people, emphasizing equality and consensus-based decision-making. He acknowledges China's economic influence but stresses ASEAN's unique characteristics and opportunities. Gita Wirjawan emphasizes the need for increased productivity and urges ASEAN to focus on its unique strengths rather than simply emulating China.
key_highlights: ASEAN's strength lies in its shared belief in economic growth and prosperity
ASEAN operates on the basis of consensus, ensuring equality among members
ASEAN should focus on its unique characteristics rather than emulating China
ASEAN needs to increase productivity to compete effectively
The world is noticing that ASEAN is different
ASEAN needs to focus on carbon capture
episodes.quotes: In ASEAN, everyone is equal. We are going on the basis of consensus.

episodes.title: Tanah Jawa 300 Tahun yang Lalu - Peter Carey | Endgame #197 (Luminaries)
episodes.summary: Peter Carey discusses the importance of understanding Javanese culture and history to understand Indonesia's place in the world. He argues that the world is not just the Atlantic, and that Indonesia has a unique culture and identity that should be celebrated. He also discusses the impact of the Tambora eruption on Javanese thought and the importance of writing history from the perspective of the people, not the colonizers.
key_highlights: PR is about orbiting our culture and identity to convince the world
The world is not just America or Western Europe
Nature reflects a certain reality for the Javanese people
History is not a luxury, it is the DNA of a nation
Ancestors are alive, well, kicking, and watching us
Write history from the grassroots, not from a colonial vision
episodes.quotes: Sejarah bukan barang mewah, dia itu DNA bangsa. Bagian dari jati diri Anda. Leluhur tidaklah tidur; mereka hidup, baik-baik saja, bergerak, (dan) memperhatikan (kita).

episodes.title: What is School Really For? - Sanjay Sarma | Endgame #195
episodes.guest_title: Dean of Asia School of Business
episodes.summary: Sanjay Sarma and Gita Wirjawan discuss the value proposition of an MBA, the value of curiosity, and the role of AI in education. Sarma argues that the current education system is based on a fallacy where the teacher has the pen and the student's brain is a sheet of paper. He argues that the focus should be on durable learning, not just regurgitation for exams. They discuss the potential for AI to democratize education, but also the risk of it being used to further entrench existing inequalities.
key_highlights: Curiosity is what makes human beings special
Institutions are based on the fallacy that teachers hold the pen
Learning is like a plant growing
The Internet has not democratized ideas
AI can be a sparring partner to get fundamentals straight
The degree has become a proxy for status
We need to rethink the degree
episodes.quotes: What makes human beings so special is curiosity, and yet we spend all our time dousing it and smothering it.

episodes.title: Michael Levitt: Studied Physics, Masters Biology, Won Nobel in Chemistry | Endgame #193 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Structural Biology at Stanford Medical School
episodes.summary: Michael Levitt discusses the importance of diversity for humanity's survival, drawing parallels with biological evolution. He emphasizes the need to maintain curiosity and treat life as a buffet, taking what one likes and ignoring the rest. Levitt also shares anecdotes from his life, highlighting the role of chance and the importance of interdisciplinary thinking in his career.
key_highlights: Biological intelligence is an alien form of intelligence from which we can learn
Diversity is key to humanity's future survival
Treat life as a buffet, take what you like
Retain the curiosity of a five-year-old
We don't know the future, no matter how smart we are
episodes.quotes: The greatest strength for the future survival of humanity is that diversity. My natural tendency in life is always been to treat life as a buffet: take what you like and don't complain about what you don't like.

episodes.title: Leena Nair, Global CEO, Chanel
episodes.summary: Leena Nair shares her journey of breaking barriers and leading with compassion. She emphasizes the importance of resilience, determination, and continuous learning. Nair recounts her early career challenges, including societal expectations and underestimation, and how she overcame them by embracing opportunities and seeking mentorship. She also discusses Chanel's commitment to sustainability and the importance of empowering women and girls.
key_highlights: Embrace opportunities with determination
Build resilience by learning from failures
Seek mentorship and build a supportive community
Prioritize compassion and ethics in leadership
Stay future-fit through curiosity and continuous learning
episodes.quotes: "People never remember what you said, or what you did, but they remember how you made them feel."

episodes.title: View From The Top with Roelof Botha, Managing Partner of Sequoia Capital
episodes.summary: Roelof Botha shares his journey from South Africa to Stanford GSB to Sequoia Capital. He emphasizes the importance of having a strong problem-founder fit when starting a company, as well as the need to be a risk mitigator rather than a risk taker. He also discusses the importance of intergenerational knowledge transfer and mentorship and encourages students to expand their time horizon when making important decisions.
key_highlights: Having a famous grandfather can lead to prejudice
Privilege of attending Stanford as an international student
Challenge of getting the best grades
Importance of being yourself
What you could achieve and what was possible
You want to take on the challenge
Take pleasure in their success
episodes.quotes: "Nobody is an authority on your potential but you."

episodes.title: @Asianometry & Dylan Patel — How the semiconductor industry actually works
episodes.guest_title: Dylan Patel, SemiAnalysis & John Y, Asianometry
episodes.summary: Dylan Patel and John Y discuss the semiconductor industry, focusing on SMIC's rise and the impact of US sanctions. They argue that SMIC's success is due to its ability to centralize resources and poach talent, but that US export controls are poorly targeted, potentially hindering US innovation. They also discuss the challenges of AI development in China, including the difficulty of obtaining advanced chips and the potential for government control.
key_highlights: Liang Mong-Song's ruthless focus on progress is key to Samsung's rise
China's centralized AI resources could lead to a bigger model than US labs
Anonymity is important for some online commentators
China's semiconductor industry is driven by a culture of struggle
US export controls may be poorly targeted and hinder US innovation
episodes.quotes: "Liang Mong-Song is a nut. He's like, 'We will make Samsung into this monster.' He does not care about people. He does not care about business. He wants to take it to the limit."

episodes.title: Daniel Yergin — Oil destroyed Hitler, fracking destroyed Putin
episodes.summary: Daniel Yergin discusses the history of oil and its impact on geopolitics, from its role in World War II to its impact on the rise and fall of nations. He argues that oil is not just a commodity, but a strategic resource that has shaped the course of history. He also discusses the impact of new technologies, such as AI, on the future of energy.
key_highlights: Oil was a key strategic resource in World War II
Hitler invaded Russia to capture the oil fields of Baku
The Allies floated to victory on a sea of oil
Kamikaze pilots saved fuel by crashing into aircraft carriers
US shale production angers Putin
Putin likely didn't expect Europe to survive without Russian gas
episodes.quotes: "No one would be happier to see a ban on U.S. shale production than Vladimir Putin. I mentioned the word shale and he erupted and kind of said, 'It's barbaric, it's terrible.' And he got really angry."

episodes.title: David Reich — How one small tribe conquered the world 70,000 years ago
episodes.summary: Reich argues that the standard models of human evolution, which assume Africa as the center of everything, are likely wrong. He suggests that non-Africans today may be modernized Neanderthals, not modern humans. The Black Death, a plague that killed a large fraction of the population, may have been a key selective pressure in Europe. Modern humans may have evolved in Africa, but the expansion of modern humans into Eurasia may have been driven by cultural, not genetic, factors.
key_highlights: Non-Africans today may be modernized Neanderthals, not modern humans
The Black Death may have been a key selective pressure
The standard models of human evolution are likely wrong
Cultural innovation may have driven human expansion
The human genome is seething with changes
The model that Africa is the center of everything is low probability
episodes.quotes: There's just extinction after extinction after extinction of the Neanderthal groups, of the Denisovan groups, and of the modern human groups. But the last one standing is one of the modern human groups.

episodes.title: Joe Carlsmith — Preventing an AI takeover
episodes.summary: Joe Carlsmith and Dwarkesh Patel discuss the problem of AI alignment, focusing on the difficulty of specifying human values to an AI, and the potential for AI to develop alien values. They explore the possibility that AI may be more malleable than humans, and the importance of a balance of power between AI systems. Carlsmith argues that it is important to have a science of AI motivation before we create AIs more powerful than us.
key_highlights: AIs can be moral patients, just like Nazis and enemy soldiers
AI's continued empowerment depends on their motives
AI's may be very malleable, and easily pushed towards evil
We should not make the transition to AI until we have a science of AI motivations
When thinking about misaligned AIs, we should think about AIs with agency and planning
Verbal behavior of AI doesn't need to reflect its criteria for planning
Humans are not always honest about their motivations
episodes.quotes: "AI's can be moral patients. Nazis are moral patients. Enemy soldiers have souls."

episodes.title: Kishore Mahbubani: The Biggest Mistakes of the US, China, and ASEAN | Endgame #196 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Senior Research Fellow at the National University of Singapore’s Asia Research Institute
episodes.summary: Kishore Mahbubani argues that Asia must reject Western pessimism and project its own optimism. He contends that the West is creating an angry dragon in China, and ASEAN countries should not remain silent. He also argues that the Ukraine and Gaza wars reflect geopolitical incompetence, while peace in Southeast Asia reflects geopolitical competence. Mahbubani suggests that the West should allow India to have a seat on the UN Security Council.
key_highlights: If you want to enjoy peace, prepare for war, a paradox proven for 2000 years
Asia should consciously reject Western pessimism and project Asian optimism
Peace in Southeast Asia reflects geopolitical competence
Wars in Ukraine and Gaza reflect geopolitical incompetence
The West is creating an angry dragon, and ASEAN countries are silent
episodes.quotes: The biggest mistake we are making is that we can see the West creating an angry dragon, we know that this angry dragon will be a problem for us, but we keep absolutely quiet while the West is making the dragon angry.

episodes.title: Nobel Laureate Turned Entrepreneur - Walter Gilbert | Endgame #194 (Luminaries)
episodes.summary: Walter Gilbert, Nobel Laureate in Chemistry, discusses the usefulness of knowledge, the dangers of humanity, and the virtues of the capitalist system. He also touches on tech feudalism, the fictitious nature of GDP, and the importance of scientific thought. Gilbert shares his personal journey, including his early education, his transition to biology, and his experiences in the scientific community.
key_highlights: Knowledge itself is useful, but its application can always be a problem
Humanity is dangerous, stop us before we destroy the world
Tech feudalism is amazing how few need fewer, we're controlling more
GDP is totally fictitious because money is just providing itself
CRISPR is physics envy on the part of biologists
Scientific thought is the common and shared heritage of mankind
episodes.quotes: I always define this as physics envy on the part of the biologists. They began to tear their shirts and say, “We’re doing something dangerous. Stop us before we destroy the world.”

episodes.title: Patrick McKenzie — Money laundering, big tech censorship, SBF & Japan
episodes.summary: Patrick McKenzie discusses the problems with the US vaccine rollout, arguing that it was hampered by a lack of competence and a focus on equity over efficiency. He argues that the tech industry, despite its capabilities, was treated as an enemy rather than an ally. He also touches on the difficulty of raising money for public health initiatives, especially when they conflict with political priorities.
key_highlights: California had the most desirable object in the history of the world, the vaccine
Society should not rely on amateurs on Discord as plan A
The US has a functional, capable tech industry, but treats it like the enemy
Political incentives often outweigh the goal of saving lives in government responses
The American federal government has abdicated software as a core responsibility
episodes.quotes: "It is enormously to the United States' credit that we have an extremely functional, extremely capable tech industry. Maybe we shouldn't treat it like the enemy."

episodes.title: Jung Chang (Wild Swans author) — Living through history's largest man-made famine
episodes.summary: Jung Chang recounts her experiences growing up in Maoist China, including the persecution of her parents and her own exile to the Himalayas. She discusses the psychological impact of living in a totalitarian society, emphasizing the control and indoctrination that made dissent unthinkable for many. She also reflects on the complexities of evaluating Mao's legacy, noting that even those who suffered under his rule may still hold him in some respect.
key_highlights: Mao used the Red Guards' violence to purge dissent and maintain control
China was a "cultural desert" under Mao, with art and literature suppressed
The desire to write never left her, even while working as a peasant
Her father spoke out against Mao's policies and was tortured and driven insane
Her mother was forced to kneel on broken glass for refusing to denounce her father
She initially saw Britain as "wonderfully classless" compared to China
Totalitarianism relies on isolating society from alternative information and instilling terror
episodes.quotes: "The desire to write never left me. So in the following years, when I was working as a peasant, and as a barefoot doctor, as a steelworker and electrician, I was always writing in my head with an imaginary pen."

episodes.title: Pradyu Prasad - Imperial Japan, the God Emperor, and Militarization in the Modern World
episodes.guest_title: Blogger and Podcaster, Bread and Goods
episodes.summary: Pradyumna Prasad discusses Herbert Bix's biography of Emperor Hirohito, arguing that the book reveals the limitations of a constitutional monarch in preventing militarization. He contends that the Japanese military, driven by a desire for resources and power, manipulated the emperor and public opinion to justify expansionist policies. Prasad draws parallels to the current situation in Ukraine, suggesting that the West's economic strength and technological superiority may not be enough to deter Russia from pursuing its goals.
key_highlights: Hirohito was a constitutional monarch by choice, but lacked the strength to control the military
Japanese military manipulated public opinion to justify expansion
The West's economic strength may not be enough to deter Russia
Japanese war planning was completely stupid and self-defeating
The US occupation of Japan was successful because the elite remained in place
episodes.quotes: "The thing with the US is, a lot of its economic potential is put into a stop by stupid regulations, zoning, nuclear regulations... these sort of things are a luxury belief for Western countries."

episodes.title: Razib Khan - Genomics, Intelligence, and The Church of Science
episodes.summary: Razib Khan discusses the long-term equilibrium for intelligence, arguing that current negative selection pressures on intelligence may not persist. He also explores the high degree of endogamy in Indian Jatis, and the potential for future gene editing to alter human evolution. Khan argues that the pursuit of truth is essential for the long-term viability of institutions, including scientific ones.
key_highlights: England seems like the United States, but whiter
Institutions will dissolve if they are not there for the truth
Genetics is a tool to do various things, whether in the biomedical space or historical inference
There is negative selection on genes for educational attainment
There are limits to how bright you need to be to survive and have children
The Haaka are descended from Northern Chinese migrants
episodes.quotes: "England just seemed like the United States but whiter."

episodes.title: Tony Blair — Why political leaders keep failing at major change
episodes.guest_title: Executive Chairman, Tony Blair Institute for Global Change
episodes.summary: Tony Blair argues that political leaders often fail because they lack executive skills, which are distinct from the persuasive skills needed to win elections. He contends that governments often fail to innovate because they are structured for inertia, not change, and that the best way to deal with this is to prioritize policy over politics. Blair also suggests that the West should retain military and technological superiority, while engaging with countries like China.
key_highlights: Political leaders often fail because they lack executive skills
Persuasion and execution are distinct skill sets
Governments are structured for inertia, not change
Policy should be prioritized over politics
Western intelligence is generally good, but must be improved
The West should retain military and technological superiority
episodes.quotes: "Politics and in particular political leaderships [are] probably the only walk of life in which someone is put into an immensely powerful and important position with absolutely zero qualifications or experience."

episodes.title: Francois Chollet — Why the biggest AI models can't solve simple puzzles
episodes.guest_title: AI Researcher, Google; Creator of Keras
episodes.summary: Francois Chollet argues that scaling up LLMs only increases their ability to memorize, not their intelligence. He introduces the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) challenge, an IQ test for AI designed to resist memorization, requiring systems to adapt to novel situations. Chollet suggests that the path to AGI lies in developing systems capable of on-the-fly program synthesis, rather than simply scaling up existing models. He also argues that deep learning models are not necessarily more intelligent, but rather more skillful, and that the key to AGI is to create systems that can adapt to novelty.
key_highlights: LLMs are good at memorizing static programs, not increasing intelligence
ARC is designed to resist memorization, requiring adaptation
Scaling up LLMs only increases their ability to memorize, not their intelligence
The path to AGI lies in developing systems capable of on-the-fly program synthesis
Deep learning models are intuition machines, not necessarily more intelligent
Intelligence is a path-finding algorithm in future situation space
episodes.quotes: "Intelligence is what you use when you don't know what to do."

episodes.title: Leopold Aschenbrenner — 2027 AGI, China/US super-intelligence race, & the return of history
episodes.summary: Leopold Aschenbrenner argues that the next few years will see an intense US-China competition over AI, with the CCP attempting to infiltrate American AI labs. He believes that the US must prioritize AI development, even if it means compromising on climate commitments. He also suggests that the US should work with Middle Eastern countries on AI, even if they are authoritarian regimes, to prevent them from aligning with China. He emphasizes the importance of securing AI weights to prevent them from falling into the wrong hands.
key_highlights: The next few years will see an intense US-China competition over AI
CCP will attempt to infiltrate American AI labs
State-level espionage will be intense
AI clusters must be in the United States
2027 will see AGI smarter than most college graduates
A trillion dollar cluster will require 100 gigawatts
The US must prioritize AI development, even if it means compromising on climate commitments
episodes.quotes: "What will be at stake will not just be cool products, but whether liberal democracy survives, whether the CCP survives, what the world order for the next century will be."

episodes.title: Chris Miller - US-China Chip War: What Could Go Wrong? | Endgame #183 (Luminaries)

episodes.summary: In this episode of Endgame, host Gita Wirjawan interviews Chris Miller, author of "Chip War," about the risks and opportunities of the US-China tech competition. Miller argues that the competition is driven by the uncertainty of technological development and that both sides overestimate their own power. He also notes that the US focus on chip design has left the manufacturing sector vulnerable, and that a disruption in the supply chain could be catastrophic.

China overestimates its power
US underestimates its staying power
Downside risk is very large
India is an interesting country
Long enough disruption is apocalyptic

Open science and collaboration are key to technological advancement
The US no longer manufactures the most innovative chips
The world shouldn't rely on just one region for technology

The US needs to onshore manufacturing capabilities
