episodes.title: Bangga Dibesarkan di Kampung | Endgame ft. Khofifah Indar Parawansa (Part 1)
episodes.summary: Khofifah Indar Parawansa discusses her childhood in Surabaya, emphasizing the strong sense of community and the values instilled by her mother. She contrasts the learning environment of her youth, which focused on practical experience and collaboration, with the more structured and technology-driven education of today. She also touches on the importance of preserving Indonesia's natural beauty and cultural traditions, particularly in East Java.
key_highlights: Childhood in Surabaya instilled strong community values and a love for nature
Learning by doing was key to education, contrasting with today's structured methods
Importance of maintaining Indonesia's natural beauty and cultural traditions
Strong emphasis on gotong royong (mutual assistance) and leadership from a young age
Concern about the impact of excessive screen time on children's development
episodes.quotes: Pak Gita, jangan dianggap kalau di Jakarta bukan belantara loh.

episodes.title: Potensi Tanpa Aktualisasi Buat Apa? | Endgame ft. Nadhira Afifa (Part 3)
episodes.summary: Nadhira Afifa and Gita Wirjawan discuss Indonesia's future in 2045, focusing on healthcare and education. Afifa argues Indonesia lags in healthcare compared to other developing nations, emphasizing the need for improved coordination between healthcare organizations. She envisions a future where Indonesia maximizes its demographic potential, but stresses the importance of prioritizing research and development and addressing the challenges of the BPJS healthcare system.
key_highlights: Indonesia lags in healthcare compared to other developing nations
Need for better coordination between healthcare organizations
Indonesia's demographic bonus must be maximized
Research and development needs prioritization
BPJS is generous but faces financial challenges
Need to prepare for future pandemics
Social distancing is more important than herd immunity
episodes.quotes: Indonesia nih ketinggalan banget loh gitu. Dari segi kemampuan SDM-nya menurut aku udah kayak negara yang menuju ke negara maju gitu. Tapi ternyata dari segi kesehatan tuh masih kayak negara-negara di Afrika, negara-negara yang sangat terbelakang gitu.

episodes.title: New Normal Bukan Karena Sudah Bebas Covid | Endgame ft. Nadhira Afifa (Part 2)
episodes.summary: Gita Wirjawan and Nadhira Afifa discuss the role of public health policy in improving productivity in Indonesia. They explore how addressing issues like malnutrition and anemia in adolescents can significantly boost the nation's economic output. The conversation then shifts to the ethical implications of emerging technologies like Neuralink and the challenges of balancing economic recovery with public health concerns during the COVID-19 pandemic.
key_highlights: Addressing malnutrition and anemia in adolescents is crucial for Indonesia's productivity
Neuralink's ethical implications must be considered alongside its potential benefits
Prioritizing public health is essential, but balancing it with economic needs is challenging
Telemedicine's potential to collapse costs and improve healthcare access is significant
Asymmetric information is a key risk factor in public health crises
The pandemic has made digitalization inevitable in various sectors
Collaboration with telecommunication companies is key to improve connectivity
episodes.quotes: Dalam logika kan kalau kita sehat, kita lebih produktif kan? Pastinya, pastinya. Kalau kita enggak kena stunting, kita enggak kena diabetis, kita enggak kena penyakit-penyakit lainnya, semestinya kan kita bisa lebih produktif kan?

episodes.title: Profesi Dokter Bukan Sekadar Prestise | Endgame ft. Nadhira Afifa (Part 1)
episodes.summary: Nadhira Afifa discusses her upbringing in Jakarta, her education, and her passion for medicine. She emphasizes the importance of human interaction and empathy in healthcare, arguing that these aspects cannot be replaced by technology. Afifa also touches on the challenges of addressing health issues like stunting and diabetes in Indonesia, highlighting the need for improved public health education and access to affordable, healthy food.
key_highlights: Growing up in Jakarta, Afifa was influenced by her mother's high expectations
Interaction with patients is the core of a doctor's irreplaceable role
Early fear of trying new things was overcome by parental encouragement
Successes of siblings opened doors and influenced Afifa's path
The need to balance dedication to profession with financial sustainability
Stunting is a complex issue, not just about poverty
Indonesia needs to empower local health cadres and improve public health education
episodes.quotes: Menurut aku itu satu-satunya peran dokter yang enggak bisa digantiin mesin gitu. Kita dari interaksi sama pasien, empati, dari mengerti apa yang pasien omongin.

episodes.title: Creating Super App to Rival Gojek & Grab | Endgame ft. Tony Fernandes (Part 3)
episodes.summary: Tony Fernandes discusses the future of ASEAN and the importance of economic and cultural integration. He argues that Indonesia is well-positioned to lead ASEAN's economic growth. Fernandes also shares his vision for Capital A, emphasizing its transition into a technology company focused on financial inclusion and e-commerce. He believes that Capital A can leverage its existing data and logistics infrastructure to create a more efficient and equitable ecosystem for merchants and consumers.
key_highlights: ASEAN's primary objective is maintaining peace, a goal it has successfully achieved
Protectionism due to COVID-19 will eventually fade
Indonesia is pro-business and will drive growth
Data is the biggest asset for AirAsia
Indonesia can lead ASEAN's tech revolution
Capital A is pivoting to become a tech company
Indonesia should lead ASEAN into economic cooperation
episodes.quotes: “I think Indonesia has more to gain from ASEAN than to lose… it’s about opening up the market”

episodes.title: Life Has No Rewind Button | Endgame ft. Tony Fernandes (Part 2)
episodes.summary: Tony Fernandes recounts his career in music and aviation, emphasizing the importance of taking risks and not being afraid to fail. He discusses the challenges of competing against national carriers and the need for Southeast Asian governments to balance COVID-19 concerns with the need to unlock tourism. Fernandes also reflects on the importance of having a vision and the need to empower people.
key_highlights: Asian parents should not live through their kids; it's important to live your own life
The importance of digging deeper into reports to find trends
It's important to take risks and not look back twice
Asian culture tends to overthink decisions
As a leader, it's important to empower people
Tourism is critical to Southeast Asia's economy
episodes.quotes: You've got to get behind the numbers, right? Otherwise, it's just numbers. You've got to take some risks.

episodes.title: AirAsia Founder Talks Childhood Dreams | Endgame ft. Tony Fernandes (Part 1)
episodes.summary: Tony Fernandes discusses his early life, including his time at boarding school in England, and his initial career in the music industry. He describes his parents' contrasting personalities and how they influenced him. Fernandes also touches on his early exposure to racism and his belief in capitalism with wealth redistribution, and his early career in the music industry.
key_highlights: Timing is everything, not just the right idea
Richard Branson thought starting an airline was a stupid idea
Doesn't believe in horoscopes, but many in Asia do
Many successful people started out with a dream
ASEAN has 700 million people, incredible cultural diversity
ASEAN has incredible tourism product and amazing food
AirAsia brought ASEAN closer together
episodes.quotes: When I started AirAsia, the first guy to call me up was Richard Branson. He said, 'Hey, I thought it was a stupid idea to start an airline.'

episodes.title: Stop Mengotak-ngotakkan Musisi | Endgame ft. Stephanie Poetri (Part 3)
episodes.summary: Stephanie Poetri discusses the future of the Indonesian music industry, emphasizing the need for greater diversity and support for artists outside Jakarta. She highlights the limitations imposed by labels on artists' creative freedom and the importance of breaking down age-related barriers. Poetri expresses optimism about the potential for Indonesian music to achieve international recognition, drawing parallels with the success of K-pop and Latin music.
key_highlights: Labels should allow artists to make their own path, not force a vibe
Age should not be a barrier to success in the industry
Music and talent are universal regardless of age
Need to democratize labels so artists don't need to attach to them
Customers must push for the music they want to hear
The ability to mobilize people helps to help many people
episodes.quotes: I feel like even for the soundtrack for… kita cerita apa? … I know the English one. It’s like, it’s not, you wouldn’t say it’s completely pop, right? But it’s like… like five years ago.

episodes.title: Keindonesiaan vs Tuntutan Go Internasional | Endgame ft. Stephanie Poetri (Part 2)
episodes.summary: Stephanie Poetri discusses her creative process, influences, and aspirations in music. She emphasizes the importance of authenticity and connecting with her audience, and the unique opportunities and challenges of being an Indonesian artist in the global music scene. She also touches on the role of 88Rising in promoting Asian talent and the importance of a social safety net for musicians.
key_highlights: 88Rising gives artists creative freedom
Balancing authenticity with commercial success is a challenge
Indonesian hospitality is a valuable asset
Music is a facade
Promoting Asian talents is a great mission
episodes.quotes: "All they did was provide me every single like um support and options and I was the one who like chose."

episodes.title: Tunda Kuliah untuk Bermusik | Endgame ft. Stephanie Poetri (Part 1)
episodes.summary: In this episode of Endgame, Gita Wirjawan interviews Stephanie Poetri, a popular Indonesian singer-songwriter. They discuss Stephanie's background growing up in Indonesia, her early musical influences, and her decision to take a gap year to pursue music. Stephanie shares her experiences navigating the music industry and her desire to connect with her Indonesian identity while pursuing a global career.
key_highlights: Stephanie's mother, Titi DJ, is a famous singer, which shaped her childhood
Growing up, Stephanie was known as Titi DJ's daughter
Stephanie's father taught her how to write country songs
She wants to write songs for other people
Stephanie's friends influenced her to get into K-pop
She regrets not spending more time with her friends in high school
episodes.quotes: I started realizing that like if I don't do anything about it, I'm wasting all of the lessons that my mom and dad gave me, you know, without them even realizing it.

episodes.title: Mimpi Kopi Kenangan Jadi Pemain Global | #Endgame ft. Edward Tirtanata (Part 3)
episodes.summary: Edward Tirtanata discusses the future of Kopi Kenangan, emphasizing the importance of brand equity over simply being the cheapest option. He highlights the shift towards digital adoption and the need for businesses to adapt to changing consumer behaviors. Tirtanata also stresses the importance of investing in brand equity, quality, and service to create customer loyalty and a sustainable business model, drawing comparisons to global brands like Starbucks.
key_highlights: Brand equity is more important than being the cheapest option
Customer loyalty is key to long-term success
Digital adoption is a game-changer for the coffee industry
Investing in quality and service is crucial for brand building
Focus on the customer experience and building brand equity
Education is key to adapting to the future of work
episodes.quotes: The only true benchmark is order.

episodes.title: Jebakan Rasa Cepat Puas | #Endgame ft. Edward Tirtanata Kopi Kenangan (Part 2)
episodes.summary: Edward Tirtanata discusses the importance of engineering teams in Beijing to overcome talent supply issues in Indonesia. He emphasizes the need for a collective effort to create academies for training engineers. Tirtanata also highlights the importance of math over English in pitching to investors, and the need for paranoia to avoid complacency and ensure long-term success. He shares Kopi Kenangan's focus on same-store sales growth and its approach to selecting investors based on added value beyond capital.
key_highlights: Engineering talent is scarce in Indonesia, necessitating a Beijing-based engineering team
Indonesia has plenty of talented engineers, but they are under-supplied
Kopi Kenangan is ready for disruption
Indonesia needs more academies to train engineers
Math is more important than English in pitching to investors
Success breeds complacency
Only the paranoid survive
episodes.quotes: "You never pitch with English, because English is open for argument. You use math."

episodes.title: Kegagalan Berbuah Kenangan | #Endgame ft. Edward Tirtanata Kopi Kenangan (Part 1)
episodes.summary: Gita Wirjawan interviews Edward Tirtanata, founder of Kopi Kenangan, about his journey as an entrepreneur. Tirtanata discusses his early life, including a turning point in college when his family faced financial hardship, which motivated him to take his studies more seriously. He also talks about his early business ventures and how his experiences have shaped his approach to building Kopi Kenangan.
key_highlights: Edward Tirtanata's family faced financial hardship in college, motivating him to study
Tirtanata started his career in natural resources with his father
Tirtanata realized that he wanted to be in consumer goods
Tirtanata's business philosophy is to start small and fail fast
Tirtanata is inspired by Jeff Bezos's disruptive mentality
Tirtanata believes that time is more valuable than money
episodes.quotes: If I don't think this is gonna be big, I don't want to do it.

episodes.title: Indonesia Bertanding Tak Sesuai Kelas | #Endgame ft. Sandiaga Uno (Part 3)
episodes.guest_title: Minister of Tourism and Creative Economy, Indonesia
episodes.summary: Sandiaga Uno discusses Indonesia's future, advocating for a focus on domestic development and a neutral foreign policy. He emphasizes the importance of equal opportunity over equal welfare, highlighting the need for structural reforms. He argues that Indonesia's low FDI per capita reflects its underperformance on the international stage, and that the country should leverage its strategic position to become a peacemaker.
key_highlights: Indonesia should focus on domestic development and structural reforms
Equal opportunity is more important than equal welfare
Indonesia should leverage its strategic position to become a peacemaker
Indonesia should aim to be a middle power
Indonesia's low FDI per capita reflects its underperformance on the international stage
episodes.quotes: We love our country too much to let personal issues get in the way of our relationship. Our relationship is based on our love for the Republic.

episodes.title: Kesempatan Emas UMKM | #Endgame ft. Sandiaga Uno (Part 2)
episodes.guest_title: Minister of Tourism and Creative Economy of Indonesia
episodes.summary: Sandiaga Uno discusses his philanthropic work after the 2019 presidential election, focusing on volunteer groups addressing COVID-19 and supporting UMKM. He highlights the importance of financial inclusion and the need for policies that empower UMKM, not just offer token support. Uno expresses concern that incrementalism in policy implementation hinders progress, emphasizing the need for quick and decisive action to support UMKM and the Indonesian economy.
key_highlights: Post-election, Uno focused on humanitarian efforts, forming volunteer groups to combat COVID-19
UMKM empowerment requires more than just financial aid; it needs knowledge and skills
Bureaucratic red tape hinders effective policy implementation and economic recovery
Digitalization offers a golden opportunity to improve financial inclusion for UMKM
The best of humanity emerges during crises, fostering a spirit of mutual care
Policy should enable people to start a business from home
episodes.quotes: Sejak Pilpres tahun lalu itu gua jobless, Bro. Engga punya pekerjaan. So, oke, rating naik dong!

episodes.title: Ada Manusia di Balik Angka | #Endgame ft. Sandiaga Uno (Part 1)
episodes.guest_title: Minister of Tourism and Creative Economy of Indonesia
episodes.summary: Sandiaga Uno discusses his childhood, including his birth in an oil field in Rumbai, Sumatra, and his education. He reflects on the culture shock of moving to Jakarta at age eight and his early interest in sports. He also shares his thoughts on the importance of human capital allocation and the need for Indonesia to focus on developing its people.
key_highlights: Sandiaga Uno was born in an oil field in Rumbai, Sumatra
He experienced culture shock moving to Jakarta at age eight
He was a mischievous student who loved basketball
He failed to get into UI but studied abroad instead
He believes in the importance of human capital allocation
He emphasizes the importance of preparation for success
He is a fan of Michael Jordan
episodes.quotes: It's good that you know that you don't know. A lot of people they don't know what they don't know. That's what's dangerous, that's what's really bad.

episodes.title: Liliyana Natsir Pasca Pensiun dari Bulutangkis | Endgame S1E03 (3/3)
episodes.summary: Liliyana Natsir discusses her professional badminton career, highlighting the mental toughness required to compete at the highest level. She contrasts the challenges of being an athlete with the risks and rewards of entrepreneurship, emphasizing the importance of financial planning for athletes' post-retirement lives. She also reflects on the role of role models in inspiring future generations of athletes, particularly in women's badminton.
key_highlights: Sang Nan Sayunlei was Liliyana's most challenging opponent due to their complete skill set
Olympic gold is the most rewarding achievement, but it's hard to repeat
It's important to invest bonuses to ensure a comfortable life after retirement
The pressure to win is higher when you are expected to win
Financial literacy is key for athletes to manage their wealth
Role models are essential to inspire future generations of athletes
episodes.quotes: "Jauh Pak, mending pemain bulu tangkis. Kalau pemain bulu tangkis, enggak ada ruginya, untung terus, Pak."

episodes.title: Liliyana Natsir & Pertaruhan Menuju Emas Olimpiade | Endgame S1E03 (2/3)
episodes.summary: Liliyana Natsir discusses the importance of IQ in badminton, defining it as the ability to analyze the game in split seconds. She emphasizes that success requires hard work, physical fitness, and luck. Natsir shares her experiences with her partner, Tontowi Ahmad, highlighting the importance of commitment and the unique challenges they faced on their journey to winning gold at the 2016 Rio Olympics.
key_highlights: IQ in badminton is analyzing the game in split seconds
Success requires hard work, physical fitness, and luck
Teamwork requires communication and understanding between partners
Killer instinct is a mix of natural instinct and learned habits
Commitment and hard work are essential for success
Pressure to win bronze in 2012 felt heavier than gold in 2016
episodes.quotes: "Indonesia ini prestasinya ada di tangan saya gitu."

episodes.title: Liliyana Natsir & Duel Melawan Diri Sendiri | Endgame S1E03 (1/3)
episodes.summary: Liliyana Natsir, known as Butet, shares her journey from Manado to becoming a world-renowned badminton champion. She discusses the challenges of being a regional athlete, the importance of mental strength, and the support she received from her family. Butet emphasizes the need for hard work, discipline, and a bit of luck to succeed in sports.
key_highlights: Regional athletes face confidence issues due to fewer competitions and sparring opportunities
Mental strength is crucial for overcoming challenges and achieving success
Parental support and guidance are essential for an athlete's development
The importance of a supportive environment in achieving success
Need for discipline and hard work to complement talent
Luck plays a role in an athlete's journey
Maintaining a balance between sports and personal life
episodes.quotes: "Rasanya dunia kiamat, Pak. Berpikir kalau kalah-kalah terus ini bisa didegradasi nih, degradasi saya pulang ke Manado nih, aduh."

episodes.title: Pindah Ibu Kota Bukan Solusi | #Endgame #RI75 Special with Prof. Emil Salim (Part 3)
episodes.guest_title: Economist and Former Environment Minister of Indonesia
episodes.summary: Emil Salim discusses Indonesia's environmental challenges, particularly climate change and its impact on the archipelago. He critiques the focus on short-term economic gains over long-term sustainability, and the lack of a holistic approach to development. Salim emphasizes the importance of education and a shift in mindset to prioritize sustainable practices and human capital development, rather than solely exploiting natural resources.
key_highlights: Indonesia's island status makes it vulnerable to climate change and rising sea levels
Short-term economic interests often overshadow long-term sustainability
The focus should be on resource enrichment, not just resource exploitation
Indonesia needs a maritime industry with deep-sea ports to become a hub
The quality of human capital is key to Indonesia's future
Indonesia needs to shift from a resource-based economy to a knowledge-based economy
A well-informed citizenry is essential for a clean democracy
episodes.quotes: “Setiap kali saya melihat di lubang di Papua, pertanyaan saya, berapa banyak dari isi bekas itu ke otak Papua? Non. Berapa banyak dari batubara ini ke otaknya orang Kalimantan? Non.”

episodes.title: Ibarat Pelari Cepat Berlomba Marathon | #Endgame #RI75 Special with Prof. Emil Salim (Part 2)
episodes.guest_title: Economist and Former Minister of Environment, Indonesia
episodes.summary: Emil Salim and Gita Wirjawan discuss Indonesia's education system, the need for a pro-poor development orientation, and the challenges of balancing economic growth with environmental sustainability. Salim expresses concern about declining PISA scores and emphasizes the importance of political will to address systemic issues. He highlights the need for a long-term vision beyond short-term political cycles and stresses the importance of empowering the poor and protecting the environment.
key_highlights: Indonesia's education system needs a pro-poor development orientation
Political parties are not real parties and lack a long-term vision
Decentralization depends on the quality of leadership in the regions
PISA scores are declining, indicating a need for improvement in education
COVID-19 has disrupted the education system and created new challenges
Indonesia's demographic bonus is a key asset for future development
The pursuit of economic growth should not come at the expense of the environment
episodes.quotes: "Orientasi pembangunan kita is not pro-poor, pro small man."

episodes.title: Renungan Tiga Zaman | #Endgame #RI75 Special with Prof. Emil Salim (Part 1)
episodes.guest_title: Economist and Former Minister of Environment, Indonesia
episodes.summary: Gita Wirjawan interviews Professor Emil Salim, an Indonesian economist, about his life experiences across three eras: colonialism, independence, and democracy. Salim discusses his childhood experiences with racial discrimination, his passion for environmentalism and education, and his belief in the importance of empowering the brain. Salim also reflects on the importance of maintaining a sense of community and the need for a more balanced approach to education that incorporates both empirical and social sciences.
key_highlights: Living through colonialism, independence, and democracy shaped Emil Salim's worldview
Racial discrimination fueled Salim's passion for empowerment and social justice
The power of the brain is the ultimate tool for liberation
Digital connectivity can strengthen community, but requires careful management
The importance of balancing empirical and social sciences in education
A need to reclaim the House of Wisdom in the modern era
episodes.quotes: "Kulit kamu itu lain dari kulit temanmu. Jadi kamu tidak boleh karena kulit. Ada apa sama kulit saya?"

episodes.title: Nikmat Itu Kalau Berdaulat | #Endgame with Mardigu Wowiek “Bossman Sontoloyo” (Part 3)
episodes.summary: Mardigu argues that Indonesia should prioritize its own economic sovereignty, even if it means deviating from global norms. He believes Indonesia's economic strength lies in its natural resources and young population, and that these should be leveraged to create a self-reliant economy. He is skeptical of quantitative easing and the reliance on the US dollar, advocating for a more independent monetary policy.
key_highlights: Fiat's market cap is only $4,000 per car sold, while Tesla's is $860,000
Narratives, not just technology, drive market value
Indonesia should focus on its own unique strengths
Quantitative tightening is bad for Indonesia
The goal is to be the number one economy
episodes.quotes: The moment is now. The time is now.

episodes.title: Seize the Moment, Write Your Own Rules | #Endgame with Mardigu Wowiek “Bossman Sontoloyo” (Part 2)
episodes.summary: Mardigu Wowiek argues that Indonesia is in a unique position to benefit from the decoupling of the US and China, and the deglobalization caused by Covid. He believes Indonesia should seize the moment to become a major player on the world stage, and that the current government is not acting quickly enough. He suggests that Indonesia should be more aggressive and less beholden to international organizations like the IMF.
key_highlights: Indonesia should seize the moment to become a major player on the world stage
Indonesia should be more aggressive
Indonesia should be less beholden to international organizations
The current government is not acting quickly enough
The current government is too focused on being a regulator
The current government is not focused enough on the bottom of the pyramid
episodes.quotes: Ambil moment, Cuk!

episodes.title: Narator Bangsa Harus Bisa Berimajinasi | #Endgame with Mardigu Wowiek “Bossman Sontoloyo” (Part 1)
episodes.summary: Mardigu Wowiek, an Indonesian entrepreneur and author, discusses his childhood, education, and career path with host Gita Wirjawan. Mardigu emphasizes the importance of imagination and storytelling in shaping national identity. He argues that Indonesia's strength lies in its rich cultural heritage and the need to promote a culture of reading and critical thinking.
key_highlights: Indonesia needs imaginative narrators to promote its unique cultural heritage
Success is man-made, not innate
The West is not the only path
Indonesia's metal culture is the highest in the world
We need to promote reading and critical thinking
Indonesia's culture is one of storytelling, not reading
episodes.quotes: "People doesn't change. Sekali gua ngeliat barat, gua akan barat sampai mati. Cuman begitu wisdom gua ada, perspektif gua melebar aja. Tapi gua nggak mungkin ke timur."

episodes.title: John Schulman (OpenAI Cofounder) — Reasoning, RLHF, & plan for 2027 AGI
episodes.summary: Schulman discusses the differences between pre-training and post-training, with the former focused on imitating internet content and the latter on aligning models with human preferences. He suggests that in the near future, AI models will be able to carry out entire coding projects, moving beyond simple search engine-like queries. Schulman also emphasizes the importance of careful deployment and coordination among AI developers to avoid potential misuse and ensure safety. He notes that a key challenge is to create models that can reason effectively and avoid simply memorizing vast amounts of data.
key_highlights: Pre-training imitates internet content; post-training aligns models with human preferences
Near-term AI can execute entire coding projects
Post-training helps models adopt a helpful chatbot persona
Models must be calibrated to assign probabilities accurately
Long-horizon tasks require more model intelligence
Models need better error recovery abilities
Coordination among AI developers is crucial for safety
episodes.quotes: "Even in one or two years, you could imagine having the models carry out a whole coding project...doing in collaboration with the model."

episodes.title: View From The Top with Tara VanDerveer, Former Coach of Stanford Women’s Basketball
episodes.guest_title: Former Coach of Stanford Women’s Basketball
episodes.summary: Tara VanDerveer discusses her career as a basketball coach, highlighting the importance of being a teacher and helping players reach their potential. She shares a story about a time when she was coaching the US Women's Olympic team and got a pep talk from President Clinton. Tara emphasizes the importance of being a great teammate, which includes forgiveness, trust, and respect. She also discusses the challenges of NIL and the importance of gender equity in sports.
key_highlights: Coaching is teaching; help people go where they can't go alone
Recruited great players who were motivated themselves
Maximize strengths, minimize weaknesses
Build trust and respect
Be a great teammate
Be direct and honest
Be a leader in everything
episodes.quotes: I think that as a coach, more than anything, number one, I'm a teacher. And I'm really trying to help people go to a place they can't go by themselves.

episodes.title: Hemant Taneja, Managing Partner and CEO of General Catalyst
episodes.guest_title: Managing Partner and CEO, General Catalyst
episodes.summary: Hemant Taneja shares his journey from a low-income household in Delhi to becoming a venture capitalist. He emphasizes the importance of learning and taking risks, inspired by his parents' move to Boston for better education. He discusses the shift from globalization to regionalization and the need for responsible innovation, especially with AI, to ensure it serves society's best interests. Taneja stresses the need for collaboration between technologists and policymakers.
key_highlights: Importance of learning and taking risks
Joy as long as you get good grades
Choice is the beginning of running your own race
Need to minimize unintended consequences
Need to be a drop out to be successful
Global resilience is a big profound theme
Be comfortable with ambiguity
episodes.quotes: “If you don't have open source, you will slow down innovation quite a bit… It's important to be a dropout, right? To have a successful career.”

episodes.title: Sholto Douglas & Trenton Bricken — How LLMs actually think
episodes.summary: The guests discuss the potential for AI to accelerate AI research, emphasizing the importance of interpretability for ensuring safety and progress. They explore the limitations of current evaluation methods, particularly their inability to capture the full complexity of long-horizon tasks. They also touch on the role of synthetic data in training AI and the potential for AI to augment human researchers, rather than replace them.
key_highlights: Context length is currently underhyped; it's a step up in intelligence
The alignment problem is not solved
Long context windows enable models to learn a new language
The attention operation can be viewed as gradient descent
AI can augment human researchers
AI is not smarter than humans
We need more nines of reliability
episodes.quotes: "I think it's really underhyped because until I started working on it, I didn't really appreciate how much of a step up in intelligence it was."

episodes.title: Pakar Energi Stanford: Solusi Iklim Macet Karena Ini - Jimmy Chen | Endgame #181 (Luminaries)
episodes.summary: Jimmy Chen argues that climate change is within our power to control, but the permitting, safety, and siting processes are too slow. He suggests that the solution lies in a combination of technological innovation, policy changes, and a shift in mindset. He emphasizes the importance of global collaboration, particularly with developing countries, and the need for a more realistic approach to achieving carbon neutrality.
key_highlights: Climate change is controllable, but current processes are too slow
Incentives and funding are available, but permitting processes are lagging
Current processes are out of phase with the speed needed
Policies and processes must change to meet the speed needed
There is a need for more realistic assumptions about climate change
We need to take carbon out of the air
There are strengths in diversity
episodes.quotes: It is within our power to control. We're not violating any laws of God, so we can actually solve this issue. But we have to do it relatively quickly.

episodes.title: View From The Top with Daniella Pierson, CEO and Founder of The Newsette
episodes.guest_title: CEO and Founder of The Newsette
episodes.summary: Daniella Pierson shares her journey from a self-described "dumb twin" to a successful entrepreneur, emphasizing the importance of financial independence for women. She discusses how her mental health challenges, including OCD and ADHD, have shaped her approach to business and life. She emphasizes the importance of having a mission greater than oneself to overcome challenges, and how to use your weaknesses as strengths.
key_highlights: Success looks different for everyone
Make your barriers your building blocks
Know what you love and pursue it
Money can buy freedom
The importance of having a mission greater than yourself
Turn your weaknesses into strengths
episodes.quotes: The measure of success is not how many times you win, it's about the times you fail and how you react to those failures.

episodes.title: Mark Zuckerberg — Llama 3, $10B models, Caesar Augustus, & 1 GW datacenters
episodes.summary: Zuckerberg discusses Meta's approach to AI, emphasizing open-source models and the importance of a decentralized AI ecosystem. He expresses concern about a future dominated by a few companies controlling closed AI models, potentially limiting innovation. He also touches on the challenges of balancing AI development with safety and ethical considerations, drawing parallels with historical technological advancements.
key_highlights: Meta's commitment to open-source AI is to prevent a handful of companies from controlling AI development
Apple's control over app features is a concern for Meta's innovation
Building AI requires massive data centers, with Meta aiming for a gigawatt facility
AI's potential for deception is a concern, especially in generating misinformation
The best way to mitigate AI risks is to have good open-source AI
AI will change how people interact with apps, making them more intelligent and personalized
Energy constraints are a key challenge in scaling AI infrastructure
episodes.quotes: "Are we set up for that with AI, where you're going to get a handful of companies that run these closed models that are going to be in control of the APIs and therefore going to be able to tell you what you can build?"

episodes.title: John Mearsheimer: What’s Behind Biden’s Blank Check Support for Israel? | Endgame #179 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Political Science at the University of Chicago
episodes.summary: Professor Mearsheimer argues that the U.S. foreign policy establishment is prioritizing the war in Ukraine and unconditional support for Israel over its own strategic interests, particularly in East Asia. He believes that the U.S. is overextended and that its focus on these conflicts is playing into China's hands. He also argues that the U.S. is unlikely to change its approach due to the power of the Israel lobby.
key_highlights: The U.S. is overextended due to its involvement in Ukraine and the Middle East
A shift to East Asia is necessary to counter China
The U.S. foreign policy establishment is not aligned with public opinion
The Israel lobby is a powerful force in U.S. foreign policy
Israel's actions in Gaza are not in America's strategic interest
The U.S. is unlikely to change its approach to Israel due to the lobby
episodes.quotes: "It's very easy for the foreign policy elites in the United States to say that we should continue the war... It's the Ukrainian people who are paying the blood price."

episodes.title: Indonesia Sedang Kehilangan Mata Anginnya - Slamet Rahardjo | Endgame Lyceum Vol. 5
episodes.summary: Slamet Rahardjo discusses the importance of art and literature in shaping imagination and critical thinking in Indonesia. He emphasizes the need to engage all five senses when experiencing art to fully grasp its meaning. Rahardjo laments the loss of cultural heritage and the challenges faced by artists in Indonesia, highlighting the need for a strong cultural foundation to guide the nation.
key_highlights: Literature awakens us from slumber, preventing imagination from becoming corrupted
A nation's worst fate is losing its moral compass
Capture your moral compass
Don't read only with your eyes, use all five senses
Happiness makes me feel rich
I choose to be rich with friends
episodes.quotes: Seburuk-buruknya nasib manusia ketika dia kehilangan mata anginnya. Tangkap mata anginmu. Jangan membaca hanya dengan mata. Kita harus memusatkan lima indra yang kita miliki.

episodes.title: Di Mana Titik Tengah Kekuasaan dan Kebebasan Individu? - Klaus Schwab | Endgame #178 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Founder and Executive Chairman of the World Economic Forum
episodes.summary: Klaus Schwab discusses the changing global order, emphasizing a shift from unipolarity to multipolarity. He highlights the importance of public-private cooperation and stakeholder capitalism for navigating complexities. Schwab advocates for embracing new technologies like AI while balancing individual and collective interests, particularly in Southeast Asia, to foster sustainable economic growth and resilience.
key_highlights: History is at a turning point, and we will not return to the past
The future lies in being smart, digital, green, and connected
Public-private partnerships are key to success
The future will be driven by rivalry
Southeast Asia is poised for a great economic future
Governments must balance individual and collective interests
episodes.quotes: The future lies in being smart, which means digital, being green, and being connected.

episodes.title: Jensen Huang, Founder and CEO of NVIDIA
episodes.summary: Jensen Huang discusses the early days of NVIDIA, including the motivation to leave LSI Logic and the company's founding mission to solve problems that general-purpose computing couldn't. He emphasizes the importance of building a strong team and culture, and the need to constantly revisit first principles. He shares his leadership philosophy of empowering employees and being transparent, even in challenging times, and the importance of having a good past.
key_highlights: Chris and Curtis inspired Huang to leave a great job and become a founder
NVIDIA's mission: solve problems general-purpose computing can't
The importance of a strong team and culture
The best CEOs have the most direct reports
Always go back to first principles
episodes.quotes: "You can make up a great interview. You could even have a bad interview. But you can't run away from your past. And so have a good past."

episodes.title: Demis Hassabis — Scaling, superhuman AIs, AlphaZero atop LLMs, AlphaFold
episodes.summary: Demis Hassabis discusses the potential for AGI-like systems within the next decade, noting the surprising effectiveness of large models. He emphasizes the importance of multimodal data ingestion, enabling AI systems to understand the physics of the real world better. Hassabis also touches on the need for AI safety and the role of open science, while acknowledging the challenges of achieving true AI alignment and control. He also notes that the most difficult part of science is asking the right question.
key_highlights: AGI-like systems may emerge within the next decade
Large models are unreasonably effective for what they are
Multimodal data ingestion is key to understanding the real world
AI systems are starting to understand the physics of the real world
The world is about to become very exciting in the next few years
There must be high-level algorithmic themes to intelligence
There are underlying principles that underpin all of intelligence
episodes.quotes: "I think the large models today, I think they're almost unreasonably effective for what they are."

episodes.title: Reed Hastings, Chairman and Co-Founder of Netflix
episodes.summary: Reed Hastings discusses his journey from a computer science background to leading Netflix, emphasizing the importance of a strong company culture and a focus on excellence. He shares his experience of overcorrecting in business decisions and the need to balance aggression with empathy. Hastings also touches on the future of entertainment, highlighting the potential of AI and the enduring value of human connection in storytelling.
key_highlights: Culture of freedom and responsibility
The importance of honesty and fairness
The value of employee giving
The power of a great team
The importance of continuous feedback
The need for hope
The power of black excellence
episodes.quotes: "The joke's on us, because people at the GSB really just want to be in a Netflix show."

episodes.title: Patrick Collison — Why Silicon Valley's most talented should leave
episodes.summary: Patrick Collison argues that Silicon Valley overvalues entrepreneurship and undervalues deep technical expertise. He suggests that people in their 20s might be better off avoiding San Francisco to pursue technical depth outside the Valley's hype. Collison also discusses the importance of long-term thinking, the limitations of shareholder capitalism, and the need for more effective organizations in science and technology. He also touches on the potential for AI to be used for both good and bad, and the need for more humility in the face of complex problems.
key_highlights: Silicon Valley overvalues entrepreneurship, while undervaluing deep technical expertise
People in their 20s might be better off avoiding San Francisco
Moats are typically kind of overrated
San Francisco doesn't encourage really deep technical depth
The world needs lots of other things besides entrepreneurship
The importance of multi-decadal abstractions
The world is perfectly capable of originating pandemics without biotech
episodes.quotes: "San Francisco doesn't really encourage the pursuit of really deep technical depth."

episodes.title: Why Climate Politicization Is Killing Us - Martin Blunt | Endgame #177 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Earth Sciences and Engineering at Imperial College London
episodes.summary: Professor Martin Blunt argues that climate change has become overly politicized, hindering pragmatic engineering solutions. He suggests focusing on easily achievable goals like reducing flaring and capturing CO2 from high-concentration sources. Blunt emphasizes the need for intellectual freedom and a consensus-driven approach, rather than political dogma, to address climate challenges effectively.
key_highlights: CO2 emissions are not decreasing
Developed countries have an imperative to act
Science is not political
Inconvenient truths are still happening
Easy wins are not being done
What is scientific is being politicized
episodes.quotes: What is fundamentally a scientific problem has become politicized, so there are some easy wins that we are not doing. But science is not political!

episodes.title: Vijay Prashad: America’s Finger-Wagging Puts Global South in Danger | Endgame #176 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Executive Director of the Tricontinental Institute for Social Research
episodes.summary: Vijay Prashad argues that the US’s finger-wagging and lecturing of other nations stems from a colonial mentality. He contends that the US’s focus on military might and its attempts to weaken Russia and China are detrimental to global stability. Prashad also highlights the importance of understanding the historical context and complexities of different regions, emphasizing the need for empathy and a rejection of ethnocentric views.
key_highlights: Democracy is in the head, not in the machine
Colonial mentality drives US lecturing
War zones are banal, filled with noise and dust
US foreign policy hurts Europe
US is not a city on a hill
US is just another country on the planet
The past grips you in Asia
episodes.quotes: “Democracy doesn’t mature when you get the most expensive voting system. Democracy is in the head, it’s not in the machine.”

episodes.title: Jeffrey Sachs: There Is No Shortcut to Peace | Endgame #175 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: University Professor and Director of the Center for Sustainable Development at Columbia University
episodes.summary: Jeffrey Sachs argues that the Ukraine War was predictable and that the US is pushing for a situation that makes peace impossible. He also contends that the US is not acting rationally in its foreign policy, particularly in the Middle East, and that the world is moving towards a multipolar order. Sachs emphasizes the importance of education as the key to economic development.
key_highlights: Ukraine will not join NATO
US leaders need to stop pushing this
There can't be peace otherwise
The current situation is no good for anybody
This was all predictable
The world looks to the US to be normal
Stop your wars, don't overthrow us
episodes.quotes: "This is stupid. Can you understand anything you people in Washington? Don't do this, you can't win."

episodes.title: How Not to Win the War, but the Peace - Stephen Kotkin | Endgame #174 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Kleinheinz Senior Fellow at the Hoover Institution
episodes.summary: Stephen Kotkin argues that the US and China must share the planet, but the terms of that sharing are what matter. He believes that history never tells you what the future will be, but it does tell you that the present is not going to last. He argues that it is possible to lose a war and win the peace, and that the West must be ready to be lucky.
key_highlights: A bilateral US-China agreement won't work to our advantage
We must share the planet with China, but on what terms?
History never tells you what the future will be
You can lose a war and win the peace
You have to be ready to be lucky
The present is not going to last
episodes.quotes: History never tells you what the future is going to be, but what history can tell you is that the present is not going to last. You have to be ready to be lucky.

episodes.title: Bernegara dan Berbisnis di Tengah Machiavellianisme - Arsjad Rasjid | Endgame #173
episodes.guest_title: Chairman of KADIN (Indonesian Chamber of Commerce and Industry)
episodes.summary: Arsjad Rasjid discusses the importance of a clear roadmap for Indonesia's economic future, emphasizing the need for a strong legal system to attract investment. He contrasts Indonesia's approach with Singapore's, highlighting the latter's attractiveness to investors due to its reliable legal framework. He also stresses the importance of having a positive mindset and respecting diverse perspectives.
key_highlights: Different perspectives are a strength, and we must respect them
Focus on the economy to achieve prosperity and justice
Rule of law is the formula for Indonesia to advance
Leaders must provide hope to subordinates and to themselves
Ambitious goals are important, but we need a dream
Justice and prosperity can only occur if there is rule of law
episodes.quotes: "Differentiation of perspective is a strength. Everyone has a different perspective, and we have to respect that."

episodes.title: Jack Dorsey's Answers to Bitcoin Naysayers | Endgame #172 (Luminaries)
episodes.summary: Jack Dorsey discusses Bitcoin's role in the future, emphasizing its potential to replace central banks and empower individuals. He acknowledges the need for improved accessibility and design in Bitcoin's ecosystem, particularly in developing countries. Dorsey also touches on the importance of energy efficiency in Bitcoin mining and the value of meditation in his personal life.
key_highlights: Bitcoin has the potential to replace central banks and empower individuals
Bitcoin's accessibility and design need improvement, especially in developing countries
Energy efficiency is critical for Bitcoin mining's sustainability
The future's already here, it's just not evenly distributed
The best way to advance is by creating more opportunities for energy
episodes.quotes: The future's already here, it's just not evenly distributed. I think it's a beautiful way to capture what we're all trying to build.

episodes.title: Ubah Pola Pikir, Dobrak Kebiasaan Lama - Irwan Tisnabudi | Endgame #171
episodes.guest_title: Head of Digital Banking Bank BTPN Tbk
episodes.summary: Irwan Tisnabudi discusses his career path, the importance of having a good team, and the need to listen to customers. He emphasizes the importance of collaboration and challenging the status quo, and the need to nurture a culture of openness and innovation. He also touches on the importance of financial inclusion and the need to educate customers on financial management.
key_highlights: When you love your job, you wake up excited to go to the office
People come to work to make a difference
Silence and listening are good for learning
Loans are often seen as a burden
Digitalization is a vehicle for financial inclusion
It's important to have a great team
Fun is as important as the results
episodes.quotes: "When you wake up in the morning and you are happy and feel excited to go to the office, that's where you love your job."

episodes.title: Pemimpin Grasak-grusuk dan Jebakan Ideologi - Zulkieflimansyah | Endgame #170
episodes.guest_title: Former Governor of West Nusa Tenggara
episodes.summary: Zulkieflimansyah discusses the importance of education and technology in Indonesia's development, emphasizing the need for a long-term vision and strategic planning. He highlights the need for local leaders to understand the big picture and avoid short-sighted decisions driven by popularity. Zulkieflimansyah also stresses the importance of creating a supportive ecosystem for innovation and entrepreneurship, particularly in less developed regions of Indonesia.
key_highlights: Indonesia needs leaders who understand the big picture, not just short-term gains
Education is the most important legacy we can leave for future generations
Indonesia needs a multi-faceted approach, not a single style, to development
Technology and innovation are key to Indonesia's future competitiveness
Prioritizing local wisdom and culture is essential for sustainable development
Leaders should focus on capacity building, not just power
episodes.quotes: Indonesia ini tidak bisa didekati dengan satu gaya dan satu cara. Perlu kepala daerah yang mengerti gambaran besar itu sehingga dia tidak grasa-grusuk for the sake of popularity, electability, kemudian kehilangan akal sehat demi kekuasaan.

episodes.title: ASEAN’s Race to Overtake China - Peng Ong | Endgame #169
episodes.guest_title: Managing Partner of Monk’s Hill Ventures
episodes.summary: Peng Ong discusses his upbringing in Singapore and how it shaped his values and career. He contrasts the entrepreneurial ecosystems of China and Southeast Asia, emphasizing the need for Southeast Asia to focus on education and innovation to compete globally. He also touches on the importance of integrity and the challenges of scaling businesses in the region.
key_highlights: A more efficient world leaves fewer places to hide for individuals and businesses
Building systems is crucial to mitigate human error in decision-making
AI creating better AI than humans is a key sign
Hard work, honesty, and Christian values shaped his fundamental operating system
Singapore's success is due to good governance and a bit of luck
Southeast Asia needs to improve education to compete with China
The best leaders are both philosophical and warriors
episodes.quotes: The older I get, the less I trust myself in positive decisions about people, so we start building systems around that.

episodes.title: Talenta & Teknologi di Era Akselerasi - Bayu Hanantasena (Lintasarta) | Endgame #168
episodes.summary: Bayu Hanantasena discusses the importance of imagination and risk assessment in leadership, emphasizing the need for government policy to understand and manage risks without stifling innovation. He highlights the importance of asking the right questions to cultivate solutions, rather than just engaging in discussions. He also shares his background, growing up in an academic environment, and the values instilled by his parents, both educators.
key_highlights: Imagination is crucial for anticipating potential risks
Government policy must balance innovation and risk management
Asking the right questions leads to solutions
Government policy should understand and manage risks without stifling innovation
Government policy should understand and manage risks without stifling innovation
episodes.quotes: Sebenarnya imajinasi kita itu sudah nyampe kepada resiko-resiko yang mungkin ada. Memang akhirnya kuncinya di government yang memegang policy itu harus betul-betul paham, yang kedua tahu bagaimana mengelola ini tanpa harus kehilangan sudut yang tepat untuk memanfaatkan.

episodes.title: Why Debating Matters? - Bo Seo | Endgame #167
episodes.guest_title: Author of Good Arguments: How Debate Teaches Us to Listen and Be Heard
episodes.summary: Bo Seo, a two-time world debate champion, discusses the value of debate as a means to bridge differences and promote critical thinking. He emphasizes the importance of listening before speaking and the need to maintain a human element in discussions. Seo also touches on the challenges of social media and the importance of preserving democratic values.
key_highlights: Debate combines logic, emotion, deliberation, and performance
Listen before you speak
Migration shaped his perspective
Debate is not just about winning
Need to be able to see the whole thing
Debate is about finding a new language
Find a purpose greater than self-promotion
episodes.quotes: I couldn't be a debater without that culture that says you try and hear what the other person is saying. You learn to listen before you speak.

episodes.title: A Techno-optimist Look at AI - Jeff Dean | Endgame #166 (Luminaries)
episodes.summary: Jeff Dean discusses the use of AI in various fields, including healthcare, education, and sustainability. He emphasizes the importance of AI principles to avoid bias and harm. Dean expresses optimism about AI's potential to improve communication, scientific discovery, and weather prediction, while acknowledging the need for multi-disciplinary collaboration.
key_highlights: AI is accelerating scientific discovery, but multi-disciplinary collaboration is needed
AI can improve communication and language learning
AI can be used to reduce emissions
AI can help predict food insecurity
AI can improve healthcare diagnostics
episodes.quotes: Often these features are feel pretty magical, but they're actually, you know, often powered by these machine learning models. It is a narrow mind which cannot look at a subject from various points of view.

episodes.title: 20 Tahun Berkarir di Jepang: Dobrak Pintu Riset Bangsa - Sastia Prama Putri | Endgame #165
episodes.guest_title: Associate Professor at Osaka University in the field of Biotechnology
episodes.summary: Sastia Prama Putri discusses her journey from Jakarta to becoming a biotechnology professor in Japan, emphasizing the importance of parental influence and the need for Indonesia to invest in its scientists. She highlights the challenges faced by women in STEM fields, particularly in balancing career and family. Sastia advocates for Indonesia to focus on quality over quantity in scientific research, leveraging its biodiversity to compete globally.
key_highlights: Father's early recognition of her talents shaped her path in science
Balancing career and family requires performing as if without family
Social expectations demand mothers manage childcare as if unemployed
Women in STEM must outperform men to be considered equal
Indonesia needs more experts in specific areas of biodiversity
Indonesia's R&D needs to connect to industry for practical application
episodes.quotes: We have to perform as if we don't have any family at home, but di sisi lain secara sosial, kita dituntut untuk mengurus anak kita seakan-akan kita enggak punya pekerjaan.

episodes.title: Lima Mitos Kuliah di Luar Negeri - Haryadi Gunawi | Endgame #164
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Computer Science at the University of Chicago
episodes.summary: Haryadi Gunawi, a professor of Computer Science at the University of Chicago, discusses his life journey, from his early years in Jakarta to his current academic position. He shares insights on the importance of mentorship, the challenges faced by Indonesian students pursuing higher education abroad, and the myths surrounding PhD programs. He emphasizes the value of pursuing one's passion and contributing to the development of human resources in Indonesia.
key_highlights: Early life in Jakarta and education at BPK Penabur
Transition from a top student to a more relaxed approach in high school
The importance of mentorship and guidance in academic pursuits
Challenges faced by Indonesian students pursuing higher education in the US
The myth of PhD programs being boring and only leading to academia
The importance of having a vision and passion for one's field
The need to address misconceptions about pursuing higher education abroad
episodes.quotes: Sebenarnya kan PhD itu combo S2 S3 di Amerika gratis gitu. Dan ketika kalau kita pulang ke Indonesia tanya siapa yang muka Amerika gratis? Pasti semua angkat banyak yang ngangkat tangan lah.

episodes.title: Reinventing Sustainable Modernization - Ian Scoones | Endgame #163
episodes.guest_title: Professor at the University of Sussex
episodes.summary: Ian Scoones discusses the need to move beyond a singular focus on growth and technology-led growth, emphasizing the importance of considering the broader context of sustainability and social equity. He argues that a more democratic and inclusive approach is needed to address global problems, and that solutions should emerge from discussions in local communities. He also notes that the environmental movement needs to be integrated into the center of society.
key_highlights: A singular focus on growth and technology-led growth is not enough
Sustainability requires a democratic and inclusive approach
Solutions should emerge from discussions in local communities
The environmental movement needs to be integrated into the center of society
We need to reinvent what a modern way of life is
episodes.quotes: A singular focus just on growth or technology-led growth is not enough these days, if it ever was. It was a modernization zeal that created very often the problems that we're facing now.

episodes.title: Climate Change: A Revised Prediction - Steven Chu | Endgame #162 (Luminaries)
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Physics and Molecular & Cellular Physiology at Stanford University
episodes.summary: In this episode of Endgame, Nobel laureate Steven Chu discusses the profound challenges of climate change, emphasizing the need to decarbonize industries beyond electricity, including agriculture, steel, chemicals, plastics, and concrete. He stresses the importance of rediscovery and independent thinking in scientific innovation, drawing from his own experiences and the biographies of great scientists. Chu advocates for a realistic approach to sustainability, acknowledging the economic and political complexities, especially in developing countries, and the need for policy and public support to drive change.
key_highlights: Climate change requires decarbonizing agriculture, steel, chemicals, plastics, and concrete, not just electricity
Rediscovery and independent thinking are crucial for scientific innovation
The full dangers of climate change are obvious
The problem is profoundly big
We need to understand more so that we may fear less
Exploratory curiosity leads to new discoveries
Learning from textbooks limits imagination
episodes.quotes: The problem is really big. It is profoundly big. This is not about fossil fuel for electricity. This is about greenhouse gas emissions in agriculture. You got to decarbonize steel, chemicals, plastics, concrete, everything.

episodes.title: Dino Patti Djalal: Harga Mahal Perdamaian | Endgame #161
episodes.guest_title: Former Indonesian Ambassador to the United States
episodes.summary: Dino Patti Djalal discusses the need for Indonesia to develop its own concept for a new world order, noting that the old US-led liberal international order is no longer viable. He argues that Indonesia should focus on regional leadership in Southeast Asia, particularly in the face of increasing geopolitical competition. He also emphasizes the importance of cultivating a new generation of diplomats with a strong sense of idealism and internationalism.
key_highlights: Indonesia needs its own concept for a new world order
The old US-led order is no longer viable
Indonesia should focus on regional leadership in Southeast Asia
Need for diplomats with idealism and internationalism
Indonesia is uniquely positioned to lead the Global South
episodes.quotes: Sekarang enggak ada tuh, yang punya konsep bagaimana merubah tata dunia yang baru, baik secara politik maupun secara ekonomi. Jadi kita stuck nih kayak gini, yang lama udah enggak laku lagi, apalagi dulu kan US-led, sekarang US sudah enggak leading lagi.

episodes.title: Butuh Berapa Tahun untuk Bangun Budaya Literasi Bangsa? - Judo Suwidji | Endgame #160
episodes.summary: Judo Suwidji, co-founder of Periplus Indonesia, discusses the importance of cultivating a reading culture in Indonesia to improve the quality of writing and storytelling. He laments the lack of Indonesian authors in international publications and emphasizes the need for a holistic approach to education that encourages critical thinking. Suwidji also shares his personal journey of developing a love for books and his dream of establishing more book clubs in Indonesia.

Reading books trains us to be able to narrate well
Reading books requires a reference to become a quality
Reading books can be developed through book clubs
The importance of a holistic approach to education
The need for Indonesian authors in international publications

episodes.title: Bom Waktu Generasi Muda: Gratifikasi Instan - Claudia Kolonas | Endgame #159
episodes.summary: In this episode, Gita Wirjawan interviews Claudia Kolonas, founder of Pluang, about her childhood, her journey into finance, and her views on the Indonesian economy. Kolonas argues that a lack of financial education and role models in Indonesia has led to a focus on instant gratification, which is detrimental to long-term wealth creation. She believes that blockchain technology and financial influencers can help to democratize access to capital and promote financial literacy.

She believes trust is the biggest challenge for Indonesian fintech companies
Kolonas is concerned about the lack of role models in Indonesian finance
She believes blockchain technology can democratize access to capital
Kolonas sees a need for financial education in Bahasa Indonesia
The guest believes that the new RUU P2SK will improve financial inclusion
She believes the key is to start investing, even if it's small

episodes.title: Ray Dalio - Economic Empires: Past, Present & Future | Endgame #158 (Luminaries) | UWRF2023
episodes.guest_title: Founder, CIO Mentor, and Member of the Board at Bridgewater Associates
episodes.summary: Ray Dalio discusses the cyclical nature of economic empires, emphasizing the importance of internal strength and adaptability. He cautions against overindulgence and highlights the need for balanced growth, emphasizing that the United States is at a critical juncture. He also notes that neutrality can be a beneficial strategy for countries navigating geopolitical conflicts, and that education is key to long-term success.
key_highlights: It's in your interest to prioritize a more austere, more secure capability
Know what phase of your life you're in
The aim is to reconcile idealism versus the realism of the future
You have to be self-sufficient plus
The greatest risk to a democracy is anarchy due to internal fighting
episodes.quotes: It's in your interest to prioritize to have a more austere, more secure capability. That will give you power and independence.

episodes.title: Ubah Aset Jadi Pembuka Jalan Dekarbonisasi - Anindya Bakrie | Endgame #157
episodes.summary: Anindya Bakrie discusses the importance of Indonesia's biodiversity as a printing machine for wealth, the need to industrialize in a way that is both sustainable and beneficial for the Indonesian people, and the importance of education in shaping the future of the country. He emphasizes the need for Indonesia to be a leader in the green economy and to develop its own technology and expertise in order to compete in the global market. Bakrie also touches on the importance of collaboration and diplomacy in achieving these goals.
