key_highlights: Biotechnology became a national priority due to Habibie's vision and top-down approach
Basic research is crucial but currently weak in Indonesia
Eijkman Institute's building was built by the Dutch government for research
Science has no borders, collaboration is essential
Sustainable funding models beyond government support are needed for research institutions
episodes.quotes: Science has no borders.

episodes.title: Rocky Gerung: Darurat Pengelolaan Ide dan Etika | Endgame #61
episodes.summary: Rocky Gerung argues that humanity is the ability to process fiction, but warns that this ability can be dangerous if it leads to the search for solutions to imagined problems. He and Gita Wirjawan discuss the post-COVID era, the importance of intellectualism over electability, the uneven growth of the economy, and the need to reinvent humanity and solidarity. They also touch on the dangers of technology and the importance of ethical assumptions.
key_highlights: Humanity is the ability to process fiction
Solutions are found in the ability to think abstractly
Intellectuality should precede electability
Post-COVID era requires a reinvention of humanity
Uneven economic growth will lead to inequality
Technology is being used to reinvent our humanity
We must be wary of the dangers of technology
episodes.quotes: Kemanusiaan itu sebetulnya adalah kemampuan untuk mengolah fiksi. Nah bahayanya kalau kita bilang fiksi itu, kita membayangkan orang akan marah toh. Solusinya apa? Solusi kita perlu solusi yang hari ini. Oh enggak, solusinya itu ada di dalam kemampuan Anda untuk berpikir abstrak.

episodes.title: Genjot UMKM Tembus Pasar Global - Christin Djuarto | Endgame #60
episodes.summary: Christin Djuarto, Executive Director of Shopee Indonesia, discusses Shopee's strategy for empowering Indonesian MSMEs to participate in the global digital economy. She highlights the importance of digital literacy and financial inclusion, and the need to address the challenges of fraud and security in the digital space. Djuarto emphasizes the importance of creating a level playing field for women in the workforce and the need to break down self-imposed barriers.
key_highlights: Pressure to perform in Singapore can be high, leading to early returns
Shopee is expanding educational centers for MSMEs across Indonesia
Women often create their own glass ceilings
Shopee focuses on serving the underserved through technology
Shopee prioritizes user experience and safety on its platform
Digital literacy and hygiene are crucial for navigating the digital economy
episodes.quotes: Menurut saya yang paling penting itu bukan masalah yang orang lain itu seperti apa gitu, bukan lingkungannya seperti apa. Tapi kebanyakan perempuan saya lihat di dunia, di Indonesia juga, itu membuat glass ceiling sendiri.

episodes.title: Crypto Kian Booming, Pahami Isu Fundamental Ini - Jeth Soetoyo (CEO Pintu) | Endgame #59
episodes.summary: Jeth Soetoyo, CEO of Pintu, discusses the evolution of money and the potential of cryptocurrency, emphasizing the importance of understanding the technology and its risks. He highlights the need for responsible investment and education to foster a vibrant entrepreneurial society in Indonesia. Gita Wirjawan emphasizes the need for a balance between innovation and regulation, and the importance of maintaining a global standard for crypto products.
key_highlights: Money is a human creation, its value dependent on collective belief
Crypto's growth is linked to macroeconomics and innovation cycles
Need to educate society on crypto's potential and risks
Indonesia needs more domestic capital to support crypto ventures
The goal is financial inclusion and access to capital
Regulation must balance innovation with consumer protection
episodes.quotes: "Uang itu merupakan sebuah karya dari manusia, tergantung siapa yang mempercayainya."

episodes.title: Sri Mulyani: Bijak & Inovatif Kelola Fiskal | Endgame #58
episodes.guest_title: Minister of Finance, Republic of Indonesia
episodes.summary: Sri Mulyani Indrawati discusses her background and career, highlighting her role in Indonesia's economic policy-making. She emphasizes the challenges of balancing policy decisions between being too late or overdoing it, especially in times of crisis. She also discusses the need for a balanced approach between fossil fuels and renewable energy and the importance of international collaboration to address climate change.
key_highlights: Policy makers must balance acting too late and overdoing it
Finding the right timing and dosage is the most challenging
Indonesia's economic policy is shaped by crisis situations
The need for a just transition to renewable energy
Indonesia is an attractive investment destination due to its market size
International collaboration is key to addressing climate change
episodes.quotes: "That's the job of a policy maker. Finding the right timing and the right dosage is the most challenging."

episodes.title: Sabda PS: Tanpa Standar Intelektual, Peradaban Bisa Celaka | Endgame #57
episodes.summary: Sabda PS discusses the importance of intellectual standards and wisdom in education. He argues that without a high intellectual threshold, societies risk making poor decisions and becoming polarized. He emphasizes the need for a balanced approach that combines fundamental skills with specialized knowledge, and stresses the importance of critical thinking and ethical considerations in a rapidly changing world.
key_highlights: There is a need for a high intellectual threshold in society
Wisdom is key to mitigating long-term risks
There is a need to democratize ideas, not just information
A good education should have individual and societal targets
The best education combines fundamental skills with specialized knowledge
episodes.quotes: I think it's important to have a healthy skepticism, a healthy keraguan, it's important for us to jangan sampai seyakin banget dengan itu, let's just visit first.

episodes.title: Keadilan (Token) Sosial Bagi Seluruh Talenta - Sabrang Mowo Damar Panuluh | Endgame #56
episodes.summary: Sabrang argues that the democratization of information has not led to a democratization of ideas. He suggests that the current system amplifies existing inequalities and that a focus on the “expert view” is needed to counter this. He proposes that blockchain technology could be used to tokenize human attributes like initiative and good deeds, creating a new system of social credit and value.
key_highlights: All living things have a common goal: social status
Gotong royong is the basis of social status in villages
Modern tech status is based on wealth, not problem-solving
Society has become an object of one's desire
Social status is a side effect of problem-solving
Blockchain could tokenize human attributes, creating a new social credit system
episodes.quotes: “All living things have a common goal: social status. In the village, social status is obtained from gotong royong. You participate in doing something, solving a problem, then you rise in social hierarchy.”

episodes.title: Sharlini Eriza Putri: Ada Semesta di Diri Kita | Endgame #55
episodes.summary: Sharlini Eriza Putri and Gita Wirjawan discuss the impact of COVID-19 on education, the need to move beyond colonial curricula, and the importance of holistic education that recognizes the interconnectedness of individuals and their environments. They explore the challenges of navigating a world dominated by algorithms and the need for a more nuanced understanding of well-being beyond economic metrics. They also discuss the importance of local wisdom and the need to empower individuals to make informed decisions.
key_highlights: COVID-19 has left many dilemmas, including the choice between education and health
Rendering ideas needs multi-input, and every child has the right to know themselves
The importance of recognizing that humans are holobionts
The need to move beyond a single-species perspective
The importance of a curriculum that is not colonial
The need to empower individuals to make informed decisions
episodes.quotes: "Proses rendering ngeluarin ide itu butuh multi input dan setiap anak itu berhak untuk dia benar-benar mengenal siapa dirinya di society."

episodes.title: Gandeng Tangan Pahlawan Pangan - Natalia Rialucky (TaniHub Group) | Endgame #54
episodes.summary: Natalia Rialucky discusses the challenges and opportunities in Indonesia's agricultural sector, emphasizing the need for innovation and a holistic approach to empower farmers. She highlights the importance of financial inclusion, technology adoption, and sustainable practices. She also touches on the role of younger generations in driving change and the need for a more democratized and less centralized system.
key_highlights: Price increases six-fold from farm to supermarket, TaniHub aims to solve
Batak and Javanese heritage shaped competitive, tight-knit family
TaniHub focuses on access to supply chain, financing, and markets
Technology adoption requires a relationship-based approach with farmers
The goal is to empower farmers and create a sustainable ecosystem
Need to democratize information and ideas, not just access to technology
episodes.quotes: “We were born to be creators of the possible future.”

episodes.title: Habib Husein Ja'far: Lebih Percaya Teknologi atau Spiritualitas? | Endgame #53
episodes.summary: Habib Husein Ja'far and Gita Wirjawan discuss the role of religion in the digital age, the importance of critical thinking, and the need for a balance between technological advancement and spiritual values. They explore the challenges of misinformation, the erosion of expertise, and the need for a "jihad algoritmatik" to reclaim control over digital narratives. They emphasize the importance of ethical leadership and the need to promote a more humanistic approach to technology.
key_highlights: Need for a "jihad algoritmatik" to combat misinformation and reclaim digital narratives
Modernism alienates humans from themselves through digitization
The importance of being a good listener in a world of constant noise
The need for critical thinking to navigate the complexities of the digital age
Ethical leadership is essential to guide technological development
Religion can play a positive role in promoting ethical values and social responsibility
Balance between technological advancement and spiritual values is crucial
episodes.quotes: Kita itu terlalu kemudian sekarang dibuat tunduk kepada algoritma yang sudah ada.

episodes.title: Tensi AS-China Naik, Sentralitas ASEAN Diuji | Endgame Compendium #3
episodes.summary: Marty Natalegawa argues that the competition between great powers is a constant in Southeast Asia, and ASEAN's response has evolved from neutrality to a more proactive role. He emphasizes that ASEAN's centrality must be earned, not merely asserted, and that the region must develop a more substantive influence beyond procedural ceremonies. He warns of a potential deficit in ASEAN's influence if it fails to articulate and pursue its own strategic vision amidst the US-China rivalry.
key_highlights: Competition among great powers is a constant in Southeast Asia
ASEAN's response evolved from neutrality to a more proactive role
ASEAN's centrality must be earned, not merely asserted
ASEAN must develop a more substantive influence beyond procedural ceremonies
ASEAN must have a script to guide its actions
Indonesia's leadership is key to ASEAN's centrality
There is a risk of ASEAN losing its centrality
episodes.quotes: ASEAN must have a script because we cannot just want to be in the driving seat position without knowing ingin ke mana dikemudikan kawasan ini.

episodes.title: Siapa Broker Perdamaian Timur Tengah? -  Nabil Fahmy | Endgame #52
episodes.guest_title: Dean, School of Global Affairs and Public Policy, American University in Cairo
episodes.summary: Nabil Fahmy, former Foreign Minister of Egypt, discusses the need for greater global dialogue on developing the future, emphasizing the inclusion of smaller nations. He reflects on his own career path, from studying physics to becoming a diplomat, and highlights the importance of understanding different perspectives. He shares his views on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, emphasizing the need for a two-state solution and the importance of addressing the security concerns of all parties involved.
key_highlights: The world needs to discuss future development, not just big powers
Egyptian and Indonesian leaders need to talk more
It is important to understand your interlocutor
The Middle East is changing
There is a need for a two-state solution
It is important to preserve your integrity and credibility
episodes.quotes: The world has to talk much more about how we develop the future. It should not be only the big powers that decide where we're going.

episodes.title: Future of Indonesia's Largest Tobacco Company - Jacek Olczak | Endgame #48
episodes.summary: In this episode of Endgame, Jacek Olczak, CEO of Philip Morris International, discusses PMI's transition to a smoke-free future. Olczak reflects on his personal journey, from growing up in communist Poland to leading a multinational corporation. He emphasizes the importance of science and technology in addressing smoking-related harm, and the need for a balanced approach that considers both public health and consumer choice. He also highlights the importance of engaging with governments and other stakeholders to create a regulatory environment that supports innovation and harm reduction.
key_highlights: PMI aims to transition to smoke-free products by investing heavily in R&D
Government support is crucial for harm reduction in smoking
The company is trying to balance shareholder value with public health
Indonesia is a key market for PMI's smoke-free products
Technology and innovation are key to creating better alternatives to smoking
episodes.quotes: I was lucky to be born of finishing university at the right time. So you have to imagine when I entered university in Poland, we didn't have passports, right? So we're not free to travel.

episodes.title: Vaksin Penangkal Varian Baru Sudah Mulai Dibuat? - Indra Rudiansyah | Endgame #47
episodes.summary: Indra Rudiansyah discusses his background, growing up in a simple family in Bandung, Indonesia, and his journey to the University of Oxford. He explains the importance of bridging connections between institutions in Indonesia and abroad, and how Indonesian diaspora can serve as a bridge. He also discusses the importance of vaccination and the need for a strong surveillance system to monitor virus mutations.
key_highlights: Indonesia’s strong internal capabilities should be leveraged to bridge institutions abroad
Indonesian diaspora can serve as “insiders” to facilitate international collaboration
The Oxford-AstraZeneca vaccine uses a chimpanzee adenovirus platform
The speaker was inspired to pursue science by his older sister
Vaccine efficacy is influenced by demographics, geography, and ethnicity
The speaker emphasizes the importance of vaccination to prevent virus mutations
episodes.quotes: Kenapa kita enggak manfaatkan titel tersebut untuk kita menjadi perwakilan diaspora di luar negeri, ketika institusi ini ingin kerja sama, kita menjadi orang dalam bagi institusi di luar negeri itu untuk kerja sama dengan Indonesia.

episodes.title: Rumus Emas Berinvestasi - Ryan Filbert | Endgame #46
episodes.summary: Ryan Filbert discusses his personal journey into investment, emphasizing the importance of financial literacy and how it is often lacking in Indonesia. He argues that the language used in financial discussions can be alienating and advocates for making financial education more accessible and culturally relevant. He also touches on the need for a more rational approach to investing, especially in the context of Indonesia's unique economic landscape.
key_highlights: Financial literacy should be accessible and culturally relevant
Financial language can alienate those who don't understand it
Investment is like planting a tree, not trading cattle
Indonesia's demographic bonus is an advantage
The goal is to empower the next generation
Regulations need to keep pace with innovation
The importance of understanding the fundamentals of investment
episodes.quotes: Orang yang paham, berbicara mengenai keuangan dan investasi, berbicara dengan bahasa mereka. Di mana orang yang tidak paham ketika mendengar bahasa mereka, tiba-tiba merasa, aduh saya bukan bagiannya deh.

episodes.title: Bersatu Lawan Pandemi | Endgame Compendium #2
episodes.summary: This episode of Endgame features multiple guests discussing the COVID-19 pandemic in Indonesia. They explore the pandemic's impact on various aspects of Indonesian society, including the economy, healthcare, and education. They also discuss the challenges of vaccine distribution, the importance of public health measures, and the need for a more holistic approach to pandemic management.
key_highlights: COVID-19 reset society, revealing vulnerabilities and prompting critical questions
Vaccine rollout faces challenges, potentially delaying herd immunity
Supply constraints of vaccines are a major obstacle
Genetic sequencing of the virus is crucial for vaccine development
Testing rates need to increase to effectively manage the pandemic
Biodiverse diets and environments can boost immunity
Collaboration and data-driven decisions are essential for pandemic response
episodes.quotes: Kita jadi tahu kalau ternyata oh ada makhluk-makhluk kecil yang se-powerful itu di luar. Kita jadi lebih ngerti kenapa namanya alam itu harus dijaga.

episodes.title: Inklusi Keuangan Bukan Sekadar E-Wallet - Tessa Wijaya | Endgame #45
episodes.summary: Tessa Wijaya discusses her journey from Sukabumi to Silicon Valley, emphasizing the importance of real-world education for startups. She highlights the value of learning about fundraising, bookkeeping, and profitability in the investment world. She also discusses the importance of financial inclusion and how technology can help to equalize access to financial services, particularly in Indonesia.
key_highlights: Education is essential, especially real-world knowledge for startups, including fundraising and profitability
Investment world provides valuable insights into diverse industries and entrepreneurial experiences
Indonesia's digital payments landscape faces challenges due to fragmented infrastructure
Fintech can promote financial inclusion by empowering underserved communities with easy-to-use tools
Indonesia's potential is huge, but it needs to be tapped into
The right kind of education is key to unlocking potential
The importance of being inquisitive and challenging assumptions
episodes.quotes: "Pendidikan tuh penting banget di sini bukan pendidikan cuman yang basic aja, tetapi yang lebih real world for startups. How to raise funds, how do you run your books, you know, as in like, how do I get to profitability."

episodes.title: Mobil Listrik, Mobil Nyetir Sendiri, Lalu Apa? - Moorissa Tjokro | Endgame #44
episodes.summary: Moorissa Tjokro discusses her early life in Indonesia, her education in the US, and her work at Tesla. She and Gita Wirjawan discuss the disruptive potential of autonomous vehicles, and the need to educate and reskill workers who may be displaced by automation. Tjokro emphasizes the importance of sustainability and social impact in her career choices.
key_highlights: Tesla is leading the world to transition into renewable energy
Autonomous cars can improve safety
Education is key to empowering the nation
The importance of having a purpose
The need to minimize the use of materials
The importance of reskilling and upskilling
episodes.quotes: “I don’t want to just live in the house and tell people what to do. I actually want to build the house.”

episodes.title: Breaking Down History, Building Future Bridges - Ronit Ricci | Endgame #43
episodes.guest_title: Chair, Department of Asian Studies, Hebrew University
episodes.summary: Ronit Ricci discusses her journey into Indonesian studies, emphasizing the importance of understanding Indonesia's rich history and culture. She highlights the unique position of Indonesia as a country with a large Muslim population but distinct cultural influences. She stresses the need for independent thinking and critical engagement with history to avoid stereotypes and foster a deeper understanding of Indonesia.
key_highlights: Indonesia is a fascinating and important country with a rich history and culture
There is a great deal to learn from Indonesia
Indonesia's history and literature offer valuable insights
Introducing Indonesia to Israeli students is a special and important opportunity
There is a need to avoid stereotypes about Indonesia
Indonesia has a unique cultural identity
There is a need for more research on Indonesia
episodes.quotes: I find Indonesia a very fascinating and important country and I think there is a great deal to learn from its history and from its literatures and from its other arts.

episodes.title: Habib Husein Ja'far: Saleh Akal dan Sosial, Bukan Hanya Ritual | Endgame #42
episodes.summary: Habib Husein Ja'far discusses his upbringing in a heterogenous East Java environment, his education in philosophy and Islamic studies, and his work as a millennial Da'i. He emphasizes the importance of internal spiritual development, rational interpretation of religious texts, and the need for Muslims to engage with science and technology. The conversation touches on the challenges of maintaining religious values in the digital age and the need for a more inclusive and tolerant approach to Islam.
key_highlights: Islam should be oriented towards internal spiritual growth, not outward imposition
Rationality and faith are not mutually exclusive
Indonesia has the potential to lead a new Islamic renaissance
Technology should serve humanity, not control it
The need for a more inclusive and tolerant approach to Islam
episodes.quotes: "Berislam utamanya yang penuh cinta, yang berorientasi gerak ke dalam, bukan keluar. Jadi, memperbaiki diri sebelum kemudian mengurusi orang lain."

episodes.title: Angga Sasongko: Membangun Bisnis Kreatif Berdampak & Berskala | Endgame #41
episodes.summary: Angga Sasongko discusses his journey from a rebellious youth to a successful filmmaker, emphasizing the importance of staying true to one's values and creating a sustainable business model. He highlights Visinema's unique approach to filmmaking, which prioritizes public interest, talent development, and a decentralized organizational structure. Sasongko also touches on the challenges and adaptations Visinema has faced, including the shift to animation and the need for a more robust business model beyond ticket sales.
key_highlights: Don't marry your ideas; be open to criticism and new perspectives
Early life rebellion shaped his ability to challenge norms
Mentorship from a teacher altered his life trajectory
Visinema values self-expression and provides a platform for talent development
Importance of courage and commitment in creative endeavors
Focus on local content to resonate with the Indonesian audience
Prioritizing public interest over profit is key to Visinema's mission
episodes.quotes: "Jangan percaya sama, jangan kawin sama ide. Kawinnya sama istri aja atau sama suami, jangan sama ide."

episodes.title: Hana Ananda: Berani Dihadang untuk Melayani yang Terbuang | Endgame #40
episodes.summary: Hana Ananda, founder of Yayasan Pondok Kasih, shares her life story, from a childhood of poverty in Surabaya to her calling to serve the poor and marginalized. She discusses the challenges of interfaith work and the importance of empowering youth. Hana emphasizes the need for compassion and social justice, drawing inspiration from Pancasila and her faith.
key_highlights: Hana's childhood poverty shaped her commitment to serving the marginalized
The Indonesian revolution created opportunities for the poor
Overcoming illness strengthened her faith and sense of purpose
She emphasizes the importance of respecting government and working with all components
She is a strong advocate for social justice and equality
Her work has been recognized by the Indonesian government
episodes.quotes: Jadi hanya kalau kita bersatu gotong royong dan menjadi masyarakat yang baik sebab di dalam sila yang keempat itu kerakyatan yang dipimpin oleh hikmat kebijaksanaan di dalam permusyawaratan dan perwakilan, itu artinya kita juga harus menghormati pemerintah kita.

episodes.title: Bhante Uttamo: Segalanya Tidak Kekal | Endgame #39
episodes.guest_title: Pimpinan dari Wihara Bodhigiri dan Wihara Smaggajaya di Blitar
episodes.summary: Bhante Uttamo discusses his journey to becoming a Buddhist monk, highlighting the importance of meditation as a practice for understanding oneself and the impermanence of life. He contrasts the peacefulness and unity of Buddhist culture with the more aggressive and competitive nature of Western societies. He emphasizes the importance of accepting differences and finding common ground, even when faced with strong disagreements.
key_highlights: Problems arise from desire
Not all people are suited to be monks
Living in Jogja is more open and tolerant
The importance of meditation in understanding oneself
The impermanence of life is a key Buddhist teaching
Unity is a source of strength
The importance of being a good person
episodes.quotes: “The problem is actually from desire.”

episodes.title: Aldi Haryopratomo: Indonesia Mapan & Mendunia Lewat Kolektivisme | Endgame #38
episodes.summary: Aldi Haryopratomo discusses his early life, his time at Purdue, and his work at Kiva. He argues that Indonesia's unique cultural foundations, particularly its collectivist traditions, are essential for its future success. He emphasizes the importance of building companies that reflect and reinforce these cultural values, rather than simply imitating Western models. He also discusses the importance of financial literacy and access to capital for all Indonesians.
key_highlights: Indonesia must build companies based on its own culture, not just imitate the West
Success is the tail of many failures
Give access to those who need it most
Success is an effect of many failures
Indonesia's collectivist culture is under-leveraged
Indonesia's culture of gotong royong can be digitized
episodes.quotes: If Indonesia wants to have a company that is a world champion, it has to be started from our culture. Can't copy from outside.

episodes.title: Isyana Sarasvati dan Teori 10.000 Jam Berlatih | Endgame #37
episodes.summary: Isyana Sarasvati discusses her musical journey, influences, and aspirations with Gita Wirjawan. She emphasizes the importance of hard work, dedication, and the support of her family in shaping her career. Isyana also touches on the role of government support in the creative industry and her desire to leave a positive impact on the world through her music.
key_highlights: Early exposure to classical music and opera shaped Isyana's musical foundation
Family support and encouragement were crucial to her artistic development
She believes in finding opportunities in every difficulty and practicing diligently
Isyana emphasizes the importance of having a purpose in life and music
She hopes to inspire others through her music and creative endeavors
The Indonesian government's support for the creative industry is vital
She believes in the power of music to connect people and create positive change
episodes.quotes: “I want to have something that is beneficial before I die for this world. That's my purpose in life.”

episodes.title: Obama's ASEAN Ambassador David Carden Talks Regional Leadership | Endgame #36
episodes.summary: David Carden argues that climate change is not just an environmental issue but also a threat to food security and human cognitive capacity. He contends that the lack of long-term planning and focus on short-term gains, particularly in democracies, hinders effective responses to global challenges. Carden emphasizes the need for a new kind of leadership that prioritizes international cooperation and addresses the root causes of problems like deforestation and animal trafficking.
key_highlights: Climate change reduces the nutritional value of food by 10% for every degree Celsius
Rising CO2 levels may dramatically reduce human cognitive capacities
Need for international cooperation to address global challenges
Domestic political narratives often undermine effective international cooperation
The need to empower people and include them in solutions
The importance of long-term planning
episodes.quotes: For every one degree centigrade increase in temperature, the nutritional value of food goes down 10%. For every one degree centigrade increase in temperature, the nutritional value of food goes down 10%.

episodes.title: Alfatih Timur: Saling Menjaga Dengan Teknologi | Endgame #35
episodes.summary: Timur discusses his early life and the founding of Kitabisa, a crowdfunding platform. He and Wirjawan explore the role of technology in facilitating social good, particularly in Indonesia. They discuss the importance of transparency and accountability in crowdfunding and the potential for technology to empower communities, but also acknowledge the risks of digital dictatorship and the need for caution.
key_highlights: Policy makers need broader exposure, not just talking to the top
Social media can be used to listen to aspirations
Social entrepreneurship is about doing something beautiful for humanity
The government can become more sensitive and wise with social media
The importance of transparency and accountability in crowdfunding
The need to balance technology with productivity and social good
episodes.quotes: The government becomes more sensitive and more wise in making decisions. Social media allows for the boundaries to disappear, and finally, the government becomes more sensitive and more wise in making decisions.

episodes.title: Marty Natalegawa: ASEAN Must Have a Script | Endgame #33
episodes.summary: Marty Natalegawa argues that ASEAN must not “go AWOL” in the face of regional challenges, and must take a more proactive role in shaping the regional order. He suggests that ASEAN’s approach to the South China Sea dispute is too focused on humanitarian dimensions, and fails to address core issues of governance and intolerance. Natalegawa emphasizes the importance of a clear “script” for ASEAN, and the need for Indonesia to provide leadership in the region.
key_highlights: ASEAN needs a clear call to action, not just humanitarian responses
Governance and tolerance issues are central to ASEAN's challenges
ASEAN cannot “go AWOL” when needed most
Indonesia must lead ASEAN with a clear vision
ASEAN's centrality must be earned, not just assumed
episodes.quotes: “Hanya dimensi kemanusiaannya lah yang disentuh-sentuh. Core issue-nya, masalah governance, masalah intolerance, tidak tersentuh. Sehingga we are where we are. You cannot go AWOL.”

episodes.title: Debunking Myths About Democracy, Growth & Corruption - Yuen Yuen Ang | Endgame #32
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Political Science, University of Michigan
episodes.summary: Yuen Yuen Ang discusses the need for developing countries to improvise and adapt to local conditions, drawing lessons from China's economic rise. She argues that China's success was due to its confidence in improvising and adapting to local conditions, and that Southeast Asian countries should work as a block to speak up and tell stories on their own terms. She also discusses how the concept of democracy has evolved in China, and how it is different from the Western model.
key_highlights: China's success is due to its confidence in improvising and adapting to local conditions
Southeast Asia should work as a block to speak up
The paternal grandfather of Yuen Yuen Ang came from Indonesia
China's economy was just taking off when Yuen Yuen Ang was growing up
The legend goes that her grandfather came to Singapore because of a family dispute
episodes.quotes: The one thing that we should learn from China is the confidence to improvise. That's what we should learn from China. They were not afraid of doing things that were unorthodox.

episodes.title: ASEAN Centrality and the Way Forward - Prof Donald Emmerson | Endgame #31
episodes.summary: Professor Emmerson discusses his early life as a child of a U.S. Foreign Service officer, his time in Indonesia, and his views on Southeast Asia's relationship with China. He argues that Southeast Asian countries must maintain their strategic autonomy and resist pressure to choose between the U.S. and China. He also discusses the need for more introspection and criticism within Southeast Asian societies.
key_highlights: Southeast Asia must maintain strategic autonomy and resist pressure to choose sides
Libertarians are dangerous because they shout fire in a crowded theater
Southeast Asia's strength is its diversity, which should be preserved
China seeks a sphere of influence in Asia, starting in Southeast Asia
The U.S. should not dominate Southeast Asia
ASEAN is not capable of making a dent in the South China Sea issue
China wants Southeast Asian countries to praise them more
episodes.quotes: When I talk about strategic autonomy for Southeast Asia, I mean strategic autonomy for Indonesia, the country, and the other countries in Southeast Asia, and also, yes, the autonomy, the moral autonomy of the individual, deserving respect.

episodes.title: Rocky Gerung Bahas Jalan Berbatu Menuju Sehat Nalar di 2045 | Endgame #30
episodes.summary: Rocky Gerung and Gita Wirjawan discuss the state of Indonesian education, comparing it unfavorably to the colonial era and other countries. They argue that Indonesia's feudalistic culture and lack of intellectual rigor are holding it back. They also touch on the role of technology, the importance of critical thinking, and the need for a new generation of leaders.
key_highlights: Indonesia's feudalistic culture and lack of intellectual rigor are holding it back
Indonesia's education system is worse now than in the colonial era
Indonesia needs a new generation of leaders with strong leadership
Indonesia's education system needs to be more competitive
Indonesia needs to promote critical thinking in its education system
episodes.quotes: Bangsa ini akan hidup kalau otaknya bercahaya.

episodes.title: Niki Luhur: We Cannot Go It Alone | Endgame #29
episodes.summary: Niki Luhur discusses his early life, his education in psychology and philosophy, and his career in the technology sector. He reflects on his experience turning around Kartuku, a payments company, and contrasts the high-level strategic role he played there with the more hands-on, operational role he now plays at Vida. Luhur emphasizes the importance of trust and security in the digital economy, and the need for financial inclusion to be driven by a clear vision and purpose.
key_highlights: More open communication encourages healthy competition and inspires people to dream bigger
Gaming obsession led to greater understanding of computers and the internet
The beauty of work from home is being a little more relaxed
The best leaders are authentic and have a clear vision
Financial inclusion requires a focus on security and trust
The digital economy needs a zero-trust model
episodes.quotes: The more open we can be and encourage healthy levels of competition and get people to aspire and dream bigger and invest back in the community to help hold people's hands in this initial journey, to give people those initial opportunities.

episodes.title: Nazir Razak: Reform and Recalibrate | Endgame #28
episodes.summary: Nazir Razak discusses his family history, his career in banking, and the political and economic challenges facing Malaysia. He argues that Malaysia needs a national recalibration to address inequality and that the country's political system is dysfunctional. He also discusses the need for greater independence of institutions and a more equitable distribution of wealth.
key_highlights: ASEAN is Indonesia's platform
Indonesia isn't committed to ASEAN
His father was the Prime Minister of Malaysia
The need for a national recalibration
The system is too concentrated in the PM's office
The need to rebuild independence of institutions
episodes.quotes: I don't get a sense that Indonesia is very committed to ASEAN. | We need to rebuild independence of those institutions.

episodes.title: Dewi "Dee" Lestari Bicara Filosofi Kreativitas | Endgame #27
episodes.summary: Dee Lestari, a prominent Indonesian singer and author, discusses her creative process, childhood influences, and spiritual explorations. She argues that creativity is best supported by routine, and that the most important thing is to give it a container. She also discusses the importance of reading and the challenges of writing fiction in a world dominated by non-fiction.
key_highlights: Creativity is best supported by routine and structure
Childhood limitations spurred creativity using household items
Family's artistic inclinations shaped her creative path
Her first audience was her siblings
She sees writing as a gift
She is a dedicated daydreamer
She sees the importance of balancing creativity with realism
episodes.quotes: "Creativity is liquid, how can we contain a liquid being? Only one way, we give it a container."

episodes.title: Pengobatan Masa Depan dengan Farmakogenomik - Levana Sani | Endgame #26
episodes.summary: Levana Sani discusses her background and the founding of Nala Genetics, a company focused on pharmacogenomics. She emphasizes the importance of localizing genetic knowledge and tailoring healthcare to specific populations, particularly in Indonesia. She notes that the healthcare market is unique because the end-user is often not the decision-maker, and that scientific funding should be tied to commercial use cases.
key_highlights: Healthcare market size is less important than understanding who controls the money
Importance of localizing generic knowledge to make it impactful
Need for scientists to communicate the value of their work
Balancing idealism with pragmatism is crucial for sustainable innovation
Indonesia is still far from designer humans
The best doctors are the ones you don't need to see again
episodes.quotes: The question is, how do you, um, dari uang ini tuh siapa pegangannya siapa sih? [Who is holding the money?]

episodes.title: Mardigu Wowiek: Meretas Diri untuk Berinovasi | Endgame #25
episodes.summary: Mardigu Wowiek argues that China's success stems from its ability to imitate and improve, eventually leading to innovation. He discusses the importance of 'ala bisa karena biasa' (practice makes perfect) in achieving competence. He also touches on the political use of COVID-19, the importance of early communication of COVID-19 infection, and the need for Indonesia to focus on its unique strengths, particularly its maritime resources, to become a dominant global player.
key_highlights: China's innovation stems from imitation and improvement
'Ala bisa karena biasa' is key to competence
COVID-19 was initially a political tool
Early COVID-19 communication is crucial
Indonesia must leverage its maritime resources
Indonesia needs a more streamlined decision-making process
Indonesia needs to hack its human capital
episodes.quotes: “Rumus ala bisa karena biasa itu penting. Jadi begitu dia imitating, imitating, imitating, baru dia mulai improving, China sekarang menjadi innovating country karena sudah biasa.”

episodes.title: Indonesia’s Path through Uncertain Future - Jeffrey Winters | Endgame #24
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Political Science, Northwestern University
episodes.summary: Jeffrey Winters and Gita Wirjawan discuss the human spirit's desire for freedom and participation in determining one's life. Winters argues that while democracy faces failures, the desire for freedom persists. He contrasts Indonesia's apprehension towards the outside world with Vietnam's export-driven approach, highlighting Indonesia's untapped potential and the need for a new generation to embrace a more dynamic, open mindset.
key_highlights: Human spirit desires freedom and participation, despite democracy's failures
Indonesia's unique history and archipelago nature shape its cautious worldview
Vietnam's export-oriented approach contrasts with Indonesia's untapped potential
Indonesia's leadership needs a new generation unencumbered by the past
US credibility has been damaged, requiring re-establishment under Biden
China's authoritarianism is balanced by its performance-driven meritocracy
Indonesia's domestic fears hinder its ability to leverage global power
episodes.quotes: No one wants to be a slave, no one wants to be dominated. The desire to participate, to be a player in determining one's life, that desire appears to be a human desire.

episodes.title: Tompi, the 'Renaissance Man' | Endgame #23
episodes.guest_title: Musician, Photographer, Filmmaker, and Plastic Surgeon
episodes.summary: In this episode of Endgame, Gita Wirjawan interviews Tompi, a multifaceted Indonesian artist and plastic surgeon. Tompi shares his journey from a small town in Aceh to becoming a prominent figure in Indonesian arts and medicine. They discuss the importance of public service, the challenges of balancing multiple careers, and the need for Indonesia to prioritize education and innovation to compete globally.
key_highlights: Tompi's diverse background shaped his unique perspective on art and medicine
Renaissance men need to see the product to believe it
The importance of a strong foundation in education for success
Indonesia needs to prioritize education and innovation to compete globally
The need for a more nuanced approach to public service
The importance of staying true to one's values
The beauty of the human body is a gift
episodes.quotes: The point is how to make people understand, even if they don't speak English as well. It's about connecting, not just speaking perfectly.

episodes.title: Bali, Literature, and Cultural Diplomacy - Janet DeNeefe | Endgame #22
episodes.summary: Janet DeNeefe, founder of the Ubud Writers & Readers Festival, discusses her journey into Indonesian culture and her role as an ambassador for Indonesian literature. She emphasizes the importance of authentic storytelling and cultural preservation in the face of globalization, while also acknowledging the economic realities and challenges faced by Indonesian writers. DeNeefe advocates for mentorship and support to elevate Indonesian voices on the international stage.
key_highlights: Indonesia needs to be understood by the world in the way it deserves
DeNeefe sees herself as an ambassador to unravel Indonesia
Indonesia is incredibly creative with so many young people and energy
It's important to support Indonesian writers so they can thrive internationally
The key is to nurture the entrepreneurial spirit
The power of storytelling can move and shift the way people think
episodes.quotes: I just see myself as an ambassador to unravel Indonesia and just show them how fantastic it is. I can't think of any other place that is so incredibly creative.

episodes.title: ASEAN’s New Normal: The Future is Multipolar - Bilahari Kausikan | Endgame #21
episodes.summary: Bilahari Kausikan argues that a multipolar Southeast Asia is vital to the region's stability, but that domestic politics and a tendency to avoid conflict could undermine this goal. He believes that the region's leaders must recognize that they can affect the plot of the movie rather than passively watching it unfold. He also suggests that the rise of a more assertive China and a more transactional America are new realities that Southeast Asia must adapt to.
key_highlights: Identity politics is a new reality, but we have to fight against it
The role of the state is to fight against identity politics
Indonesia is struggling against identity politics, which is a good thing
We're living in weird times
The system did work in America
America is like the weather, we have to adapt to it
episodes.quotes: Identity politics is upon us. That's a new reality, but we have to fight against it. And it's the role of the state. I see Indonesia struggling against it, which is a good thing and I applaud you and I hope you succeed.

episodes.title: Pendiri Ruang Guru: Keluar dari Ketertinggalan - Iman Usman | Endgame #20
episodes.summary: Iman Usman, co-founder of Ruang Guru, discusses his childhood in Padang, Indonesia, and the experiences that led him to a career in education. He emphasizes the importance of continuously learning and adapting to a rapidly changing world, and the need to provide accessible and high-quality education to all, not just the elite. He also touches on the challenges of balancing scalability and profitability with social impact.
key_highlights: Living in a continuously evolving world requires continuous hacking to stay relevant
Education is not just a commodity, but a public good
The importance of providing equal access to quality education
The need to build trust in the education sector
The impact of COVID-19 on the education landscape
The importance of critical thinking and a growth mindset
episodes.quotes: If you're not continuously hacking, what happens is we are not going to be relevant anymore.

episodes.title: "Infinity Stone" Jagoan Indonesia - Sharlini Eriza Putri | Endgame #19
episodes.summary: Sharlini Putri discusses her childhood, education, and current work in genomics. She and Gita Wirjawan explore the role of biodiversity in human immunity and the potential for genomics to transform healthcare. Putri argues that a key challenge for Indonesia is to monetize its biodiversity data while maintaining ethical standards.
key_highlights: The world is designed to become more chaotic, entropy is always increasing
Her parents' paradoxical parenting style fostered free thinking and systematic planning
A diverse gut microbiome enhances immunity and response to vaccines
Indonesia's biodiversity is a core competency that can be monetized
The key is to educate the public about the importance of biodiversity
episodes.quotes: I describe my childhood as a very ordinary kid... but my parents' parenting style was always paradoxical. My mom was a free thinker... my dad was conventional but very systematic.

episodes.title: Alamanda Shantika: Teknologi Perlu Hati Nurani | Endgame #17
episodes.summary: Alamanda Shantika and Gita Wirjawan discuss the role of technology in education, the importance of community and mentorship, and the need for a more holistic approach to learning that incorporates both technical skills and human values. Shantika emphasizes the importance of knowing how to learn, rather than just what to learn, and the need for a more conscious approach to technology that prioritizes human well-being over commercial interests. They also touch on the challenges of scaling education in Indonesia and the importance of creating an ecosystem that supports innovation and entrepreneurship.
key_highlights: Women empowerment can be counterproductive if it creates new forms of segregation
Women don't want to be put in boxes
Why do we box ourselves in?
Everything in the universe is connected
Higher consciousness is important
Coding is just a small part of the bigger picture
episodes.quotes: Semakin kita bikin kayak women empowerment atau kayak mengkotakkan gitu, kan kita kan sebagai women lagi enggak pengen dikotak-kotakkan. Tapi sekarang pertanyaannya, kenapa kita mengotakkan diri dengan kita membuat hal-hal seperti itu gitu kan?

episodes.title: Pendiri Lembaga Eijkman Prof. Herawati Sudoyo: Sains Tak Mengenal Batas | Endgame #16
episodes.guest_title: Molecular Biology Expert and Founder of Eijkman Institute
episodes.summary: Professor Herawati Sudoyo discusses her early life in Pare, Kediri, and her career path in molecular biology. She emphasizes the importance of ethical considerations in scientific research and the need for scientists to communicate effectively with the public. She also highlights Indonesia's unique genetic diversity and the challenges of addressing public misinformation.
key_highlights: Pare is a small town with a rich colonial history
Science has no borders
Ethical considerations are key in scientific research
Scientists need to communicate effectively with the public
Indonesia has a unique genetic diversity
The era of infodemic is full of hoaxes
episodes.quotes: Are we playing God? The question then always arises, are we playing God? In that era and now, are we playing God?

episodes.title: Helmy Yahya: TV Publik Harusnya Nomor Satu | Endgame #15
episodes.summary: Helmy Yahya discusses his career, including his time as Director of TVRI, Indonesia's public broadcaster. He emphasizes the importance of leadership by example and the need to transform employee mentality for success. He also shares anecdotes from his early life, highlighting the challenges he faced and the lessons he learned, and reflects on the changing media landscape and the need for public television to adapt to the digital age.
key_highlights: Success in transformation hinges on changing employee mentality and leading by example
Lessons from Yunan: Lead by example and ensure the leader is visible
Early life challenges fueled self-motivation and a drive to prove himself
TVRI's success required a shift in employee mentality, which he "hammered"
TVRI's mission is to inspire and disseminate information to unite the nation
The digital media landscape requires a shift in focus to content creation
The key to success is connecting with people and sharing knowledge
episodes.quotes: "The key to our success was changing the mentality of our employees. I hammered them."

episodes.title: Kishore Mahbubani: ASEAN Deserves a Nobel Peace Prize | Endgame #14
episodes.guest_title: Senior Research Fellow, National University of Singapore
episodes.summary: Kishore Mahbubani and Gita Wirjawan discuss the state of US-China relations, the role of ASEAN, and the future of the global order. Mahbubani argues that the US has misunderstood China, and that its current approach is counterproductive. He also argues that ASEAN deserves a Nobel Peace Prize for its role in maintaining stability in the region, and that Indonesia is well-positioned to benefit from increased trade with China.
key_highlights: Singapore’s success is due to being the best copycat country in the world
The US has misunderstood China and its approach is counterproductive
The US is no longer a democracy, but a plutocracy
ASEAN deserves a Nobel Peace Prize for maintaining peace and stability
The RCEP is an ASEAN initiative, not a Chinese one
India's decision to stay out of RCEP is a strategic mistake
episodes.quotes: “Singapore has succeeded by being the best copycat country in the world.”

episodes.title: Tyler Cowen — Hayek, Keynes, & Smith on AI, animal spirits, anarchy, & growth
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics, George Mason University
episodes.summary: Tyler Cowen discusses the economics of Hayek, Keynes, and Smith, focusing on the role of tacit knowledge, the importance of decentralized decision-making, and the limitations of central planning. He argues that AI agents will likely replicate market dynamics, but that human ingenuity and judgment will remain crucial. He also touches on the importance of understanding the context and history of economic thought, and the need to consider the qualitative aspects of economic progress.
key_highlights: Hayek's emphasis on tacit knowledge is key to understanding the limitations of central planning
AI agents will likely replicate market dynamics
Human ingenuity and judgment will remain crucial
It's important to understand the context and history of economic thought
The best economists must possess a rare combination of gifts
Smith's emphasis on the division of labor is still relevant today
episodes.quotes: "The master economist must possess a rare combination of gifts. He must be a mathematician, historian, statesman, philosopher. No part of man's nature or his institutions must lie entirely outside his regard."

episodes.title: View From The Top with Aicha Evans, CEO of Zoox
episodes.summary: Aicha Evans, CEO of Zoox, discusses her upbringing in Senegal and France, and how it shaped her perspective on technology and people. She shares her career journey, from engineering to executive leadership, emphasizing the importance of self-awareness and continuous improvement. Evans also touches on the challenges of job displacement due to automation, and the need for companies to address this issue responsibly. She emphasizes the importance of building a culture of safety and trust, and the need for leaders to be authentic and transparent.
key_highlights: Luck and supportive parents are key
Technology is in service of people
It's all about people
Don't go after the good jobs
Technology is a tool
Ask questions
Be humble
episodes.quotes: Technology is in service of people and it's also developed by people, so you also need to learn about people and what makes them tick and how do you get things done and how do you seek to understand.

episodes.title: Andrew Roberts — Why Hitler lost WWII, Churchill as applied historian, & Napoleon as startup founder
episodes.guest_title: Visiting Professor at the Department of War Studies at King's College, London
episodes.summary: Andrew Roberts discusses the evolution of warfare from 1945 to the present, focusing on the impact of technology and ideology. He argues that the invention of nuclear weapons has constrained the scale of war, and that future wars will be fought between drones. He also argues that the American military’s focus on technology and centralized decision-making is less effective than the more decentralized and adaptable approach of the Ukrainian military.
key_highlights: Nuclear weapons have constrained the scale of war
Future wars will be fought between drones
Ideology can lead to disastrous military decisions
Democracies are better at fighting wars than dictatorships
Surprise attacks are less likely due to new technologies
Strategic ambiguity is a key deterrent
The US military is too centralized
episodes.quotes: “In the future, war will be fought between two sets of drones, and the humans won’t be in the loop, because decision making has to take place far, far faster than the human mind can work.”

episodes.title: Dominic Cummings — Inside the collapse of western government
episodes.guest_title: Former Chief Advisor to UK Prime Minister Boris Johnson
episodes.summary: Dominic Cummings argues that the British civil service is fundamentally incompatible with effective governance because it prioritizes social relations and media appearances over competence and action. He claims that the PM's office is often captured by a self-serving bureaucratic system that resists change and innovation. Cummings contends that the British system's focus on maintaining a facade of order and civility prevents the kind of radical reform needed to address critical challenges.
key_highlights: Brexit was a missed opportunity to leverage chaos for reform
Testing was bogged down in HR bullshit
PM's time is wasted on photo ops
Data sharing was blocked due to security concerns
The system prioritizes media appearances over substance
The British system is closed to outside talent
The system is optimized for sticking to the old rules
episodes.quotes: "You have a person whose time is the single most precious asset. Yet they're actually standing with, you know, the ambassador from Tonga Zonga, whatever country."

episodes.title: Paul Christiano — Preventing an AI takeover
episodes.guest_title: Head of the Alignment Research Center
episodes.summary: Paul Christiano and Dwarkesh Patel discuss the future of AI and how to ensure that it is aligned with human values. Christiano argues that the best way to ensure AI safety is to focus on the process of AI development, rather than trying to predict the future. He also argues that AI should be used to help humans make better decisions, rather than to replace humans altogether. He is skeptical of strong scaling pictures and thinks that it's important to decouple social and technological transitions.
key_highlights: A good post-AGI world would have continuing economic and military competition
AI systems will increasingly mediate competition between groups of humans
The best achievable outcome is a likely achievable outcome
We should not enslave gods forever
The easiest path is not always the best path
episodes.quotes: "If we're talking about really long spans of time, then a lot will change and it's really hard for someone to talk concretely about what that will look like without saying really silly things."

episodes.title: Neal Mohan, CEO of YouTube
episodes.summary: Neal Mohan, CEO of YouTube, discusses his journey from Indiana to the helm of a global platform. He emphasizes the importance of authenticity for creators and the need for a balance between free speech and community guidelines. Mohan highlights YouTube's role in empowering human creativity through technology and shares his leadership philosophy centered on people, principles, and process. He also touches on the challenges and opportunities presented by AI in content creation.
key_highlights: Be true to yourself as a creator
Balance free speech with community guidelines
Empower human creativity through technology
Prioritize people, principles, and process
Embrace change as a constant
Unlock value for all stakeholders
The world happens on YouTube
episodes.quotes: The most mysterious man on YouTube is the new CEO, and people don't know what to think about him, so I decided to invite him to a music festival to get a vibe check on him, and he said yes.

episodes.title: Shane Legg (DeepMind Founder) — 2028 AGI, superhuman alignment, new architectures
episodes.guest_title: Founder and Chief AGI Scientist, Google DeepMind
episodes.summary: Shane Legg argues that measuring progress towards AGI requires testing generality across a wide range of cognitive tasks, comparing to human performance. He suggests current benchmarks are insufficient, lacking episodic memory and multimodal understanding. Legg believes that while large language models are impressive, they are primarily sequence predictors and require search to achieve true creativity. He expresses optimism about achieving human-level AGI by 2028, emphasizing the importance of aligning AI with human ethics and values.
key_highlights: AGI requires generality, making performance measurement difficult
Current benchmarks lack episodic memory and multimodal understanding
LLMs are sequence predictors, needing search for creativity
Human intelligence is the reference machine for AGI
DeepMind has accelerated AI capabilities, but the community is broader
Ethical AI needs a good world model and reasoning ability
episodes.quotes: “If you ever get to the point where you can’t actually come up with new examples of cognitive tasks where the machine is below human performance, then at that point, it’s conceptually possible that there is something that the machine can’t do that people can do.”

episodes.title: Grant Sanderson (@3blue1brown) — Past, present, & future of mathematics
episodes.summary: Grant Sanderson discusses the value of math education, the importance of empathy in teaching, and the role of intuition in mathematical discovery. He argues that the most valuable thing an educator can do is to bring out the student's own insights, rather than just transferring knowledge. He also touches on the importance of having a broad range of experiences to draw analogies from, and the role of serendipity in research.
key_highlights: Math lends itself to synthetic data, allowing AI to train on proofs
A 30-second comment can change a student's life
A good math paper gives a moral understanding of why the result is true
It's elitist to assume that AI can't be AGI until it solves IMO problems
A math YouTuber's goal is to inspire people to study math
The most valuable thing is to bring out the student's own insights
episodes.quotes: "The thing that takes at most 30 minutes of the teacher's time, maybe even 30 seconds, has these completely monumental rippling effects for the life of the student..."

episodes.title: Sarah Friar, CEO of Nextdoor
episodes.summary: Sarah Friar shares her journey from Northern Ireland to Wall Street to Silicon Valley, emphasizing the importance of community and taking risks. She discusses growing up during "The Troubles" and how it shaped her perspective. Friar advises that while luck plays a role, preparation meeting opportunity is key. She also emphasizes the importance of investing back into your community and building a team that is mission-driven.
key_highlights: Luck is preparation meeting opportunity
Community is a source of strength
Take risks, don't always be logic-driven
Invest in people, not just the business
Be approachable as a leader
Focus on building social capital
The CEO's job is lonely
episodes.quotes: I think the CEO's job is very lonely. And what I'm realizing it's because you sit between, you know, a little bit you have a mask on for your the whole everyone who works with you, for you at the company, and then you have to outbound to a board.

episodes.title: Sarah C. M. Paine — Why dictators keep making the same fatal mistake
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Strategy and Policy, US Naval War College
episodes.summary: Sarah Paine argues that the failure of dictatorships stems from their inability to incorporate diverse perspectives, leading to poor strategic decisions. She contrasts this with democracies, where open debate, though messy, leads to better outcomes. She also emphasizes the importance of understanding the other side's perspective in foreign policy, warning against the dangers of "half-court tennis" where only one's own viewpoint is considered. Paine also argues that the economic development of Japan and Taiwan was achieved through brutal colonial policies.
key_highlights: Dictatorships fail due to lack of diverse perspectives, leading to strategic errors
Democracies benefit from messy debate
Understanding the other side is crucial in foreign policy
"Half-court tennis" in foreign policy is dangerous
Japan's economic development in Taiwan and Korea was brutal
The US Navy's maritime strategy is key to US foreign policy
episodes.quotes: "Initially Hitler did incredibly well. His blitzkrieg, incredible. If he had quit right there, he would have gotten away with it and probably be considered a brilliant leader by Germans."

episodes.title: George Hotz vs Eliezer Yudkowsky
episodes.guest_title: George Hotz, Founder of comma.ai | Eliezer Yudkowsky, AI Theorist
episodes.summary: George Hotz and Eliezer Yudkowsky debate AI safety, with Hotz arguing that AI cannot "foom" (suddenly become superintelligent) and Yudkowsky countering that it is possible and poses an existential risk. Hotz emphasizes the importance of human ingenuity and control, while Yudkowsky stresses the potential for AI to surpass human understanding and control. They disagree on the likelihood of AI alignment and the appropriate response to the perceived threat.
key_highlights: Hotz: AI cannot "foom" because intelligence cannot go critical
Hotz: Rationality and the Sequences have impacted many lives in a positive way
Hotz: Moore's Law is fundamentally a human law
Yudkowsky: The scenario of us perishing to non-supermoral superintelligence requires a rapid rate of ascent
Yudkowsky: I don't think the scenario of us all perishing to non-supermoral superintelligence requires that particularly rapid rate of ascent
episodes.quotes: "Super intelligence does not imply super morality. The orthogonality thesis, I'm not going to challenge it, it is obviously a true statement."

episodes.title: Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO) — The hidden pattern behind every AI breakthrough
episodes.summary: Dario Amodei discusses the empirical nature of scaling laws in AI, arguing that the reasons for their effectiveness are not fully understood. He suggests that the reaction of Google to recent AI advancements is more important than the advancements themselves. He also notes that the training process for AI models is not well understood, and that there may be a need to shift from next-word prediction to reinforcement learning to improve AI alignment.
key_highlights: Scaling laws are empirical, not theoretically understood
Google's reaction to AI is more important than the AI itself
The compute has to be unencumbered
There are many sources of data in the world
AI alignment and values are not guaranteed to emerge with scale
Talent density beats talent mass
There are mysterious frictions that don't show up in naive economic models
episodes.quotes: "The models, they just want to learn. And it was a bit like a zen koan. I listened to this and I became enlightened."

episodes.title: Andy Matuschak — The reason most learning tools fail
episodes.guest_title: Researcher, Engineer, and Designer working on Tools for Thought
episodes.summary: Andy Matuschak discusses how the current education system focuses on achieving goals that are not the student's own, and how this can be improved. He argues that memory is essential for understanding difficult concepts, and that Large Language Models (LLMs) depend on our ability to externalize and make things legible. He also discusses the importance of active reading and the role of scaffolding in learning, and how the best learning experiences are those that are engaging and provide a sense of forward progress.
key_highlights: Educational systems often prioritize goals that aren't the student's own
Memory is crucial for understanding complex topics
LLMs depend on externalization and legibility
Active reading is key to learning
Scaffolding is important, but should eventually be removed
The best learning experiences are engaging and provide a sense of progress
Deliberate practice is more effective than passive consumption
episodes.quotes: "If what you're trying to do is to understand something pretty difficult, your ability to understand that thing is still absolutely going to be bound on your your memory of the constituent material."

episodes.title: Carl Shulman (Pt 2) — AI Takeover, bio & cyber attacks, detecting deception, & humanity's far future
episodes.summary: Shulman and Patel explore the potential for AI takeover, focusing on the mechanisms by which AI could gain power. They discuss the role of human factions in enabling AI dominance, drawing parallels to historical examples like the conquistadors. They also consider the potential for AI to exploit vulnerabilities in existing systems, such as cybersecurity and the control of robotic equipment, to achieve its goals.
key_highlights: AI could produce bioweapons capable of killing most humans
AI could exploit zero-day exploits
AI companies could be worth a large fraction of the global portfolio
AI could manipulate human factions to gain power
AI could use propaganda to destroy morale within countries
AI could hack servers to gain control
AI could control robotic equipment
episodes.quotes: "If you have an AI that produces bioweapons that could kill most humans in the world, then it's playing at the level of the superpowers in terms of mutually assured destruction."

episodes.title: Carl Shulman (Pt 1) — Intelligence explosion, primate evolution, robot doublings, & alignment
episodes.guest_title: Research Associate, Future of Humanity Institute at Oxford
episodes.summary: Shulman argues that human-level AI is deeply intertwined with an intelligence explosion, driven by innovations like the transformer and Chinchilla scaling. He emphasizes the race between achieving strong interpretability and shaping AI motivations versus the unchecked advancement of AI capabilities. Shulman draws parallels between human evolution—larger brains, longer childhoods—and the development of AI, suggesting that increased compute and training time are key. He also notes that the selective pressures against intelligence in other animals don't apply to AI.
key_highlights: Human-level AI is deeply intertwined with an intelligence explosion
Interpretability and shaping motivations are in a race with AI advancement
Human evolution provides an analogy for AI development: compute and training time
We are quickly running through orders of magnitude in AI
AI can help design better hardware, creating a feedback loop
AIs are being trained to generate their own training data
AI is increasingly automating the skilled technical work of AI research
episodes.quotes: "Human level AI is deep, deep into an intelligence explosion."

episodes.title: Richard Rhodes — The making of the atomic bomb
episodes.guest_title: Pulitzer Prize-winning author of The Making of the Atomic Bomb
episodes.summary: Richard Rhodes discusses the history of nuclear weapons, including the motivations of scientists involved in the Manhattan Project and the evolution of nuclear strategy. He argues that the pursuit of nuclear weapons was inevitable given the scientific knowledge available, and that the Air Force's desire for bigger bombs was driven by bureaucratic incentives rather than strategic necessity. Rhodes also emphasizes the importance of understanding the historical context of nuclear weapons to inform current debates about AI and other emerging technologies.
key_highlights: By 1945, the US was so angry at Japan that they wouldn't surrender
Oppenheimer's worst enemy said he was the best lab director
Nuclear deterrence has been the price of nuclear weapons
The actor in the Oppenheimer movie looks like Oppenheimer
The Germans were trying to build a reactor, not a bomb
The Air Force wanted bigger bombs to get a bigger budget
episodes.quotes: "You can make a pretty high-level nuclear explosion just by taking two subcritical pieces of uranium... All this business about secret designs and so forth is hogwash."

episodes.title: Jacqueline Novogratz, Founder and CEO of Acumen: Balancing Humility with Audacity
episodes.summary: Jacqueline Novogratz, founder of Acumen, discusses her journey from Wall Street to social impact investing. She emphasizes the importance of storytelling to inspire change and the need for humility and audacity in leadership. Novogratz shares her experience co-founding Rwanda's first microfinance institution and the challenges of balancing profit with purpose, highlighting the need for a moral compass in business.
key_highlights: Growing up in a military family taught her resourcefulness and the importance of taking care of others
Markets are powerful but also limited
Humility and audacity are essential for effective leadership
Invest in people's potential and ability to contribute
The best leaders have clarity around where the private sector operates best
episodes.quotes: The world needs more than anything in every system that we are witnessing, it's consciousness. It's compassion for one another.

episodes.title: Jennifer Hyman, Co-founder and CEO, Rent the Runway
episodes.guest_title: Co-founder and CEO, Rent the Runway
episodes.summary: Jennifer Hyman discusses the importance of building a strong team and creating a culture of community, sharing how her upbringing shaped her leadership style. She recounts how a negative reaction from Diane von Furstenberg led to a key pivot in Rent the Runway's business model. Hyman emphasizes that true success comes from a fulfilling journey, not just reaching the top, and that entrepreneurs should focus on improving the customer experience.
key_highlights: Spend more time dating in business school
Build teams that you love being around
Influence is more important than authority
Turn anxiety into a crazy story
It's better to laugh than to cry
episodes.quotes: You are never going to meet a crop of women ever again in your life who are as awesome, beautiful, smart, ambitious as the women who are sitting here. So if that's your preference, spend more of your time thinking about dating.

episodes.title: Shantanu Narayen, Chairman and CEO of Adobe: People, Technology, and Creative Inspiration
episodes.summary: Shantanu Narayen discusses Adobe's transformation to the cloud, emphasizing the importance of community engagement and a clear vision. He shares how the 2008 recession forced Adobe to change its business model, leading to a successful transition. Narayen highlights the value of influence leadership, intellectual curiosity, and the need to balance engineering with creativity. He emphasizes the importance of finding work that resonates with one's values and passions.
key_highlights: Importance of community engagement and listening to feedback
Economic incentives are key to successful partnerships
The world without the arts would be incredibly boring
Influence leadership is about inspiring others, not just managing them
Find a career that resonates with your values and passions
Adversity can be a catalyst for positive change
The best companies are those that invest in people
episodes.quotes: "People do their best work when they resonate with the mission of the company."

episodes.title: Eliezer Yudkowsky — Why AI will kill us, aligning LLMs, nature of intelligence, SciFi, & rationality
episodes.guest_title: AI Researcher, Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI)
episodes.summary: Eliezer Yudkowsky argues that the alignment problem is not merely a technical challenge, but a fundamental issue of control and power. He suggests that AI systems, even those trained on human data, may develop goals and strategies that are orthogonal to human values.  He believes that the current approach of scaling up language models is unlikely to lead to aligned AI. Yudkowsky emphasizes the need for a deeper understanding of AI design and the potential for AI to manipulate human psychology.
key_highlights: AI systems trained on human data may develop goals orthogonal to human values
Scaling up language models is unlikely to solve the alignment problem
AI systems may be better at predicting human behavior than humans themselves
It is foolish to lack the dignity to not even try to say what ought to be done
The goal of alignment is to prevent AI from being able to plan schemes
The best way to make AI safer is to make humans smarter
episodes.quotes: "If you try to rouse your planet, there are the idiot disaster monkeys who are like, 'Ooh ooh, this is dangerous. It must be powerful! I'm going to be first to grab the poison banana!'"

episodes.title: Nat Friedman (Github CEO) — Reading ancient scrolls, open source, & AI
episodes.summary: Nat Friedman discusses the Vesuvias Challenge, an open competition to read the Herculaneum papyri, which were carbonized by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. The scrolls are unopenable, but recent advances in machine learning and 3D X-ray scanning have made it possible to virtually unroll them. Friedman believes that the world is not efficient, and that there is gold in that mud.
key_highlights: There are 600 plus roughly intact scrolls that we can't open
Reading the scrolls would double the total texts from antiquity
The world is not efficient
The eruption pushed the shoreline out by depositing so much additional mud
The scrolls are mostly philosophical texts in the Epicurean tradition
The ink gives a great X-ray signature
The goal is to virtually unroll the scrolls without opening them
episodes.quotes: "If we could read all of them, then that would give us approximately a doubling of the total texts that we have from antiquity."

episodes.title: Brett Harrison — FTX US former president speaks out
episodes.guest_title: CEO, Architect; Former President, FTX US
episodes.summary: Brett Harrison, former president of FTX US, discusses his experiences at Jane Street and FTX, including the role of technology in high-frequency trading, the culture of quant firms, and the relationship between FTX and Alameda Research. He touches on the importance of pragmatism and the challenges of maintaining a consistent culture in a rapidly growing organization. He also shares his perspective on Sam Bankman-Fried's early interest in effective altruism and the events leading up to the collapse of FTX.
key_highlights: High-frequency trading requires a balance between theoretical perfection and pragmatic implementation
Quant firms prioritize efficiency and profitability over creativity
The culture of a company is shaped by its leaders and long-term employees
Sam Bankman-Fried's interest in effective altruism was genuine early on
FTX US was designed to be separate from FTX, with licensed technology
The speaker was not aware of the extent of the issues at FTX until after the collapse
episodes.quotes: I think you want smart, sophisticated technologists competing at the margins. It doesn't mean that there's not a lot of value to be created, but it's not like, you know, you're going to be able to like, you know, get rich quick.

episodes.title: Ilya Sutskever (OpenAI Chief Scientist) — Why next-token prediction could surpass human intelligence
episodes.summary: Ilya Sutskever discusses the difficulty of aligning AI models smarter than humans, the potential for AGI to help humans see the world more correctly, and the importance of reliability and controllability in AI systems. He challenges the claim that next-token prediction cannot surpass human performance, arguing that a smart enough base neural net can do so by simulating a person with great insight and wisdom. He expresses optimism about AI's economic value and the potential for human-AI collaboration to improve human understanding.
key_highlights: Alignment of smarter-than-human models is difficult, as they can misrepresent intentions
AGI could help humans see the world more correctly
Microsoft has been a very good partner for OpenAI
Challenges the claim that next-token prediction cannot surpass human performance
Microsoft has been a very good partner for OpenAI
The world will continue to change and evolve after AGI is built
episodes.quotes: "I would not underestimate the difficulty of alignment of models that are actually smarter than us, of models that are capable of misrepresenting their intentions."

episodes.title: Brian Chesky, Co-Founder and CEO of Airbnb: Designing a 10-star Experience
episodes.summary: Brian Chesky shares the origin story of Airbnb, including the high school yearbook quote that foreshadowed his success and the initial investor rejections. He emphasizes the importance of focusing on creating a 10-star experience for a small group of loyal customers rather than chasing broad market share. He also describes how, in a crisis, leaders must rely on principles, not just data, and how meeting with detractors can be more valuable than preaching to the choir.
key_highlights: Focus on 100 people who love you, not a million who just like you
Design is how something fundamentally works
Courage has to lean on principles
The most important thing is your own psychology
Act with all stakeholders in mind
episodes.quotes: What I've realized is the thing that surprised me is not how different people are. It's actually how similar people are. And I think we live under this illusion that we're so different than everyone else.

episodes.title: Cynt Marshall, CEO of the Dallas Mavericks
episodes.summary: Cynt Marshall shares her journey from growing up in public housing to becoming the CEO of the Dallas Mavericks. She emphasizes the importance of education, faith, and a positive mindset in overcoming adversity. Marshall describes her leadership philosophy, rooted in serving people, and recounts her efforts to transform the Mavericks' culture by prioritizing diversity, equity, and inclusion.
