She stresses the importance of setting a clear vision, listening to all voices, and empowering employees to reach their full potential.
key_highlights: Importance of education and faith in overcoming adversity
Focus on what gets you up in the morning
Leadership is about serving people
Diversity, equity, and inclusion are critical for success
Set the tone at the top
Listen to all voices, not just the loudest
Words matter, especially as a leader
episodes.quotes: It's not about where you live, it's how you live. Zip code doesn't matter, education does. I'm just fired up all the time.

episodes.title: Tristan Walker, CEO of Walker & Company: The Problem with Sameness
episodes.guest_title: Founder and CEO, Walker & Company Brands
episodes.summary: Tristan Walker shares his journey from a broke childhood to Wall Street to founding Bevel, a shaving brand for people of color. He emphasizes the importance of authenticity, consistency, and knowing one's values. He also discusses the challenges of raising capital and the importance of finding investors who understand the mission.
key_highlights: Growing up broke fueled his ambition to become as wealthy as possible
Having a clear understanding of one's values is key
Authenticity is more important than chasing the next big thing
The best ideas solve problems you personally experience
Success requires stamina to overcome inevitable obstacles
Privilege can provide access and information
The best investors share your values
episodes.quotes: "I realized I was a victim of bad circumstance, bad situation, bad culture, and realized that I had something in me to create good circumstance, good culture."

episodes.title: Marc Andreessen — AI, crypto, 1000 Elon Musks, regrets, vulnerabilities, & managerial revolution
episodes.guest_title: Co-founder and General Partner, Andreessen Horowitz
episodes.summary: Marc Andreessen discusses the impact of AI on software development, arguing that future apps will be more conversational and that AI will upend fundamental assumptions about how software is built. He also touches on the role of venture capital in fostering innovation, the importance of contact with reality for entrepreneurs, and the cultural unease surrounding money and trade. He contrasts the old model of bourgeois capitalism with the managerial revolution, and speculates on the future of venture capital.
key_highlights: AI will transform software development into a dialogue between computer and machine
Incumbent education system is trying to destroy itself
Venture capital is essential for innovation; without it, the economy becomes 100% managerial
Starting a company is like chewing glass; eventually you start to like the taste of your own blood
The concept of money and trade have some underlying unease in different religions and cultures
The economy is 99% managerial, and we need to keep the 1% of the old model alive
episodes.quotes: "Starting a company is like chewing glass; eventually you start to like the taste of your own blood."

episodes.title: Lars Doucet — Progress, poverty, Georgism, & why rent is too damn high
episodes.summary: Lars Doucet argues that Georgism, the philosophy of 19th-century economist Henry George, offers a way to reconcile pro-business and pro-worker interests. Georgism emphasizes land as a key economic factor and advocates for a land value tax to address inequality. Doucet argues that Georgism is not just about land value tax, but about a broader understanding of land's role in history and the economy. He also argues that the current tax system is regressive and that a land value tax would be a more just and efficient alternative.
key_highlights: Georgism is often reduced to its policy prescription of a land value tax
Land has a hidden role in the economy that is underrated
Control over land is the oldest struggle of human history
Land speculation causes land values to constantly increase
The current tax system is a gateway drug to the surveillance state
A land value tax is pro-worker and pro-business
Georgism is an abundance mindset
episodes.quotes: "Over the last century we've had this huge conflict, all the oxygen's been sucked up by capitalism and socialism duking it out. We have this assumption that you either have to be pro worker or pro business, that you can't be both."

episodes.title: Holden Karnofsky — History's most important century
episodes.summary: Holden Karnofsky discusses the "most important century" thesis, arguing that this century may be the most important in history due to the potential development of transformative AI. He explores the implications of this thesis for philanthropy, emphasizing the need to be cautious and vigilant about the future. Karnofsky also discusses the potential for AI to automate tasks currently done by humans, leading to explosive growth in science and technology.
key_highlights: The worst possible rule is to dismiss transformative AI as crazy
We need to be nervous and vigilant about what comes next
Economic growth has been exploding and accelerating recently
AI could fill the "more ideas" part of the economic growth loop
We should be investing more in AI alignment research
episodes.quotes: "If we had AI systems that could do everything humans do to advance science and technology, that would be insane."

episodes.title: Garett Jones — Immigration, national IQ, & less democracy
episodes.summary: Garett Jones discusses his book, *The Cultural Transplant*, arguing that migrants make economies more like the ones they left. He also discusses the relationship between national IQ and economic prosperity, arguing that high national IQ is crucial for innovation and long-term growth. Jones suggests that policies aimed at increasing national IQ, such as genetic selection, could have positive effects on society, but acknowledges the ethical concerns.
key_highlights: Migrants make economies more like the ones they left
High national IQ is crucial for innovation and long-term growth
Government policies and productivity are shaped by the citizens in a nation
The average traits of our ancestors are having an effect on our current productivity
Libertarians and open border advocates are rarely interested in long-term outcomes
episodes.quotes: It's weird how everybody's obsessed with running the experiment in America, right? Why not run it in Iceland first? Because America's got a lot of great institutions.

episodes.title: Bethany McLean — Enron, FTX, 2008, Musk, frauds, & visionaries
episodes.summary: Bethany McLean discusses the fine line between visionaries and fraudsters, arguing that self-delusion is a key component of business failures like Enron and FTX. She explores the role of incentives, culture, and the difficulty of identifying red flags in real-time. McLean also touches on the role of journalism and the importance of understanding the big picture, even if it means challenging popular narratives.
key_highlights: Self-delusion is a key component of business failures
Visionaries and fraudsters are closer than we think
Short-term incentives can lead to bad behavior
Culture is incredibly compelling
It's hard to see red flags in real-time
Finance is supposed to enable, not be the world itself
episodes.quotes: "The line between a visionary and a fraudster…they’re where the ends of the circle meet. Self-delusion is such a strong component of all of these stories of business gone wrong."

episodes.title: Nadia Asparouhova — Tech elites, democracy, open source, & philanthropy
episodes.guest_title: Author of "Working in Public", Nadia Asparouhova
episodes.summary: Nadia argues that effective altruism is more closely aligned with the finance/Wall Street elite mindset than the startup mindset. She and Dwarkesh discuss the motivations of tech billionaires, the role of charismatic founders in movements, and the trade-offs between community-driven and top-down governance. Nadia suggests that the most effective philanthropy may involve identifying and empowering talented individuals rather than building large, long-lasting institutions.
key_highlights: Effective altruism is closer to finance than startups
Founders read books and essays, influencing how they think
SBF's actions are more in line with finance than tech
Bezos's Amazon was motivated by a graph, not philosophy
Aristocratic elites have a sense of responsibility to give back
The most effective philanthropy may involve empowering talented individuals
Tech elites are increasingly skeptical of mainstream media
episodes.quotes: "They're like reading books. They're reading essays and like, and then those like books and essays influence how they think about stuff."

episodes.title: Byrne Hobart - FTX, Drugs, Twitter, Taiwan, & Monasticism
episodes.summary: Byrne Hobart and Dwarkesh Patel discuss the FTX collapse, speculating on the balance of fraud vs. incompetence, and the role of new drugs in financial markets. They explore the impact of parental involvement on success, and the archetype of the founder. They also consider the impact of war on social capital and the importance of having an adversary.
key_highlights: Impact of new drugs on financial markets is underrated
Atoms-to-bits interface not fully completed, which is merciful
Monastic tradition was stupid, independently discovered by numerous stupid civilizations
Invasion is the reason AI does not happen
The atoms-to-bits interface has not been fully completed
In a world where an invasion is the reason AI does not happen
episodes.quotes: What drives the response to what happened with FTX and Alameda is that if you think the story is pure fraud, it's very easy to say you would never do that.

episodes.title: Edward Glaeser - Cities, Terrorism, Housing, & Remote Work
episodes.guest_title: Chair of the Harvard Economics Department
episodes.summary: Glaeser argues that Silicon Valley is becoming a dangerous one-industry town, resembling Detroit in the 1950s more than its innovative 1960s self. He advises Elon Musk to avoid central planning when building cities on Mars, and instead to create a flexible grid that allows for organic growth. He notes that cities are shaped by the dominant transportation costs of their era, and that the high cost of travel to Earth will limit urbanization on Mars.
key_highlights: Silicon Valley is becoming a dangerous one-industry town
Demand for urbanism on Mars will be limited
Cities are shaped by transportation costs
Cities are defensible space
Central planning is bad
episodes.quotes: "The thing that many of us worry about in terms of Silicon Valley more recently is it feels much more like it's a one industry town, which is dangerous, and it feels more like it's a bunch of industrial behemoths rather than a bunch of smart and scrappy startups."

episodes.title: Kenneth T. Jackson - Robert Moses, Hero of New York?
episodes.guest_title: Jacques Barzun Professor Emeritus of History, Columbia University
episodes.summary: Kenneth Jackson discusses the legacy of Robert Moses, the urban planner who shaped much of New York City. Jackson argues that Moses, while ruthless, was essential for New York's survival and development, preventing it from declining like other Northeastern cities. He contrasts Moses's era of large-scale public works with today's NIMBYism and bureaucracy, highlighting the trade-offs between efficiency and democratic control.  Jackson also notes that Moses’s vision was limited, and he didn’t foresee the problems of car culture.
key_highlights: Moses's projects prevented New York from declining like other Northeastern cities
Moses was ruthless, arrogant, honest, and single-minded
Moses built for the future, but his vision was limited
Public works in New York are now more difficult due to bureaucracy
The Power Broker is flawed because it only used Moses's records
New York is still the most important city in the world
episodes.quotes: "Robert Moses represented a past, a time when we wanted to build bridges and superhighways. We're not building superhighways now. We're not building vast bridges like Moses built all the time."

episodes.title: Andy Dunn, Founder and CEO of Bonobos
episodes.summary: Andy Dunn, founder of Bonobos, shares his journey from launching the company at Stanford GSB to its $310 million acquisition by Walmart. He emphasizes the importance of mental health, sharing his personal experience with bipolar disorder and its impact on his leadership. Dunn discusses the challenges of co-founder relationships, the need for transparent communication, and the importance of having a clear decision-maker. He also shares his insights on innovation, the changing landscape of retail, and the need for leaders to create hope while defining reality.
key_highlights: Great entrepreneurs are thieves
Redefine risk as not doing what you love
The future is here, just unevenly distributed
The partnership eclipses the friendship
Create hope and define reality
Transparency with loved ones is critical
episodes.quotes: The friendship ends the day that you go into business together because the partnership eclipses it.

episodes.title: Brian Potter - Future of Construction, Ugly Modernism, & Environmental Review
episodes.guest_title: Engineer and Author of Construction Physics Blog
episodes.summary: Brian Potter discusses the challenges of innovation in the construction industry, contrasting it with other sectors. He argues that the industry's project-based nature, high material costs, and decentralized decision-making hinder productivity gains. He also touches on the role of regulation, the demand for customization, and the potential for new technologies like AR/VR and mass timber to improve construction processes.
key_highlights: Construction's project-based nature hinders economies of scale
High material costs limit potential for cost reduction
Decentralized decision-making slows adoption of new technologies
Demand for customization limits standardization
AR/VR can improve accuracy and reduce manual labor
Mass timber offers a more sustainable and efficient building material
Regulations and risk aversion stifle innovation
episodes.quotes: A one-dimensional city is like the worst possible arrangement for just like transportation, right? It's like you're maximum amount, maximum amount of distance between any two points.

episodes.title: Eric Yuan, CEO of Zoom
episodes.summary: Eric Yuan discusses the founding of Zoom, his journey as an immigrant to the US, and the challenges of scaling the company during the pandemic. He emphasizes the importance of focusing on customer needs and building trust. Yuan shares his leadership philosophy, which prioritizes employee happiness and social responsibility, and he reflects on the importance of adapting to change and learning from mistakes.
key_highlights: Focus on customer pain points to drive innovation
Embrace challenges as opportunities for growth
Prioritize employee happiness to create happy customers
Build trust through transparency and open communication
Focus on long-term sustainable happiness, not short-term gains
Delegate effectively and surround yourself with great leaders
episodes.quotes: The purpose of life is really about happiness. And also the sustainable happiness comes from making others happy.

episodes.title: Bryan Caplan - Feminists, Billionaires, and Demagogues
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics, George Mason University
episodes.summary: Bryan Caplan argues that feminism has become less true over time, as women are now less likely to be treated unfairly than men. He suggests that the focus on exaggerated or false claims of unfairness distracts from real issues. Caplan also contends that the ethos of "women and children first" is a form of reverse sexism. He also argues that the focus on demographic representation is misguided, and that meritocracy should be the guiding principle.
key_highlights: Feminism has become less true over time
The focus on demographic representation is misguided
Meritocracy should be the guiding principle
Women are now less likely to be treated unfairly than men
The ethos of "women and children first" is reverse sexism
episodes.quotes: "The amount of time that American feminists spend on female infanticide in China and India, could it even be 1% of the rhetoric? It's just not something that they care about."

episodes.title: Tyler Cowen - Why Society Will Collapse & Why Sex is Pessimistic
episodes.summary: Tyler Cowen discusses talent, culture, and the future of society with Dwarkesh Patel. Cowen argues that the existence of sex is the most pessimistic thing there is. He also suggests that the best talent scouts are not themselves super talented. The conversation explores the role of education, the nature of intelligence, and the impact of cultural and demographic shifts on talent and innovation.
key_highlights: The existence of sex is the most pessimistic thing there is
Talent scouts are not necessarily super talented
Wokeness is not a result of colleges
The best moral instruction is the teacher being a good person
Europe is less woke than the United States
The best talent is geographically clustered
episodes.quotes: "So the existence of sex is the most pessimistic thing there is. I find that ironic. So I'm not the pessimist, sex is, right?"

episodes.title: Charles C. Mann - Americas Before Columbus & Scientific Wizardry
episodes.guest_title: Author of 1491: New Revelations of the Americas Before Columbus
episodes.summary: Charles Mann discusses the history of the Americas before Columbus, emphasizing the sophisticated civilizations that existed and the ecological impact of their interaction with Europeans. He argues that the average podcast interviewer is more knowledgeable than the average mainstream journalist, and that elites throughout history have often colluded to exploit the poor. He also touches on the role of technology in addressing environmental dilemmas, and the importance of recognizing the limits of natural systems.
key_highlights: Podcast interviewers are often more knowledgeable than mainstream journalists
Elites often collude to exploit the poor
Science and technology can help produce our way out of environmental dilemmas
Natural systems are governed by laws and have carrying capacities
There was a huge epidemiological disparity between the Eastern and Western Hemispheres
episodes.quotes: The average podcast interviewer is far more knowledgeable and thoughtful than the average mainstream journalist interviewer. I just find that amazing!

episodes.title: Austin Vernon - Energy Superabundance, Starship Missiles, & Finding Alpha
episodes.summary: Austin Vernon discusses the potential of Starship as a kinetic weapon, arguing that its low cost could make it a more effective means of delivering mass to a target than conventional ballistic missiles. He also touches on the challenges of achieving true efficiency in software development, drawing parallels to the Toyota Production System and the need for a holistic, vertically integrated approach. He also touches on the difficulty of achieving true efficiency in software development.
key_highlights: Starship's low cost could make it a more effective kinetic weapon than conventional ballistic missiles
The blockchain could reimagine enterprise software
The internet knows only one JPEG of Austin Vernon
The Toyota Production System has a lot to teach software development
Vertical integration is key to avoiding local optimization in complex systems
Russia's fancy parade-style weapons are not as effective as cheaper alternatives
episodes.quotes: "The blockchain application which excites me personally the most is reimagining enterprise software."

episodes.title: Steve Hsu - Intelligence, Embryo Selection, & The Future of Humanity
episodes.summary: Steve Hsu discusses the science behind genomic prediction, particularly in the context of embryo selection. He explains how the additive nature of genetic traits makes prediction possible, and how that knowledge can be used to select for desirable traits. He also touches on the ethical implications of embryo selection and the potential for AI to play a role in this process.
key_highlights: The future AI will find papers proving genomics revolution possible
Most traits are controlled by thousands of disjoint variants
It's possible to engineer humans to live much longer
The rate of evolution is dominated by additive variance
The concept of a gym was incomprehensible in the early 20th century
episodes.quotes: You have to divorce your, you have to be totally rational about it. You're never going to see that girl again. This is your one interaction with her. Do what you have to do. And if she for some reason doesn't like you, just go to the next one.

episodes.title: Will MacAskill - Longtermism, Effective Altruism, History, & Technology
episodes.summary: Will MacAskill argues that moral progress is not guaranteed, and that we should actively shape the future by identifying and promoting the right values. He suggests that the Industrial Revolution may have happened in India had different values prevailed. He also argues that academia avoids tackling big, interdisciplinary moral questions, and that long reflection is necessary before locking in values.
key_highlights: Moral progress is not guaranteed, we should actively shape the future with the right values
Industrial Revolution in India may have led to different values
Academia avoids tackling big, interdisciplinary moral questions
Long reflection is necessary before locking in values
Effective Altruism's success is somewhat contingent
Modern Western values aren't necessarily that good
episodes.quotes: "You know, we should not think we're at the end of moral progress and we should not think, oh, we should lock in the kind of Western values we have now."

episodes.title: Joseph Carlsmith - Utopia, AI, & Infinite Ethics
episodes.summary: Joseph Carlsmith discusses the concept of utopia, defining it as a profoundly better future, and explores the ethical implications of infinite worlds. He argues that while many believe in the possibility of a much better world, they often underestimate the potential for radical improvement. He also highlights the importance of considering the long-term effects of our actions, even if they seem insignificant in the present, and the need for a more nuanced understanding of moral patienthood.
key_highlights: Utopia is defined as a profoundly better future that is actually possible
Infinite ethics grapples with how to act with respect to infinite worlds
The future is too complex to model with our limited minds
Lossy abstractions are necessary when modeling the future
A middle ground exists between ignoring AI and becoming a Jain
The sooner something is, the more you need to plan for it
episodes.quotes: "Utopia for me just means a profoundly better future, and I think it's important because I think it's just actually possible. I just think it's actually something that we could do."

episodes.title: Fin Moorhouse - Longtermism, Space, & Entrepreneurship
episodes.guest_title: Research Scholar, Future of Humanity Institute, Oxford University
episodes.summary: Fin Moorhouse discusses the scope and potential of Effective Altruism (EA), emphasizing the importance of being antifragile with respect to being wrong. He argues that EA should prioritize making the world go well over personal financial gain, and that the diminishing returns of money are less relevant when focusing on global impact. Moorhouse also touches on the implications of the many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics for decision-making and the importance of criticism within EA.
key_highlights: The scope for what we could achieve is extraordinarily large
Anti-fragility with respect to being wrong is a key property
Diminishing returns of money are less relevant when focusing on global impact
The world is an extremely big place
The question of 'what should we do?' is important
The importance of being receptive to criticism
episodes.quotes: If you have like 10 million in the bank and you make another 10 million, does your life get twice as good? Obviously not, right. If on the other hand, you just care about like making the world go well, then the world's an extremely big place.

episodes.title: Alexander Mikaberidze - Napoleon, War, Progress, and Global Order
episodes.guest_title: Professor of History, Louisiana State University
episodes.summary: Alexander Mikaberidze argues that Napoleon was not a child of the revolution, but the last of the enlightened despots, who did not support the radicalism of revolution. He argues that the Napoleonic Wars were a major factor in the radicalization of the revolution, and that the revolution would not have radicalized as rapidly or to the extent that it did without the war. He also argues that the French Revolution and Napoleon's reign had a lasting impact on the concept of the nation-state, and that the quest for equality is a key theme in European history.
key_highlights: Napoleon was a product of revolutionary circumstances, but not necessarily a revolutionary himself
War made things possible that peacetime would have made unthinkable
The Napoleonic Code enforced a new legal framework
The French Revolution was about a quest for equality
The French Empire collapsed because it lacked the resources of the British Empire
The Napoleonic Wars pioneered new mechanisms for dealing with defeated powers
episodes.quotes: "Napoleon is not necessarily the child of revolution. To me, he's the last of the enlightened despots."

episodes.title: Sam Bankman-Fried - Crypto, FTX, Altruism, & Leadership
episodes.summary: Sam Bankman-Fried discusses the messiness of rapid company growth, the importance of fighting through unfairness, and the need for a community that accepts failure. He also touches on the limitations of EA's narrow focus, the likelihood of stablecoins and blockchains becoming settlement mechanisms, and the importance of having a team that can adapt to new challenges. SBF also suggests that the richest people in the world will be utilitarian philanthropists.
key_highlights: Rapid company growth leads to internal mess
EA can be too narrow-minded and specific
Stablecoins becoming an important settlement mechanism is likely
Blockchain becoming a settlement mechanism seems likely
Collateral clearing mechanism seems decently likely
Success requires fighting through unfairness
episodes.quotes: "When you start a company, there's enormous amounts of shit that looks like that. Things that are like dumb or annoying or broken or unfair or not how the world should work. But it's how the world does work."

episodes.title: Agustin Lebron - Trading, Crypto, and Adverse Selection
episodes.guest_title: Author of The Laws of Trading and Founder, Stealth
episodes.summary: Agustin Lebron argues that software development is fundamentally an exercise in sociology, emphasizing the importance of culture and conventions in building software. He suggests that financial markets may be overvalued, with finance representing too large a percentage of GDP. He also argues that the best way to hire talent is to select for people who enjoy the process of learning and problem-solving, rather than those solely motivated by financial gain.
key_highlights: Software development is fundamentally an exercise in sociology
Finance may be too large a fraction of GDP
The Straussian reading of 'Laws of Trading' is: don't trade
The best motivation for traders is winning the game
Trading firms provide a service to the world
Trading is labor intensive, value is in the seat
It's important to have many opportunities to learn things and try things
episodes.quotes: "Software development is fundamentally an exercise in sociology. You should think of yourself as having many opportunities to learn things and try things and do things."

episodes.title: Gwynne Shotwell, President and COO of SpaceX
episodes.summary: Gwynne Shotwell discusses the importance of risk-taking and feedback in building a successful space exploration company. She shares the story of how she almost turned down the opportunity to be president of SpaceX, and how a pre-launch ritual of placing "Scotland" notes in her shoes helps her stay grounded. Shotwell emphasizes the importance of building a production system to build rockets, much like the automotive industry.
key_highlights: Take risks in your career
Always aim high
Feedback is a gift
Focus on the task at hand
Be honest with yourself
Be objective and don't personalize
You can't be afraid to make mistakes
episodes.quotes: We are SpaceX because of you. And if you ever feel uncomfortable at work, please call me.

episodes.title: Anne Wojcicki, Co-founder and CEO of 23andMe
episodes.summary: Anne Wojcicki discusses the importance of transparency and choice in healthcare, and how the pandemic accelerated the trend of virtual care. She shares that 23andMe has always prioritized customer consent and data privacy, and had to pivot when the FDA challenged their initial approach. She emphasizes that it's important to have a supportive company culture where people can be themselves and learn from each other, and that all experiences add up.
key_highlights: Make genetics relatable and fun
People want scientific information that's fun
Make genetics accessible to everyone
Don't be afraid to pivot
Be authentic
Prioritize transparency and choice
Don't treat people like human subjects
episodes.quotes: “People want scientific information that’s like kind of fun, kind of the cocktail party conversation, and something that’s interesting.”

episodes.title: Ananyo Bhattacharya - John von Neumann, Jewish Genius, and Nuclear War
episodes.summary: Ananyo Bhattacharya discusses the life and legacy of John von Neumann, emphasizing his unique combination of mathematical genius and practical engagement with real-world problems. He highlights von Neumann's early predictions of the Holocaust and nuclear war, his contributions to the Manhattan Project, and his later work on game theory and computing. Bhattacharya argues that von Neumann's involvement in applied problems, while controversial, was essential to his intellectual development.
key_highlights: Von Neumann predicted the Holocaust and nuclear war years in advance
He was a mathematical genius who learned calculus by age eight
Von Neumann's game theory work was rooted in a desire to understand conflict and cooperation
His work was essential to the development of the atomic bomb
He was deeply uncomfortable with authoritarianism
Von Neumann's engagement with real-world problems was essential to his intellectual development
episodes.quotes: "There is no Hungarian phenomenon. The only phenomenon that needs explaining is Johnny von Neumann."

episodes.title: Dara Treseder, SVP, Global Head of Marketing, Communications & Membership of Peloton
episodes.guest_title: SVP, Global Head of Marketing, Communications & Membership of Peloton
episodes.summary: Dara Treseder discusses her career journey, from her early days in finance to her current role at Peloton. She emphasizes the importance of having a strong support system, particularly for women of color in leadership positions. Dara shares her approach to leadership, which is rooted in her Christian faith and values of love, acceptance, respect, and inclusion. She also shares a story about her daughter, which highlights the importance of making sure that people understand who you are and what you stand for.
key_highlights: Ambition with contentment is a key guiding principle
Be intentional about professional development
Protect your peace
People want to know who you are
Be authentic to your purpose
No balance, only harmony
Show up even when it's hard
episodes.quotes: No matter how high you are, be humble. And no matter how low you are, be hopeful. Because things are never, you're never as amazing as I think I am, and I'm never as terrible as I think I am, right? There's there's that all that space in between.

episodes.title: Tony Elumelu, founder of The Tony Elumelu Foundation
episodes.summary: Tony Elumelu discusses the importance of entrepreneurship in Africa, drawing from his experience building a pan-African investment firm and his foundation's work supporting African entrepreneurs. He emphasizes the need for a unique approach to capitalism that prioritizes social impact and shared prosperity. Elumelu shares his belief that the private sector has a crucial role to play in Africa's development, and that good governance and accountability are essential for success.
key_highlights: Entrepreneurship is in my DNA; I preach, encourage, and catalyze it
Savings are the foundation of capital formation
Africa needs a unique approach to capitalism
Prioritize people and culture for successful mergers
Good governance and accountability are essential for success
Health is wealth
The private sector must help develop the continent
episodes.quotes: Entrepreneurship is in my DNA. I preach entrepreneurship, I encourage entrepreneurship, I also try to catalyze more entrepreneurs in Africa.

episodes.title: Manifold Markets Founder - Predictions Markets & Revolutionizing Governance
episodes.summary: Stephen Grugett, CEO of Manifold Markets, discusses the design and future of prediction markets. He argues that play money can work because people are driven more by status and competitiveness than greed. He also contends that prediction markets can provide a public good by aggregating information that society wouldn't otherwise be able to produce. Finally, he notes that the best way to improve as a predictor is to clarify and crystallize one's views about the world.
key_highlights: Play money can work because people are driven more by status and competitiveness than greed
Prediction markets can provide a public good
People don't want to know the answers to a lot of questions
Top predictors tend to have experience and hone their skills
You can't bet on the apocalypse because you won't be there to collect your winnings
It's hard to operationalize success into one thing that a market can measure
episodes.quotes: "People are driven more by status and competitiveness than greed... We see ourselves as a social game where people can come on to hone their skills at predicting."

episodes.title: David Vélez, Founder and CEO of Nubank
episodes.summary: David Vélez shares his journey from Colombia to founding Nubank, the world's largest digital bank. He emphasizes the importance of a family of entrepreneurs in shaping his problem-solving mindset. He discusses the importance of culture, especially in the early stages of a company, and how it drives motivation. Vélez also reflects on the need to balance reinvention with leveraging existing knowledge, and the importance of aligning incentives with customers.
key_highlights: Entrepreneurial family instilled problem-solving mindset
Importance of hustle in small businesses
Questioning rules that don't make sense
Culture is the driving force for motivation
Balance reinvention with leveraging existing knowledge
Align incentives with customers
Prioritize impact per unit of time
episodes.quotes: Consumers don't come to you because of your products, consumers come to you because of the culture. They are consumers of culture, they're not consumers of products.

episodes.title: Sundar Pichai, CEO of Google and Alphabet
episodes.summary: Sundar Pichai shares his journey from growing up in India with limited tech access to leading Google. He emphasizes the importance of being authentic as a leader, not trying to fit someone else's mold. Pichai highlights Google's commitment to sustainability, aiming to be carbon-free by 2030, and the company's focus on digital skilling to empower underserved communities. He underscores the value of employee input and the need for thoughtful regulation in the tech industry.
key_highlights: Don't read about yourself online
Technology done correctly enables real-world interactions
Be authentic and the best leader you can be
Value effort, not just outcomes
Acknowledge, own, and commit to making things better
Transparency doesn't equal context
Tech is going to be a regulated industry
episodes.quotes: I think the essential struggle of humanity with every technology is harnessing it so that it benefits society. Don't try to be in someone else's mold.

episodes.title: Jimmy Soni - Peter Thiel, Elon Musk, and the Paypal Mafia
episodes.guest_title: Author of "The Founders: The Story of PayPal and the Entrepreneurs Who Shaped Silicon Valley"
episodes.summary: Jimmy Soni discusses the PayPal Mafia, contrasting the PayPal founders with the scientists at Bell Labs. He argues that the PayPal founders' success was due to the intense competition and pressure they faced, which forced them to innovate. Soni also argues that the PayPal story is one of failure, which is a good thing because it makes it okay for other people to do the kind of work they do.
key_highlights: PayPal's success was due to intense competition, not a comfortable monopoly
The PayPal story is one of failure, which is a good thing
The right person to answer the Bell Labs question is John Gertner
Shannon worked on information theory as a side hustle
It's important to be able to see potential in people
episodes.quotes: "If we pretend that they're not superheroes for a minute, if we bring them back down to earth, if we tell the stories of like accidentally hot-swapping hard drives and almost losing the company's entire source code, it makes it okay for many other people to do the kinds of work that they do."

episodes.title: Bryan Caplan - Labor Econ, Poverty, & Mental Illness
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics, George Mason University
episodes.summary: Bryan Caplan and Dwarkesh Patel discuss the economics of labor, poverty, and mental illness. Caplan argues that a large percentage of the working-age population is zero or negatively productive, and that government redistribution disincentivizes work. He also suggests that cultural norms, rather than inherent productivity differences, explain wage disparities. They discuss the success sequence, the role of impulse control, and the challenges of measuring productivity in the modern economy.
key_highlights: 3% of working-age population has zero/negative productivity
Mismatch of job to person is a common misunderstanding
Working moms are a large share of non-workers
Government redistribution disincentivizes work
Cultural norms explain wage disparities
The success sequence is a recipe to avoid poverty
Low sexual impulse control is the root of other problems
episodes.quotes: "The fact that someone has zero productivity or negative productivity at the job they're doing doesn't show that they are a zero or negative productivity person."

episodes.title: Jessica Alba, founder and Chief Creative Officer of The Honest Company
episodes.guest_title: Founder and Chief Creative Officer, The Honest Company
episodes.summary: Jessica Alba shares the story of how her health challenges as a child and her experience as a mother led her to found The Honest Company. She describes how she identified a need for affordable, eco-friendly products for children and families, and the challenges she faced in convincing investors that a values-driven company could be successful. She emphasizes the importance of authenticity and knowing your audience, and how she had to distill her vision to appeal to a specific market.
key_highlights: Trust your gut and intuition
Know your audience and their capacity
Make it entertaining
Don't be afraid to hand over the baton
Be humble
Be honest with yourself
Focus on the long-term
episodes.quotes: I think it really was for me filling a need and finding a real opportunity to show up for not just myself but for many people in a way that I think I was seeing that companies that make products that really interact with you every single day and could have an effect on your health and well-being over time.

episodes.title: Richard Hanania - Foreign Policy, Fertility, and Experts
episodes.guest_title: President, Center for the Study of Partisanship and Ideology
episodes.summary: Richard Hanania argues that the unitary actor model of foreign policy is naive, as it assumes states are rational actors pursuing common goals. He proposes a public choice theory model, where foreign policy is driven by competing interests within the state. Hanania also discusses the decline of war, the role of experts, and the cultural factors influencing fertility rates, suggesting that government attempts to influence fertility often backfire.
key_highlights: Academics study foreign policy assuming states are rational actors, but this is a naive oversimplification
Grant strategy is a corollary of the rational actor model
Diplomatic, economic, and military means are assumed to be put towards the same goals
A public choice model of foreign policy works better than the rational actor model
The decline of war may be bad because it allows political institutions to become sclerotic
Libertarianism is being made a scapegoat for negative trends
The US sees Russia as a potential regime change target
episodes.quotes: "The idea is that diplomatic, economic, and military means tend to be put towards the same goals. They're all basically moving in the same direction."

episodes.title: Hamdi Ulukaya, CEO and founder of Chobani
episodes.summary: Hamdi Ulukaya shares his journey from a shepherd's son to the founder of Chobani, emphasizing the importance of authenticity and community. He recounts how a boring drive led to the name Chobani, and how his upbringing shaped his values. Ulukaya highlights the power of business to create positive social impact, particularly for refugees, and encourages entrepreneurs to embrace their unique backgrounds and build genuine connections.
key_highlights: Authenticity: Stay true to your background and values
Business can create positive social impact
Community is essential for success
Embrace the culture of the place you are in
The importance of a strong supply chain
Don't be afraid to be angry
The importance of human connection
episodes.quotes: "The true power of business comes when you see that it has a direct impact on people's lives."

episodes.title: David Deutsch - AI, America, Fun, & Bayes
episodes.summary: David Deutsch argues that AGI will not surpass human intelligence because human brains are Turing complete, and any cognitive task humans can perform, AGIs can too. He also critiques empiricism as a methodology for science, arguing that it is not the best way to generate explanations. He also argues that the American system of government is flawed because it diffuses responsibility, making it difficult to assign blame.
key_highlights: AGI will not surpass human intelligence because human brains are Turing complete
AGIs will be capable of all the same types of cognition as humans
The reason is twofold: computation hardware and software
Our brains are Turing complete bits of hardware
The reason is that our brains are Turing complete
Empiricism is not the best way to generate explanations
The American system of government is flawed because it diffuses responsibility
episodes.quotes: "The reason is twofold. And uh, one half is about computation, hardware, computation hardware, and the other is about software."

episodes.title: Malala Yousafzai, Global Activist for Girls’ Education and Co-Founder of the Malala Fund
episodes.guest_title: Cofounder and Board Member, Malala Fund
episodes.summary: Malala Yousafzai discusses her journey as an activist for girls' education, highlighting the importance of staying true to one's values and the power of local leadership. She emphasizes the need for a holistic approach to education, encompassing not just academics but also life skills and community engagement. Malala stresses the importance of staying optimistic and ambitious, even in the face of adversity, and encourages everyone to find their own way to contribute to positive change.
key_highlights: Stay true to your values and speak your truth
Inaction has worse consequences than action
Education is a right, not a privilege
Support local activists and their solutions
Remain optimistic and ambitious in your goals
Understand the importance of community engagement
The world is more complex than social media
episodes.quotes: “The Taliban tried to silence me, but they made a huge mistake, because I'm in a position where I can not only speak about my right to education and girls' in Swat Valley, but I can speak out for girls globally.”

episodes.title: Steve Kerr,  Head Coach of the Golden State Warriors
episodes.summary: Steve Kerr discusses his leadership style, which he likens to raising his children: giving them a lot of rope, but holding them accountable. He emphasizes the importance of building a culture of joy and compassion, and being authentic to one's values. Kerr shares a story about a rookie on the Portland Trailblazers asking for time off at Christmas, which highlights the importance of understanding the challenges of others.
key_highlights: Leading a team like raising children: give rope, but hold accountable
Importance of joy, compassion, and mindfulness
Be authentic to your values
Balance work and life
Proximity to people you're trying to help
Players should feel ownership
It's about the journey, not just the destination
episodes.quotes: I lead my team a little bit like I raised my children… we always sort of gave them a lot of rope, but they kind of knew they were responsible for that freedom.

episodes.title: Byrne Hobart - Optionality, Stagnation, and Secret Societies
episodes.summary: Byrne Hobart discusses the application of financial concepts to understanding human behavior, arguing that many decisions can be boiled down to expected value, optionality, and volatility. He argues that a focus on optionality can lead to a neglect of conscientiousness, which is necessary for solving complex problems. Hobart also discusses the importance of understanding the incentives of institutions and the role of randomness in outcomes.
key_highlights: Financial concepts like optionality can be usefully applied in many contexts
Models are useful, but you must be aware of their deficiencies
Coordination is a hard problem when solving complicated problems
Institutions often have mandates that are not to solve problems
It is hard to keep secrets alive
The US has not had the wealth drawdowns that other countries have experienced
episodes.quotes: "Coordination is it's just a really hard problem to to solve it's a hard problem to think about... a lot of times the institutional mandate is not to solve that problem, it's something else"

episodes.title: David Friedman - Dating Markets, Legal Systems, Bitcoin, and Automation
episodes.summary: Friedman discusses the dating market, arguing that it is inefficient because individuals are not substitutable, and potential dates have different tastes. He also argues that legalizing polygyny would benefit women by raising the price of a wife. He also discusses the difficulty of enforcing contracts in a world of strong privacy, and the importance of Schelling points in coordinating behavior.
key_highlights: Online dating is inefficient because it is difficult to sort people by taste
Legalizing polygyny would benefit women by raising the price of a wife
The effect of polygyny is the opposite of what people expect
Search costs are central to the dating problem
The marriage market is not a competitive market
The effect of polyandry is the opposite of what people expect
The effect of polygyny is to benefit women and harm men
episodes.quotes: If men can have multiple wives, that's going to bid up the price of a wife. It means that some men can in effect go on the market and try to bid for two wives instead of one.

episodes.title: Sarah Fitz-Claridge - Taking Children Seriously | The Lunar Society #15
episodes.summary: Sarah Fitz-Claridge discusses her educational philosophy of "Taking Children Seriously," which emphasizes treating children as rational, creative individuals capable of making their own choices. She argues against coercion in education and parenting, advocating for creating consent and solving problems collaboratively. She contends that traditional schooling and authoritarian parenting stifle children's natural curiosity and problem-solving abilities, leading to resentment and a lack of self-trust.
key_highlights: Children should learn what they want, what interests them
Children should learn how to solve problems
Institutionalization for 12 years is not the way to learn
Coercion decides issues under an irrational institution
Might makes right is false
Children are creative and rational from birth
episodes.quotes: "What children should be learning is what they want, is what interests them, is how to solve problems. They don't learn that by being institutionalized for 12 years and bossed about by an authoritarian teacher who doesn't know very much."

episodes.title: Michael Huemer - Anarchy, Capitalism, and Progress
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Philosophy, University of Colorado
episodes.summary: Michael Huemer discusses the problem of political authority, arguing that the state's claim to authority is not self-evident and requires justification. He argues that common sense morality and ethical intuitions should be applied to the state. Huemer suggests that the state's authority is often based on a combination of fear, Stockholm syndrome, and a bias towards the status quo. He also discusses the potential for technology to drive moral progress, particularly in areas like animal welfare.
key_highlights: The state's claim to authority is not self-evident and requires justification
Common sense morality should be applied to the state
The state's authority is based on fear, Stockholm syndrome, and status quo bias
Moral progress is driven by a small number of people
Technology can drive moral progress, especially in animal welfare
episodes.quotes: "The law could be things that you're not already obligated to do."

episodes.title: Thasunda Duckett, President and CEO, TIAA
episodes.summary: Thasunda Duckett shares her journey from a modest upbringing to leading major financial institutions. She emphasizes the importance of staying true to one's character, even when it means taking risks. Duckett highlights the value of kindness and empathy in leadership, and how it can be a powerful force for positive change. She also stresses the importance of financial literacy and equity, particularly for underserved communities.
key_highlights: Rent your title, own your character
Mentorship is all around you
Extraordinary is kindness
Show up with your ownable assets
Be okay with not being okay
Don't water down your story
Be outcome-driven
episodes.quotes: I would rather take those lessons getting started so that when you level up, you're prepared because you've been tested.

episodes.title: Priscilla Chan, Cofounder & Co-CEO, Chan Zuckerberg Initiative
episodes.summary: Priscilla Chan discusses her family's journey as refugees from Vietnam to America, emphasizing the importance of gratitude and optimism. She shares her experience as a physician and the realization that systemic change is necessary to address health disparities. Chan highlights the importance of collaboration and understanding the context of people's lives to create meaningful impact. She emphasizes the need for clear communication and the courage to name problems, even when it's uncomfortable.
key_highlights: Be clear about the problem you're trying to solve
Understand the context of people's lives
Collaboration is key to solving complex problems
Don't be afraid to name the problem
You can't stop, you have to do better
It's a lifetime of work
Have faith things will improve
episodes.quotes: If you hide, you're powerless. If you name what's hard, if you name why you're different, if you name your story, that gives you power.

episodes.title: Bozoma Saint John, Global Chief Marketing Officer, Netflix
episodes.summary: Bozoma Saint John discusses her career journey, starting at Spike Lee's advertising agency and moving through senior roles at Pepsi, Apple, Uber, and now Netflix. She emphasizes the importance of authenticity, diversity, and intentionality in leadership. Saint John shares her personal experiences with grief and loss, highlighting how these challenges have shaped her perspective and approach to life and work.
key_highlights: Be your authentic self
Have integrity in your intentions
Use entertainment to normalize experiences
Don't be afraid to speak up
Live deeply and with urgency
Be impatient with mediocrity
episodes.quotes: “I’m really just trying to do some good work, some fun work every single day. That is my mission, which is that I really love people. I love culture. I’m not ashamed of that.”

episodes.title: Sonia Syngal, MSE ’95, CEO of Gap Inc.
episodes.summary: Sonia Syngal, CEO of Gap Inc., discusses her career journey, from her engineering background to leading a global fashion company. She emphasizes the importance of knowing oneself, getting hands dirty, and building a strong team with diverse perspectives. Syngal shares her approach to navigating the challenges of leading Gap Inc. during the pandemic, focusing on clear communication, empathy, and a commitment to the company's purpose of inclusivity.
key_highlights: Passion for fashion started at a young age due to fit issues
Importance of being highly functional in any room
Connecting with humans at a human level unlocks value
Boldness and being right are key to success
People are inherently capable and want to help
Know yourself and get your hands dirty
episodes.quotes: “It’s cliché but get in the muck and follow your passion and know that you can change the world. Watching others or helping others do it is not nearly as fun.”

episodes.title: Tony Xu, MBA ’13, Cofounder and CEO, DoorDash
episodes.summary: Tony Xu, DoorDash's cofounder and CEO, discusses the importance of finding something you are obsessive about to fuel your entrepreneurial journey. He emphasizes the value of transparency within a company and the need to build a culture that supports it. He also shares the importance of focusing on inputs rather than outcomes, and of building systems to scale a company's culture.
key_highlights: Find something you are obsessive about
Transparency is crucial for company culture
Focus on inputs, not just outcomes
Build systems to scale your culture
Plan for variance, both up and down
episodes.quotes: “If you put your mind to whatever new thing it is, and you think you have nothing to lose, there’s a lot of upside.”

episodes.title: Stacy Brown-Philpot, MBA ’02, Founding Member, SB Opportunity Fund, and Former CEO, TaskRabbit
episodes.guest_title: Founding Member, SB Opportunity Fund, and Former CEO, TaskRabbit
episodes.summary: Stacy Brown-Philpot shares her journey from investment banking to leading TaskRabbit, emphasizing the importance of community and authenticity. She discusses her decision to leave Google to lead TaskRabbit, driven by a desire to impact everyday people's lives. Stacy highlights the importance of building a team of people better than you and the need to be vulnerable to build deeper connections. She also emphasizes the importance of knowing yourself and bringing your authentic self to work.
key_highlights: Authenticity is key to building deeper connections
Prioritize community and values in business decisions
Listen to your gut and trust your instincts
Create economic opportunities for everyday people
Embrace change and be willing to learn
Surround yourself with people better than you
Integrity is the ultimate measure of a leader
episodes.quotes: It doesn't really matter what you said, what matters is what they heard and how they interpreted what you said.

episodes.title: Uncle Bob - The Long Reach of Code, Automating Programming, and Developing Coding Talent
episodes.guest_title: Software Engineer and Author, Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob)
episodes.summary: Uncle Bob argues that AI will not automate programming because programming fundamentally requires human sentience to specify the program's purpose. He contends that programmers are detail managers, filling in the gaps left by business users. He also suggests that the industry's focus on ethics and standards is misplaced, and that the real issue is the lack of a formal licensing body to ensure competence. He concludes that the best way to train programmers is through mentorship and apprenticeship.
key_highlights: AI cannot automate programming because it lacks human sentience to specify the program's purpose
Programmers are detail managers, filling in gaps left by business users
Human intuition is needed to fill in the blanks of business specifications
The industry's focus on ethics is misplaced; competence is the real issue
A programming guild structure with licensing could improve the industry
The best way to train programmers is through mentorship and apprenticeship
episodes.quotes: "In order to finally replicate programming, that machine would have to have human sentience, and we are very, very far away from that kind of a machine."

episodes.title: Scott Aaronson - Quantum Computing, Complexity, and Creativity
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Computer Science, University of Texas at Austin
episodes.summary: Scott Aaronson discusses his early life as a prodigy, his research on the Busy Beaver function, and the limitations of existing set theory. He also touches on the social dynamics of academia and the challenges of pursuing research outside of traditional academic institutions. Aaronson emphasizes the importance of imagination and the potential for new discoveries in quantum computing.
key_highlights: Becoming a world expert on a tiny problem
Quantum mechanics was not always the final word
The importance of having a good math teacher
The limitations of set theory
The importance of imagination in research
The value of being on the margins of academia
episodes.quotes: It really doesn't take that long to become the world expert on one particular tiny little problem. So try to become the world expert on something, you know, even something very, very narrow.

episodes.title: Eric Garcetti, Mayor of Los Angeles
episodes.summary: Mayor Garcetti discusses the importance of cities as laboratories for democracy and innovation. He emphasizes the need for leaders to be both humble and fearless, and to prioritize listening over speaking. Garcetti shares his approach to leadership, which involves balancing urgent needs with long-term vision, and leading with love. He also highlights the importance of rehumanizing people and fostering human connection in an increasingly virtual world.
key_highlights: Cities are laboratories for democracy and innovation
Balance urgent needs with long-term vision
Lead with love and empathy
Rehumanize people in a virtual world
Prioritize listening over speaking
Be humble but fearless
Build strong relationships with stakeholders
episodes.quotes: The word for city in Greek has the same root as what we now use for politics. People came to cities to engage with each other, and part of that obligation of citizenship was to engage in politics.

episodes.title: Scott Young - Ultralearning, The MIT Challenge
episodes.summary: Scott Young discusses the phenomenon of "miracle years" in the lives of outlier individuals like Newton and Einstein, arguing that it is largely a selection effect. He also discusses the importance of specialization, and the need to understand the specific goals of a learning project in order to optimize it. He contrasts this with the more general, broad-based learning that is often promoted, and argues that the two have different goals and outcomes.
key_highlights: Most people never have a year where they accomplish anything
Selection effect: smart people working on the right problem
Einstein's insights led to physics breakthroughs
Most people don't deserve to be on lists of geniuses
The importance of doing research before starting a project
Need to understand what success looks like
episodes.quotes: "Rewarding kind of a culture where you want to do kind of ambitious original things that um are kind of interesting and you don't know where they're going to lead."

episodes.title: Beth Ford, President and CEO, Land O' Lakes
episodes.guest_title: President and CEO, Land O' Lakes
episodes.summary: Beth Ford, President and CEO of Land O' Lakes, shares her journey from a working-class background to leading a Fortune 200 company. She emphasizes the importance of authenticity, empathy, and understanding the needs of others, particularly in rural communities. Ford discusses the challenges and opportunities in the agriculture industry, including sustainability, food security, and the digital divide. She highlights the need for innovative incentive structures to support farmers and promote biodiversity.
key_highlights: Calmness is contagious in crisis; pump the brakes, don't jerk the wheel
Prioritize people, not just profits, in leadership
Be a self-advocate and ask for what you want
Be aware of the digital divide and its impact
Seek out opportunities to enable others' success
You can't be a mind reader, so ask
The most important career decision is who you marry
episodes.quotes: If you want something, you ask for it. And otherwise, people aren't going to guess. Nobody, I promise you, cares more about your career than you do.

episodes.title: Charles Murray - Human Accomplishment and the Future of Liberty | The Lunar Society #10
episodes.guest_title: Political Scientist and Author, American Enterprise Institute
episodes.summary: Charles Murray discusses the nature of human accomplishment, the role of religion in Western Civilization, and the decline of social sciences. Murray argues that secular humanism lacks the staying power of religion and that the decline in religious adherence is correlated with a decline in societal values. He also discusses the importance of hard work and the cultural milieu in fostering human achievement.
key_highlights: Secular humanism lacks the staying power of religion
The importance of religion to Western civilization
Secularization is correlated with a decline in societal values
Hard work is a common theme among high achievers
The importance of cultural milieu in fostering human achievement
episodes.quotes: Secular humanism didn't have the staying power that it needs.

episodes.title: Alex Tabarrok - Prizes, Prices, and Public Goods
episodes.guest_title: Bartley J. Madden Chair of Economics at the Mercatus Center
episodes.summary: Alex Tabarrok discusses the Grand Innovation Prize, which aims to incentivize the rapid development of vaccines, diagnostics, and therapeutics. He argues that speed is crucial in pandemics and that existing incentives for companies are insufficient. Tabarrok also explores the Baumol effect, explaining why prices in stagnant sectors like education and healthcare rise due to productivity gains in progressive sectors, and how this affects skilled labor.
key_highlights: Speed is crucial in pandemics, existing incentives for companies are insufficient
Grand Innovation Prize is meant to incentivize quick vaccine production
Vaccine manufacturers are unwilling to build factories until vaccines are proven safe
Social incentives are needed to speed up vaccine production
Baumol effect explains rising prices in stagnant sectors
Vaccine manufacturers are unwilling to build factories until vaccines are proven safe
Vaccine manufacturers are unwilling to build factories until vaccines are proven safe
episodes.quotes: "We want to find a way of speeding up the incentives to produce a vaccine or a diagnostic or a therapeutic."

episodes.title: Rosalind Brewer, COO, Starbucks
episodes.summary: Rosalind Brewer shares her journey from a chemistry degree to leading Starbucks operations. She emphasizes the importance of integrity, building trust, and using one's voice to drive change. Brewer recounts a pivotal moment when she realized the need to bring her whole self to work, and how the company's response to the Philadelphia incident shaped her leadership. She encourages students to embrace their unique perspectives and to be a voice for change.
key_highlights: Integrity is a core value in leadership
Build trust with your team
Use your voice to drive change
Bring your whole self to work
Focus on impact, not just titles
Meet your employees where they are
Be an adept glass-cutter
episodes.quotes: "Those glass ceilings are real, but Roz Brewer has become an adept glass-cutter. She has walked the same road as many of you and with relentless determination."

episodes.title: Caleb Watney - America's Innovation Engine
episodes.guest_title: Director of Innovation Policy, Progressive Policy Institute
episodes.summary: Caleb Watney argues that America's innovation engine is slowing due to three trends: declining high-skill immigration, a weakening university system, and the erosion of industrial clusters. He contends that while online communication platforms can partially replicate the benefits of physical agglomeration, they cannot fully substitute for the spontaneous idea-sharing and cultural context that arise from in-person interactions. Watney suggests that the US should focus on policies that attract and retain top talent, promote basic research, and foster a culture of risk-taking and experimentation.
key_highlights: The US's innovation engine is slowing due to declining high-skill immigration
Remote work can't fully replace physical agglomeration for innovation
China's innovation strategy is talent acquisition, not just R&D spending
The H-1B visa program is poorly structured for attracting top talent
The US needs to promote a culture of risk-taking and experimentation
The government should make data more accessible to foster innovation
episodes.quotes: "When you have all of those people in a geographical region where they're running into each other spontaneously and they're sharing ideas and they're having long conversations...that they wouldn't otherwise be able to replicate simply online, that ends up leading to more creativity."

episodes.title: Robin Hanson - The Long View and The Elephant in the Brain
episodes.guest_title: Research Associate, Future of Humanity Institute, Oxford University
episodes.summary: Robin Hanson discusses the lack of long-term planning in society, arguing that it's not just a matter of short-sightedness, but a consequence of the fact that optimizing for the long term is often ineffectual or even counterproductive given the inherent unpredictability of the future. He suggests that the most powerful institutions today are not necessarily the ones that thought about the long-term future in the past, and that the true unit of selection is not the individual but the organization. Hanson also argues that the lack of clear signals about long-term orientation makes it difficult to identify and reward individuals who are truly committed to the future.
key_highlights: Optimizing for the long term is often ineffectual or even counterproductive due to unpredictability
The most powerful institutions aren't necessarily long-term planners
Individual genes can't save resources for themselves, only for the whole creature
Human discount rates are influenced by the fact that children only share half our genes
The most important thing is to promote the thing that embodies the long view
episodes.quotes: "If it's unpredictable, then optimizing should take that into account."

episodes.title: Jason Crawford - The Roots of Progress & the History of Technology
episodes.summary: Jason Crawford discusses the history of technology and the philosophy of progress. He argues that progress is not automatic and requires a conscious effort to protect and nurture it. He also notes that the linear model of innovation is oversimplified, as science and technology often influence each other in complex ways. Crawford emphasizes the importance of prescriptive optimism, which is the determination to act to create a better future, regardless of the odds.
key_highlights: Progress requires a conscious effort to protect and nurture it
The linear model of innovation is oversimplified
Science and technology influence each other in complex ways
Prescriptive optimism is key to creating a better future
Progress is not automatic or inevitable
Progress studies is a school of thought, not a new field
episodes.quotes: Progress is not automatic or inevitable, it doesn't just keep going with its own momentum, it can slow down, maybe it's running into friction, let's take a look at that.

episodes.title: Matjaž Leonardis - Science, Identity and Probability
episodes.summary: Leonardis argues that identifying too strongly with "science" can be counterproductive, as it can lead to a focus on whether one's work is "scientific" rather than on the pursuit of truth. He also discusses the Popper-Miller theorem, which shows that evidence always decreases the probability of the inductive part of a theory. He suggests that the Enlightenment narrative is a fiction, and that people are naturally interested in everything.
key_highlights: Identifying too strongly with "science" can be counterproductive
People are naturally interested in everything
The Enlightenment narrative is a fiction
The Popper-Miller theorem shows that evidence decreases the probability of the inductive part of a theory
There is no such thing as an unsolved problem
episodes.quotes: "Polymath is not something you learn to be, it's something you unlearn to be."

episodes.title: Tyler Cowen - The Great Reset
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics, George Mason University
episodes.summary: Tyler Cowen discusses his 2017 book, *The Complacent Class*, and its prediction of a "Great Reset" — a crisis that would disrupt the complacency of American society. He reflects on how the COVID-19 pandemic has fulfilled this prediction, highlighting the failure of the regulatory state and the executive branch to respond effectively. Cowen also explores the potential for a burst of dynamism in biomedicine and the challenges of maintaining progress given the inherent limitations of human nature and the ever-present threat of war.
key_highlights: The Great Reset arrived sooner than Cowen anticipated
The American regulatory state has failed to handle the pandemic
Biomedicine is poised for a burst of innovation
Complacency has finally caught up with us
Predictions should be conditional
The Fed and Supreme Court have been quite good
episodes.quotes: "At some point, our country will face an immediate crisis, and there won't be the resources or more fundamentally the flexibility to handle it."

episodes.title: Bryan Caplan - Nurturing Orphaned Ideas, Education, and UBI
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics, George Mason University
episodes.summary: Bryan Caplan discusses the importance of disagreeableness for intellectual progress, arguing that it's more effective than charm for truth-seeking. He defends his radical views on open borders and critiques the conventional wisdom surrounding education, arguing that it's largely signaling. Caplan also expresses skepticism about UBI, suggesting that it's based on a flawed understanding of the causes of poverty, which he attributes to bad government policies and individual irresponsibility.
key_highlights: Charm is useful for gaining power, but disagreeableness is more effective for truth-seeking
Most of what is learned in school is not broadly useful
Open borders are a moral imperative
Nuclear weapons have increased peace
The poor are often held in poverty by bad government policies
Personal responsibility is a major cause of poverty
episodes.quotes: "I've got enough controversial statements under my belt that people focus on that."

episodes.title: Stephen Curry, 2X MVP & 3X NBA Champion, Golden State Warriors
episodes.guest_title: 2X MVP & 3X NBA Champion, Golden State Warriors
episodes.summary: Stephen Curry shares his journey from Davidson College to the NBA, emphasizing the importance of family, faith, and a strong work ethic. He discusses the transition from a shaky Warriors team to a dynasty, highlighting the value of communication and humility in leadership. Curry also touches on his venture into media and investing, aiming to inspire the next generation through storytelling and creating opportunities for underserved communities.
key_highlights: Family provides a sense of foundation, no matter what life throws
Don't take yourself too seriously in your job
Embrace differences in personality on a team
Don't lose confidence, even with roadblocks
Stay educated and be authentic
Don't be afraid to share the spotlight
It's important to have a village around you
episodes.quotes: I'm only as good as the people around me. I try to deflect as much as possible to my team, because I know that I have power because of who I get to roll with every single day on the court.

episodes.title: David Solomon, CEO, Goldman Sachs
episodes.summary: David Solomon shares his career journey, emphasizing the importance of embracing change and the role of luck. He discusses Goldman Sachs' commitment to sustainability and financial inclusion, and the need for a more open, accessible culture. Solomon also reflects on his personal journey, including his passion for music and how it has shaped his leadership style.
key_highlights: Value people, capital, and technology
Embrace change and be adaptable
Focus on sustainability and financial inclusion
Prioritize a modern, open, and accessible culture
Balance work and personal life
Be authentic and bring your whole self to work
episodes.quotes: “If you wake up two-thirds of the time and you’re really happy with your job, you’re really lucky and you’re in a really good place.”

episodes.title: Rose Marcario, President & CEO, Patagonia
episodes.summary: Rose Marcario shares her journey from private equity to leading Patagonia, driven by a desire to align her work with her values. She describes Patagonia's commitment to environmentalism, its unique workforce policies like subsidized childcare, and its activism against environmental deregulation. Marcario emphasizes the importance of curiosity, self-examination, and taking a stand for what's right, even when it's difficult, and how business leaders should consider the planet as a key stakeholder.
key_highlights: Align work with personal values for a more meaningful career
Fearless leadership is essential for making difficult decisions
Prioritize quality over quantity in product development
Business must evolve to consider multiple stakeholders
Environmental activism is a core part of Patagonia's brand
Onsite childcare promotes gender parity in management
episodes.quotes: You don't get points for holding back.

episodes.title: Stewart Butterfield, Cofounder and CEO of Slack
episodes.summary: Stewart Butterfield shares his journey from philosophy student to tech entrepreneur, discussing the importance of starting early and the challenges of building a company with a new concept. He emphasizes the need for a strong team and a clear vision, and the importance of being thoughtful and considerate of others. He also shares his experience of selling Flickr to Yahoo and the lessons he learned from that experience.
key_highlights: Start early, even if you don't know where you're going
Build a strong team
Have a clear vision
Be thoughtful and considerate of others
Don't see people as instruments or obstacles
episodes.quotes: The real fundamental challenge of leadership is the same as the fundamental challenge of just being a human being.

episodes.title: Ruth Zukerman, Co-Founder of SoulCycle and Flywheel Sports
episodes.summary: Ruth Zukerman, co-founder of SoulCycle and Flywheel Sports, shares her journey from ballet to boutique fitness. She emphasizes the importance of customer service and creating a personal connection with clients, drawing parallels to her experience as a mother. Zukerman also discusses the challenges of partnership, the importance of self-knowledge, and the need to be vulnerable as a leader. She reflects on the impact of her difficult childhood and how therapy helped her discover her voice.
key_highlights: Dance as a way to express herself without words
Importance of therapy and self-discovery
Customer service and personal connection are key
Importance of knowing yourself when choosing partners
Empathy in moderation is important for leadership
Everything happens for a reason
No wrong decisions, just learning opportunities
episodes.quotes: It made sense that I got so addicted to dancing because it was it became my way of expressing myself without words, without a voice.

episodes.title: Satya Nadella, CEO of Microsoft
episodes.summary: Satya Nadella discusses his childhood in India, his parents' influence, and the importance of empathy in leadership. He shares a story about his high school cricket captain, who gave him the ball back after a bad performance, teaching him the value of confidence and humility. Nadella emphasizes that Microsoft's mission is to empower others, not to be cool. He highlights the importance of a growth mindset, and the need for leaders to create a culture that supports learning and innovation.
key_highlights: Microsoft's mission is to make others cool, not to be cool
Importance of confidence and humility in leadership
Team sports teach valuable lessons about leadership and collaboration
Need to balance growth with responsibility to society
The best leaders have high empathy and a growth mindset
episodes.quotes: The two of them, I think, growing up in Hyderabad in India in the late 70s, early 80s, gave me in spite of all of that a lot of confidence because to him, he felt like look, it's a marathon, you'll catch up, this is not that hard.

episodes.title: Marcos Galperin, MBA ’99, Founder and CEO of MercadoLibre, on Taking Risks and Being Resilient
episodes.summary: Marcos Galperin, CEO of MercadoLibre, shares his journey of building the company in Latin America. He emphasizes the importance of building strong relationships, having a long-term vision, and being resilient in the face of challenges. Galperin also highlights the significance of adapting to local market conditions and building a strong team with a shared sense of purpose.
key_highlights: Start with a success case to inspire others
Build relationships
Have a long-term vision
Be resilient
Adapt to local market conditions
Build a strong team
episodes.quotes: They got tired of telling me that my idea was not going to work.

episodes.title: Ruth Porat, CFO at Alphabet and Google
episodes.summary: Ruth Porat discusses her career path, from her early beginnings to Morgan Stanley to Alphabet and Google. She emphasizes the importance of education, which was instilled in her by her father, who escaped Austria during World War II. She also shares how she learned to be flexible and adjust as the world changes, and how she learned to use her voice at the right time. She stresses the importance of having a sponsor and being a sponsor to others.
key_highlights: Education is your passport for life
Gratitude to be in a country with freedom
Importance of having a career
Be flexible and adjust as the world changes
Use your voice at the right time
Importance of having a sponsor
Importance of analytical rigor
episodes.quotes: I would rather die an educated man. | The world is small and life is long. | Anchor everything in data and the rest will follow.

episodes.title: Ray Dalio, Founder and Chairman, Bridgewater Associates
episodes.summary: Ray Dalio shares his journey from a Long Island paperboy to the founder of Bridgewater Associates.
