He emphasizes the importance of thoughtful disagreement and radical transparency in building a successful organization. Dalio discusses his early investment in the stock market at age 12 and his decision to pursue commodity trading over equity trading. He also reflects on the importance of learning from failures and the value of having a strong sense of community.
key_highlights: Embrace thoughtful disagreement and radical transparency
Importance of having a strong sense of community
Learn from failures and turn them into principles
Value the process of personal evolution
Prioritize meaningful work and relationships
Balance audacity with humility
Understand history to anticipate future trends
episodes.quotes: “The greatest tragedy of mankind is people who hold opinions in their mind that they’re attached to that are wrong, that they could so easily stress test.”

episodes.title: Phil Knight, MBA ’62, Co-founder and Chairman Emeritus, Nike
episodes.summary: Phil Knight shares his journey building Nike, starting with his thesis at Stanford Business School about importing Japanese running shoes. He emphasizes the importance of having a niche and a passion for what you're building, and the need to adapt leadership styles as a company grows. Knight also reflects on the importance of a strong company culture, and how Nike's early culture was built on a shared belief and a willingness to challenge the status quo.
key_highlights: Find a niche that fits your passion
Good shoes are made by good factories
Hero leadership is not autocratic
You can't be afraid of offending people
You have to take a stand on something
It starts with the product
You need to believe
episodes.quotes: It doesn't matter how many people hate your brand, as long as enough people love it.

episodes.title: Michael Moritz, Partner, Sequoia Capital
episodes.summary: Michael Moritz shares his journey from a boy in Wales to a journalist and eventually a venture capitalist. He discusses the importance of having a long-term vision, and the value of a broad-based humanities education for success in technology. He also emphasizes the importance of listening and having a stable team for enduring organizations.
key_highlights: Wanting the ball to come in your direction
The importance of a broad-based humanities education
The importance of being a clear communicator
The importance of having a long-term vision
The importance of having a stable team
The importance of listening
episodes.quotes: The only thing that separated him and me on the rugby field was when he took to the field, he wanted the ball to come in his direction, and I of course wanted the reverse. I never wanted to see it come in my direction.

episodes.title: Dara Khosrowshahi, CEO, Uber
episodes.summary: Dara Khosrowshahi shares his journey from Iran to the US as a child, fleeing the revolution and seeking asylum. He emphasizes the importance of family and education in his assimilation. Khosrowshahi discusses his career path, highlighting the influence of mentor Barry Diller, and his decision to join Uber, driven by the opportunity to make a significant impact. He stresses the importance of transparency, customer obsession, and building a diverse leadership team.
key_highlights: Children assimilate more easily than adults
Family provides a constant source of support
Sports can be a tool for assimilation
Always answer "no" if you don't understand
Bet on people, not just companies
Go to the source for information
Success imprints more forcefully than failure
episodes.quotes: It's about doing something great. | I want your portfolio to be me. | There are very few times in your life when you can change the world. | We lost, they won, next.

episodes.title: Diane Von Fürstenberg, Founder & Chairman, DVF
episodes.summary: Diane Von Fürstenberg discusses her career, starting with an apprenticeship in a factory. She emphasizes the importance of being open to opportunities, even if they don't seem glamorous. She describes how getting pregnant and marrying a prince led to her starting her own business, and how her mother's strength and resilience influenced her. She also shares her thoughts on the importance of character, and how to be a strong and independent woman.
key_highlights: Be open to opportunities, even if they don't seem glamorous
You don't know what you're going to do
The dress made me
The most important relationship is with yourself
You can never be a victim
The power of color
Women have to stick together
episodes.quotes: The most important relationship you have is the one you have with yourself. You have yourself as a refuge, as a shelter, and no matter what happens, you always have yourself.

episodes.title: Oscar Munoz, President & CEO, United Airlines, on Knowing Yourself
episodes.summary: Oscar Munoz shares his journey from a working-class family in Southern California to leading United Airlines, emphasizing the importance of knowing oneself and building strong relationships. He recounts a pivotal moment with a high school counselor that led him to college and discusses the challenges of integrating diverse cultures after the United-Continental merger. Munoz stresses the value of a caring culture and the responsibility of leaders to advocate for social causes.
key_highlights: Environment and upbringing shape who you are as a person
Latino heritage emphasizes family, humility, and loyalty
Concept of entitlement was not strong in his world
Being first-generation undergrad brings big responsibility
Better to be principled than just profitable
Importance of building trust with employees
You have to know yourself, really
episodes.quotes: "I think environment, how you grew up, who you grow up with, has a meaningful impact on who you are as a person."

episodes.title: Ken Frazier, CEO of Merck & Co, Inc.
episodes.summary: Ken Frazier shares his journey from death penalty lawyer to CEO of Merck, emphasizing the role of opportunity and access in shaping his career. He recounts his experience with school desegregation, which instilled a deep awareness of the opportunity gap. Frazier stresses the importance of business leaders having a broader purpose beyond shareholder value, focusing on contributing to humanity. He also notes the importance of having a board with diverse perspectives and the need to be willing to challenge the status quo.
key_highlights: Opportunity gap exists in society
Social engineers engaged in school desegregation
Business education has real value
There is serendipity in everyone's career path
The importance of giving back to the community
There are two fundamental metrics: people helped and amount of help
Focus on what's right, not what people think
episodes.quotes: What other people think about you is none of your damn business.

episodes.title: Sara Blakely, Founder and CEO, Spanx
episodes.summary: Sara Blakely, founder of Spanx, shares her entrepreneurial journey from selling fax machines to building a billion-dollar company. She emphasizes the importance of intuition over data, the power of vulnerability, and the need to embrace humor in business. Blakely also discusses her commitment to empowering women and the importance of creating a culture where employees are not afraid to fail.
key_highlights: Start small, think big, scale fast
Be willing to look stupid
Trust your intuition
Hire your weaknesses
Use humor to move through pain
episodes.quotes: I feel like success just makes you more of who you already were. I feel like money and success just does that.

episodes.title: Stephen Harper, 22nd Prime Minister of Canada
episodes.summary: Former Canadian Prime Minister Stephen Harper discusses his career in politics and leadership. Harper explains how he never intended to be a politician, but rather became interested in policy and economics. He also shares that he made it a point to consult with every major sector of Canadian society when making trade deals. Harper also shares that he believes that leaders should be respected, and that any leader should love their country.
key_highlights: Never intended to be a politician
Importance of economic policy
Importance of public support
The need to respect opponents
Importance of understanding interests
Importance of incrementalism in policy
Importance of loving one's country
episodes.quotes: I always felt that I could lose more votes on answering that question than on any serious issue of policy. I will take any one of them. I'm desperate at this point.

episodes.title: Ginni Rometty, Chairman, President, and CEO of IBM
episodes.guest_title: Chairman, President, and CEO of IBM
episodes.summary: Ginni Rometty shares her career journey, starting in the automobile industry and moving to tech. She emphasizes the importance of passion in one's work and the need to continually reinvent oneself. Rometty discusses IBM's commitment to responsible stewardship of technology, particularly in areas like AI and blockchain, and the company's focus on skills development and inclusion.
key_highlights: Importance of passion in one's work
Continually reinventing oneself
Importance of purpose
Technology should be used with responsibility
Importance of skills development
Importance of inclusion and diversity
IBM's commitment to open innovation
episodes.quotes: "Life is too short. You've got to keep moving till you find what that is. I could only see what it wasn't."

episodes.title: Jeffrey Katzenberg, Co-founder and Former CEO of Dreamworks Animation
episodes.guest_title: Co-founder and Former CEO, Dreamworks Animation
episodes.summary: Jeffrey Katzenberg discusses his career journey, from working for Mayor John Lindsay to co-founding Dreamworks. He emphasizes the importance of adaptability and continuous learning, sharing his experience of starting over after being fired from Disney. Katzenberg highlights the value of strong partnerships, citing his collaborations with Steven Spielberg and David Geffen, and stresses the significance of courage in taking risks and pursuing original ideas in the entertainment industry.
key_highlights: Always find a way to bring laughter to people
Value your voice and your vote
Work smarter, not harder
Embrace failure and take risks
The truth will find you
Mentorship is invaluable
Be a builder and an operator
episodes.quotes: The credit you give yourself isn't worth having.

episodes.title: Tom Steyer, MBA '83, Founder and President of NextGen America
episodes.summary: Tom Steyer reflects on his time as a GSB student, his investment career, and his shift to environmentalism and political activism. He discusses the importance of investing in people and the need for broader democracy to address the challenges facing the United States. Steyer emphasizes the importance of having a meaningful life and contributing to society, and he shares his perspective on the role of money in politics.
key_highlights: Significance of Stanford Business School in terms of leadership in society
Interpersonal dynamics class was a chance to see how people interact
Competitive advantage on a national level is important
The importance of having a different perspective
Measure the results of your investments
The importance of internal communication
The importance of having a meaningful life
episodes.quotes: I think I have a different perspective than on the importance of this institution now. And obviously, I'm a lot older and have more to I at the time I brought you guys going full time and wouldn't have time for anything.

episodes.title: Chamath Palihapitiya, Founder and CEO Social Capital, on Money as an Instrument of Change
episodes.summary: Chamath Palihapitiya discusses his journey from a welfare-dependent childhood to becoming a successful venture capitalist. He emphasizes that money is an instrument of change, and that his goal is to amass capital to reallocate it towards his worldview. He encourages the audience to be good copiers, but to also develop their own unique perspectives. He also shares his belief that the current social media landscape is eroding society and that it is important to be aware of the psychological effects of these tools.
key_highlights: Money is a tool to empower oneself to do what one wants
Empathize with people who are like you
Don't covet money, use it to empower others
Be a good copier, but be honest
It's powerful to say "I don't know"
Be aware of the psychological effects of social media
Be anonymously generous
episodes.quotes: I feel very lucky because I don't think if you ask my sisters, they got to the same place that I did. Um, but I ended up not coveting it, and I found it to be something that I could use to really empower myself to do the things that I wanted to do.

episodes.title: Jamie Dimon, Chairman, President, and CEO of JPMorgan Chase
episodes.guest_title: Chairman, President, and CEO of JPMorgan Chase
episodes.summary: Jamie Dimon discusses his career path, leadership style, and views on the financial industry. He recounts his decision to leave Harvard Business School to work for Sandy Weill, emphasizing the value of being part of an organization. He stresses the importance of honesty, transparency, and strong character in leadership. Dimon also shares insights on the impact of technology on financial services and the need for competitive tax reform in the US.
key_highlights: Don't shave the truth; say the same thing to everyone
Bureaucracy and politics destroy companies
Character is a sine qua non
You can have it all, but not all at the same time
Be a patriot and help your country
You should be loyal to the institution
Be careful about who you put in charge
episodes.quotes: “When you meet men and women our age at JP Morgan today, what leadership characteristics are you looking for in them? Character is a sine qua non. And by character, I also mean that they tell the whole truth, nothing but the truth.”

episodes.title: Sheryl Sandberg, COO of Facebook, on Using Your Voice For Good
episodes.summary: Sheryl Sandberg shares her career journey, emphasizing the importance of taking opportunities on "rocket ships" and not planning a straight line. She discusses the value of building relationships and contributing to a company's mission. Sandberg also touches on the challenges women face in leadership, including societal biases and the need for supportive partners. She encourages the audience to be authentic and to use their voices to make a difference.
key_highlights: Don't plan a straight line in your career
Find the rocket ship that matters to you
Invest in yourself and others' success
Prioritize process over substance in new roles
Long-run dream, short-run plan
Be authentic, not packaged
Be ambitious for the women in your life
episodes.quotes: "When you get offered a seat on a rocket ship, you don't ask what seat."

episodes.title: Mary Barra, MBA '90, Chairman and CEO of General Motors, on Achieving Results with Integrity
episodes.summary: Mary Barra discusses the importance of understanding customer pain points to create value and new experiences. She emphasizes the need for a collaborative environment and the value of diverse perspectives. Barra also stresses the importance of transparency and acting with integrity, especially during a crisis. She encourages young people to broaden their studies and to find a job they love.
key_highlights: Put the customer in the center of how you look at things
People are still going to need to move from point A to point B
Focus on long product cycles and lean operations
Move from a hardware-centric world to a software-focused one
Understand how to create value for the customer
Provide new experiences that are better for the customer
To change behaviors, you have to be collaborative
episodes.quotes: "If you don't love your job, if you don't think you're being fairly compensated or challenged or it's not a fulfilling job, you need to act on that."

episodes.title: Eric Schmidt, Executive Chairman, Alphabet Inc.: Just Say "Yes"
episodes.summary: Eric Schmidt discusses the age of abundance, characterized by too much information and food, and abundant housing and transportation as a service. He emphasizes that while material needs are being met, conflicts around religion, nationalism, and personal issues persist. He encourages students to embrace personal and economic freedom, to be grateful for their opportunities, and to focus on serving others.  He advises students to say "yes" to new experiences, to be curious, and to develop their own luck.
key_highlights: Students are getting more impressive every year
Entering an age of abundance
Material things in great supply
Religious, nationalistic, personal conflicts will persist
Personal freedom and economic freedom are key
Turn off Twitter to focus
Brush your teeth, you'll need them
episodes.quotes: You really are the best. And my guess is that ten years from now, the students will be even stronger. That's extraordinary for the statement of what Stanford has been able to do.

episodes.title: Debra Lee, BET Chairman and CEO, on Leading a Company and a Community
episodes.summary: Debra Lee discusses her journey to becoming CEO of BET, emphasizing the importance of authenticity and community leadership. She recounts the challenges of transitioning from a legal background to a leadership role, highlighting the need to develop management skills and a clear vision. Lee shares anecdotes from her career, including producing a tribute concert for the Obamas at the White House, and emphasizes the need to respect, reflect, and elevate one's audience.
key_highlights: Authenticity and transparency are key to connecting with an audience
Leadership requires developing a clear vision and management skills
Community leadership is integral to a company's success
Respect, reflect, and elevate your audience
Embrace the power of media to educate and inspire
Don't be afraid to step off the fast track
Work should be fun
episodes.quotes: I'm still that fan little girl, but I'm also lucky enough to to have a company where we pay tribute to African Americans.

episodes.title: Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel on Policy-Making & Negotiation
episodes.summary: Former Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel discusses the importance of public service and the need for leaders to be both idealistic and ruthless. He shares his approach to negotiation, emphasizing the need to understand the other party's wins and to be flexible on the means while staying true to the end goal. Emanuel also reflects on the challenges of being a political leader, including the importance of taking political hits for the greater good and the need to build a strong team.
key_highlights: Chicago has the lowest cost of living of the 10 biggest US cities
Chicago is the #1 city for female entrepreneurs
Leaders need to be both idealistic and ruthless
Find out what the other side needs to declare victory
Don't confuse means with ends
Build a strong team
episodes.quotes: "I always try to start when we negotiate something, sit at the table with my staff and write out to me their wins and the ones we can afford to give them."

episodes.title: LinkedIn CEO Jeff Weiner on Compassionate Management
episodes.summary: Jeff Weiner discusses LinkedIn's response to the 2017 executive order on immigration, emphasizing employee safety and well-being. He shares LinkedIn's vision to create economic opportunity for every member of the global workforce, irrespective of origin or belief. He highlights the importance of balancing personal beliefs with representing the company, and shares how LinkedIn supports refugees in finding work, and has accelerated the launch of the Welcome Talent program in the US.
key_highlights: Well-being and safety of employees is paramount
Consider audience, constituencies, and objectives when addressing issues
Create economic opportunity for every member of the global workforce
Balance personal beliefs with representing the company as CEO
Innovation at the exclusion of strong building blocks can be detrimental
Managing compassionately involves understanding others' perspectives
Strive for a culture of transformation, not just innovation
episodes.quotes: This country, the United States, was founded on a first principle and belief that all people should have an unalienable right to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.

episodes.title: Marissa Mayer on Life and Leadership Lessons
episodes.summary: Marissa Mayer discusses her career pivot from pre-med to Symbolic Systems at Stanford, influenced by a pediatric heart surgeon who balanced medicine and teaching. She emphasizes the importance of surrounding oneself with smart people and taking risks, recounting her decision to join a small startup (Google) over more secure offers. Mayer also shares her approach to leadership, prioritizing the well-being of her children and the importance of being present in the moment.
key_highlights: Don't let criticism shape who you are
Surround yourself with interesting and smart people
Take risks, specialize later
Focus on the child's perspective
Make a choice and commit to making it great
Technology companies reinvent themselves through acquisitions
Find a way to cut through the noise
episodes.quotes: "I'm emptying my parents' retirement accounts... we're investing a lot here and I should do something at Stanford that's really unique to Stanford."

episodes.title: Marc Andreessen on Change, Constraints, and Curiosity
episodes.summary: Marc Andreessen discusses the importance of intellectual curiosity and lifelong learning, emphasizing the value of history and the need to challenge assumptions. He shares his perspective on the venture capital industry, highlighting the shift towards a more competitive landscape and the need for a "slugging percentage mentality." He also touches on the importance of creating a culture of curiosity and learning in organizations, and the challenges of balancing action with thoughtful reflection.
key_highlights: Venture capital offices should look like libraries, not insurance companies
Embrace a "slugging percentage mentality" in venture capital
There's so much more to learn from the past
Be ruthlessly open-minded and challenge assumptions
There is a shortage of new technology in the world
Don't self-identify with beliefs; treat them as abstract objects
It's important to have perspective of people who have it harder
episodes.quotes: "For very little money and for a few hours of time, you can literally learn from somebody's accumulated experience."

episodes.title: Hank Paulson, 74th U.S. Secretary of the Treasury: "It’s the People Skills that Matter"
episodes.summary: Hank Paulson shares his accidental path to becoming an investment banker and CEO, emphasizing the importance of interpersonal relationships over technical skills. He recounts his experience as Treasury Secretary during the 2008 financial crisis, highlighting the difficulty of gaining public support for unpopular but necessary measures. Paulson advises students to prioritize personal growth and finding work that aligns with their values, rather than solely focusing on career engineering. He underscores the significance of building trust and understanding in cross-cultural collaborations, particularly with China.
key_highlights: Accidental career paths can lead to unexpected success
Interpersonal skills are crucial for effective leadership
Don't be afraid to admit mistakes and change course
Build trust and understanding in cross-cultural collaborations
Prioritize personal growth and align work with values
Balance career ambitions with personal life
Embrace change and be wary of career engineering
episodes.quotes: You can't ask the firm to tell you how many hours a week you're going to work. If you're working 50, how about 55, how about 60, how about 65? You have to learn to say no. You have to plan your own life.

episodes.title: Indra Nooyi, PepsiCo Chairman and CEO & Doug McMillon, Walmart President and CEO
episodes.guest_title: PepsiCo Chairman and CEO & Walmart President and CEO
episodes.summary: Indra Nooyi and Doug McMillon discuss the challenges of leading large companies in a rapidly changing world. Nooyi emphasizes the importance of design thinking and the need to connect with consumers on an emotional level, not just through utility. McMillon highlights the need to balance short-term demands with long-term vision, and the importance of staying true to core values. Nooyi shares her approach to change management, which involves helping employees understand the reasons behind the changes and empowering them to contribute to the process.
key_highlights: Design is about romance, drawing consumers to the product
Leaders must be lifelong students, reading widely
Balance short-term demands with long-term vision
Stay true to core values, even when facing external pressures
Embrace change, but respect the company's heritage
Connect with consumers on an emotional level
Actively shape culture, don't just be a custodian
episodes.quotes: Design is something that you embed into a product or a service or an offering that romances the consumer and draws them to the shelf or to the product or to the service or whatever it is.

episodes.title: Arianna Huffington: "We Are Drowning in Data and Starved for Wisdom"
episodes.summary: Arianna Huffington discusses her upbringing in Greece, her early ambition to attend Cambridge, and the importance of sleep. She shares the story of her parents' meeting and how her mother's encouragement helped her overcome obstacles. She also talks about the importance of being a platform for others, and how she created a civil environment at the Huffington Post. She stresses the need to prioritize well-being and sleep, and how it can improve productivity.
key_highlights: Live life as though it were rigged in your favor
Failure is a stepping stone to success
Always be all in
Prioritize your health and sleep
Create a civil environment
Elevate the online conversation
Reimagine journalism
episodes.quotes: Failure is not the opposite of success, it's a stepping stone to success.

episodes.title: Renee James, former President of Intel: "Everybody Who Takes Risks Fails"
episodes.summary: Renee James shares her career journey, from starting as a technical assistant to becoming president of Intel, and her decision to leave after 30 years to pursue her own venture. She emphasizes the importance of intellectual rigor, pattern recognition, and the need for CEOs to understand corporate finance. She also discusses the challenges of promoting diversity in large organizations and the importance of knowing what is safe and good in a world that is less physical.
key_highlights: Intel's manufacturing culture valued discipline and execution over creativity
Mentorship is a two-way street; consider what you offer
It's dangerous to follow a strategy you don't believe in
You need frameworks and tools to think
It's important to know what's safe and good
Being a CEO is about setting strategy and creating a leadership environment
You have to be able to fail
episodes.quotes: "If you ever get to the point where you no longer agree with the strategy, what are you doing?"

episodes.title: American Airlines CEO Doug Parker: Go Take Risks
episodes.summary: Doug Parker, CEO of American Airlines, shares his career journey, emphasizing the importance of relationships and doing what's right for the organization, even if it means taking risks. He recounts his experience leading America West through bankruptcy and the subsequent mergers with US Airways and American Airlines. Parker stresses the importance of treating everyone with respect and dignity, and making decisions that benefit the team, not just oneself.
key_highlights: Focus on what's right for the organization, not just your career
Relationships are key to success
Treat everyone with respect and dignity
Take calculated risks
Be a team player
Communication is key during times of crisis
It's important to adapt to change
episodes.quotes: “A lot of people are in industry that you will find just grew up with jet fuel in their veins and sitting and watching airplanes take off.”

episodes.title: DaVita CEO Kent Thiry on Building a Signature Company Culture
episodes.summary: DaVita CEO Kent Thiry discusses the importance of building a community-first culture, where employees feel emotionally engaged and cared for. He emphasizes the importance of intentionality and transparency in leadership, even when it leads to difficult decisions. Thiry shares a story about a time he had to overrule his team's recommendation to make sure the company's vision was realized. He also shares that the company's culture has made him a better human being.
key_highlights: Community first, company second
Community means caring and respect
Spend more than 50% of your waking hours at work
Work should be central to your life mission
Be emotionally engaged with your coworkers
Raise the bar of expected behavior
Intentionality is fundamental
episodes.quotes: We think it's a grotesque concession that some people have to make, and a lot of people don't have to, and people like the ones in this room are going to have a spectacularly big impact on the folks that work for you through the course of your life.

episodes.title: Former Manchester United Manager Sir Alex Ferguson: Practice, Practice, Practice
episodes.summary: Sir Alex Ferguson discusses his management style, emphasizing the importance of communication, discipline, and trust. He shares anecdotes about managing star players like David Beckham and Cristiano Ronaldo, highlighting the need to balance individual talent with team cohesion. Ferguson also stresses the significance of building a strong foundation, recognizing the contributions of every team member, and maintaining a relentless focus on winning.
key_highlights: Discipline is not a weapon, but a way to educate
Communication and recognition are vital for team success
Trust your instincts in decision-making
Never allow complacency to creep in
Acknowledge and address the human side of players
Focus on building a strong foundation
Always be honest with your players
episodes.quotes: "Practice makes you a real footballer."

episodes.title: Earvin "Magic" Johnson: Understand Your Customers and Over-Deliver
episodes.summary: Earvin "Magic" Johnson discusses how the principles of focus, strategy, and discipline from basketball apply to business. He shares the importance of having mentors, and how his relationship with Dr. Jerry Buss of the Lakers helped him transition to business. Johnson emphasizes knowing your customer and over-delivering to them, even if it means changing your product. He also stresses the importance of resilience and not letting others define you.
key_highlights: Focus, strategy, and discipline are key in both basketball and business
Mentors are critical to success
Know your customer and over-deliver
Be ready to adapt and adjust
Don't let others define you
Be a perfectionist
Take advantage of your platform
episodes.quotes: "The same principles really apply from being a basketball player as well as a CEO. When you think about focus, strategy, discipline, I'm still that same guy that has those same principles as a CEO."

episodes.title: Former NYTimes Executive Editor Jill Abramson on Resilience in Leadership
episodes.guest_title: Former Executive Editor, The New York Times
episodes.summary: Jill Abramson shares her journey from a childhood steeped in reverence for the New York Times to becoming its first female executive editor. She emphasizes the importance of resilience, drawing from her own experience of being fired from the Times and her father's encouragement to persevere. Abramson also reflects on the changing media landscape, highlighting the need for quality journalism and the challenges of balancing business interests with journalistic integrity.
key_highlights: The New York Times took the place of religion in her household
Importance of being true to your values
Investigative reporting is lonely work
Passion for ambitious stories is key
The importance of listening more and talking less
Need to balance business interests with journalistic integrity
Resilience is key to success
episodes.quotes: The New York Times took the place of religion. If the Times said it, it was the absolute truth.

episodes.title: Vinod Khosla, MBA '80: Failure does not matter. Success matters.
episodes.summary: Vinod Khosla, founder of Sun Microsystems and Khosla Ventures, discusses his leadership and investment philosophy. He shares a story of his early days at Sun, when he refused to leave a client's lobby until he secured a meeting, emphasizing the importance of making what you believe happen. Khosla advises against listening to customers when creating innovative products and encourages a willingness to fail, as success is what ultimately matters. He emphasizes the importance of having a strong belief system and internal compass.
key_highlights: Make what you believe happen
Customers aren't always right
Don't always listen to customers
Failure does not matter, success does
Don't be afraid to fail
Have a strong belief system
Have an internal compass
episodes.quotes: Whatever I believe, I should make happen. It's that simple. And if a customer is making a mistake, the customer isn't always right, trust me. If you want to do great products, don't listen to your customers by the way.

episodes.title: Mitt Romney on Leadership: Know Your Values
episodes.guest_title: Former Governor of Massachusetts and U.S. Senator for Utah
episodes.summary: Mitt Romney discusses his career path, his decision to leave Bain Capital to lead the Salt Lake City Olympics, and his run for President. He emphasizes the importance of having something bigger than yourself to believe in, and the value of real-world experience before entering politics. He shares an anecdote of inviting a group of Boy Scouts to his house for a pool party while running Bain Capital, highlighting his commitment to community.
key_highlights: The currency in life is the people you love
Have something bigger than work
Don't compete with your parents
Service is a core value
Follow the career path you enjoy
The goal is to lift more people
Conservative principles help people see rising incomes
episodes.quotes: I believe that God loves all of us, and I believe he loves us as you would love your children. Some are doing naughty things, some are doing nice things, but you love them all.

episodes.title: HP CEO Meg Whitman on Integrity & Courage in Leadership
episodes.summary: Meg Whitman shares her journey from losing a gubernatorial race to becoming the CEO of HP. She emphasizes the importance of understanding a company's founding DNA and aligning financial incentives with strategy. Whitman advises aspiring leaders to focus on what a company does well, and to build a reputation for integrity and doing what you say you will. She also shares why it's important to have perspective, and to remember what really matters in life.
key_highlights: Losing a political race can lead to unexpected opportunities
Companies like HP matter to Silicon Valley, California, and the world
Focus on what a company does right and do more of it
Business principles apply regardless of industry
It's critical to have a technology vision
Win hearts and minds by doing what you say you'll do
Your reputation is key to long-term success
episodes.quotes: "What made you decide to take on the role?" "So it's a good question."

episodes.title: Walmart CEO Doug McMillon on the Impact of Globalization and Culture
episodes.summary: Doug McMillon shares insights on Walmart's global strategy, the importance of culture, and his personal leadership journey. He emphasizes the need for a long-term view, highlighting Walmart's investments in renewable energy and employee development. McMillon recounts his early days unloading trucks and stresses the importance of creating a meritocracy where employees can exceed their expectations. He also discusses the challenge of balancing speed and collective wisdom in decision-making.
key_highlights: Create a meritocracy where employees can exceed their own expectations
Run a great business, and society will benefit
Find ways to bring stores and technology together
Culture is behavior; values must be acted upon
Use the size and scale of the company to engage in sustainability
The only thing that's constant is change
Make important decisions and move on
episodes.quotes: We've been joking the leadership at Walmart about how cool it is to go to work on a day and give 500,000 people a raise. I hope you get to do that someday. It's really, really pretty great.

episodes.title: Gates Foundation CEO Sue Desmond-Hellmann on Fierce Leadership
episodes.summary: Sue Desmond-Hellmann shares her leadership journey, emphasizing the importance of values, risk-taking, and collaboration. She recounts lessons from her parents, her experience studying HIV/AIDS in Uganda, and her time at Genentech. Sue stresses the need for a balance between ambition and self-management, and the value of diverse perspectives in decision-making. She also emphasizes the importance of being a good partner and seeking truth, even when it's uncomfortable.
key_highlights: Fierce love of reading and learning is key
Privilege to be involved in health
Don't be afraid to take risks
Be focused on people who are suffering
Being a good partner is key
episodes.quotes: I want people to say, you know, during my career, I was at the Gates Foundation. Every bit as much as Meg says, you know, I was a product manager at P&G. Because that means that's a special life-changing experience for that person.

episodes.title: Blackstone's Stephen Schwarzman on Hiring Phenomenal People
episodes.summary: Stephen Schwarzman discusses the importance of a leader's integrity and the value of creating a culture where people feel secure and valued. He shares his experience starting Blackstone, emphasizing the need to have a distinctive strategy and the courage to face rejection. He also highlights the importance of continuous learning and adapting to change, even when it means challenging established norms. Schwarzman emphasizes the importance of hiring people who share your values and the need to have a thick skin.
key_highlights: Entrepreneurship is better after success
Immense pain keeps you in touch with the past
Figure out what you're doing before doing it
Being an entrepreneur requires a thick skin
Hire people who are consistent with your values
Strive for excellence
The most important thing is to be honest
episodes.quotes: Being an entrepreneur is much better after you're successful than when you're thinking about it, let alone doing it.

episodes.title: Coursera CEO, Rick Levin: Leaders Must Communicate Their Vision
episodes.summary: Rick Levin, CEO of Coursera, shares his leadership lessons from his time as President of Yale. He emphasizes the importance of articulating a clear vision, testing it with key stakeholders, and then communicating it relentlessly. He shares the story of how Yale's physical infrastructure was crumbling, which led to a loss of market share, and how he worked to address the issue by investing in both the infrastructure and the city of New Haven.
key_highlights: Articulate a clear vision and test it with key stakeholders
Communicate vision relentlessly
Invest in infrastructure and the city
Prioritize economic development
Redress the balance of strengths and weaknesses
episodes.quotes: “The major initiatives that I laid out in my inaugural address turned out to be the things that occupied me for 20 years.”

episodes.title: Sequoia Capital's Doug Leone on Luck & Taking Risks
episodes.summary: Doug Leone shares his journey as an immigrant, emphasizing the role of fear and hard work in his success. He recounts an early job cleaning bathrooms, which solidified his determination to succeed in business. He emphasizes the importance of working with younger people and the need to be a lifelong learner. Leone also discusses the importance of building a team with heterogeneous viewpoints and the value of decentralization.
key_highlights: Small things play pivotal roles
Fear keeps you going
Starts at the bottom
No means but lots of love
I'm now in and now I'm going to get you all
I don't want to hang out with old people
I have to catch myself
episodes.quotes: "I have to catch myself from letting my ego and my insecurities get the best of me, as I want my high school friends who I haven't seen for 40 years realize how wrong they were."

episodes.title: MasterCard CEO Ajay Banga on Taking Risks in Your Life and Career
episodes.summary: Ajay Banga shares his journey from Nestle to MasterCard, emphasizing the importance of learning from everyone and taking risks. He recounts how his father's advice to "be comfortable in your own skin" helped him navigate challenges as a visible minority. Banga stresses the need for a clear strategy, a strong team, and a culture that embraces innovation. He also discusses the importance of financial inclusion and the role of technology in creating a world beyond cash.
key_highlights: Learn something from everyone, regardless of their position
Never take no for an answer
Humility is a key part of success
Take risks with your life and career
Build strong relationships with your team
Focus on what you can control
You can change who you are as you go along
episodes.quotes: "There is always a way to get to the right solution if you apply your mind."

episodes.title: Ron Johnson: Trust in Your Imagination and Instinct
episodes.summary: Ron Johnson discusses his career, from his early days playing sports to his leadership roles at Target, Apple, and JCPenney. He emphasizes the importance of trusting your imagination and instinct, even when it goes against conventional wisdom. Johnson shares his experience building the Apple Store, where the goal was to create a place for community, not just a place to buy products. He also reflects on his time at JCPenney, where he learned the importance of earning trust and the challenges of leading a large, established organization.
key_highlights: Say yes to Stanford
Don't just rely on data
Build relationships to earn trust
Be willing to start again
Innovation is in the eye of the innovator
episodes.quotes: I didn't come here to improve. I came here to transform. Every single leader in America who works to improve their business year after year... can create gradual results, but the only question is, how fast did you improve compared to someone else? You don't fundamentally change your position in the marketplace.

episodes.title: Mindy Grossman, CEO of HSN, Inc: Culture Trumps Strategy
episodes.summary: Mindy Grossman shares her career journey, emphasizing the importance of taking risks and embracing unconventional paths. She recounts her decision to leave law school and pursue a creative field, leading her to HSN. Grossman stresses the significance of culture over strategy, advocating for engaged employees and a strong connection with customers. She describes how she transformed HSN by prioritizing customer engagement, storytelling, and creating a fun, innovative environment.
key_highlights: Culture trumps strategy in building a sustainable company
Unconventional risk-taking can transform your entire life
Know yourself and what gets you excited
Focus on the 'why' behind your decisions
Be a shepherd for your brand and company
Find people with soul, not just talent
episodes.quotes: “Risk-taking and boldness are the essence of transformation.”

episodes.title: Oprah Winfrey on Career, Life, and Leadership
episodes.guest_title: Media Executive, Actress, Talk Show Host, Television Producer, and Philanthropist
episodes.summary: Oprah Winfrey shares her journey from local news anchor to media mogul, emphasizing the importance of knowing what you *don't* want to do. She describes how she learned to trust her instincts, which led her to a career in talk television. Oprah also discusses the importance of having a clear intention to use one's platform to raise consciousness and help others live better lives, and how that has guided her philanthropic work.
key_highlights: Knowing what you *don't* want to do is key
Listen to your instincts
Use your platform to raise consciousness
Philanthropy should come from your being
You need both emotion and logic
episodes.quotes: "You have to know what sparks the light in you so that you, in your own way, can illuminate the world."

episodes.title: Thomas Friedman: Lessons Learned After 20 Years of Writing Columns
episodes.guest_title: Foreign Affairs Columnist, The New York Times
episodes.summary: Thomas Friedman shares his journey as a columnist, emphasizing the importance of having a point of view rooted in personal experience. He discusses the nine types of columns, highlighting the need to challenge one's own base and avoid cynicism. Friedman stresses that a successful column produces a reaction, and that the best columns come from a deep place, written quickly. He concludes with personal lessons, including the value of being a big tipper and always calling your mother.
key_highlights: Have a point of view rooted in personal experience
Challenge your own base
Avoid cynicism
Produce a reaction
Write from a deep place
Always be nice to the help
Always call your mother
episodes.quotes: I have 70 million competitors because we do live in a world where anyone who has access to a blog is now a potential columnist, anyone who has access to Twitter is a reporter, anyone who has access to YouTube is a filmmaker or a paparazzo.

episodes.title: Marc Andreessen on Big Breakthrough Ideas and Courageous Entrepreneurs
episodes.guest_title: Co-Founder of Netscape, Co-Founder & Partner of Andreessen Horowitz
episodes.summary: Marc Andreessen discusses the importance of courage and genius in entrepreneurship, noting that while courage can be learned, genius is innate. He emphasizes the need to look beyond surface-level trends and to focus on building real businesses with strong foundations. He also shares his perspective on the news business, highlighting the shift from a monopoly-driven model to a more competitive landscape.
key_highlights: Markets are more sensitive to bubbles after a crash
Sensitize yourself to bubbles before they happen
There are no silver bullets, only lead bullets
The best entrepreneurs are not coachable
The best entrepreneurs are determined to succeed
The best entrepreneurs are product-centric
episodes.quotes: "We think failure sucks...We think success on the other hand is wonderful."

episodes.title: View From The Top: General (Ret.) Stanley McChrystal
episodes.guest_title: General (Ret.) Stanley McChrystal, Former Commander of U.S. Forces in Afghanistan
episodes.summary: General McChrystal discusses the importance of adaptability in leadership, contrasting it with efficiency. He shares an anecdote about the Maginot Line as an example of efficiency without adaptability. He recounts his experience leading JSOC in Iraq, where he learned that the key to success was not just individual excellence, but also shared consciousness and trust. He emphasizes that it's more important to do the right thing than to do things right.
key_highlights: Adaptability is more important than efficiency
Shared consciousness requires trust and purpose
Drones lower the threshold for using military force
Perceptions matter in the world
A team is not the sum of its parts
episodes.quotes: If you don't violate your processes, the principles you've been taught, people won't criticize you even if you fail. But if you lost, you obviously didn't do the right thing.

episodes.title: CEO Carlos Ghosn of Renault-Nissan Alliance on Innovation
episodes.summary: Carlos Ghosn shares his experience turning around Nissan in 1999, emphasizing the importance of execution over strategy. He recounts how he went to Japan without speaking Japanese and with his family recently relocated to Paris. Ghosn explains his decision to forgo outside consultants, instead focusing on the internal talent and expertise within Nissan. He stresses the importance of empathy and connecting with people across cultures and functions to drive change.
key_highlights: Opportunities come unexpectedly; be ready to make a decision
Success is irrelevant; focus on making a lasting contribution
Solutions come from within the organization
Prioritize execution over strategy
Connect with people; understand their perspective
Make decisions at the right time, not necessarily quickly
episodes.quotes: "I think from time to time, you have opportunities coming you are not expecting them... You just have to make a decision, you take them or not. Independently of how much sense of success you have."

episodes.title: View From The Top: Ken Chenault, CEO of American Express
episodes.summary: Ken Chenault discusses the importance of a strong brand, particularly in the context of American Express, and how it has been maintained over 163 years. He emphasizes the need for a balance between decisiveness and compassion as a leader, especially during times of crisis. Chenault also shares his perspective on the importance of having a higher purpose beyond just selling a product, and how that purpose can drive innovation and long-term success.
key_highlights: Authenticity and humility are critical leadership attributes
Importance of customer service and building a strong brand
Need for both short-term and long-term strategies
The importance of constructive confrontation and transparency
The need to empower people to make a difference
A leader must be an active listener
A leader must be decisive and compassionate
episodes.quotes: "You've got to tell people both the truth, and you don't talk down to people. And you explain to people the reasons why..."

episodes.title: View From The Top: Carlos Brito, CEO, Anheuser-Busch InBev
episodes.summary: Carlos Brito, CEO of Anheuser-Busch InBev, shares his definition of leadership: delivering sustainable results through the team, doing it the right way. He emphasizes that leaders are formed, not born, and that anyone can be a leader if they have a target and need a team to achieve it. Brito stresses the importance of candor and honesty, even when delivering difficult news, and the need to stay humble and constantly seek improvement. He says to be wary of status symbols and to focus on building a strong, unified culture.
key_highlights: Leaders are formed, not born
Leadership is delivering sustainable results through the team
Do things the right way
Don't be selfish, focus on the company
Be a people person, inspire others
Be humble, always seek improvement
Face the brutal facts
episodes.quotes: Leadership is something very simple to define, very hard to live. It's about delivering results on a sustainable basis through the team, doing it the right way.

episodes.title: Michelle Rhee: Lead from the Front
episodes.summary: Michelle Rhee shares her experiences as the former Chancellor of the D.C. Public Schools, emphasizing the importance of courage and conviction in leadership. She recounts a story about receiving advice from Joel Klein, former Chancellor of the New York City school system, to get a boyfriend, and how that advice helped her realize the loneliness of leadership. Rhee stresses the importance of transparency and accountability in education and shares her belief that teachers are the solution to the problems in education.
key_highlights: Be okay with not being liked as a leader
Lead from the front
Prioritize the interests of kids
Unions are doing what they are supposed to do
The most effective strategy is education
Poverty is not an excuse for failing kids
episodes.quotes: "You have to lead from the front... If you get mired in the muck... you're not going to be able to move your organization as far as you can, as fast as you can."

episodes.title: Al Gore: Leaders Must Supply Vision, Values & Goals
episodes.summary: Al Gore discusses the importance of sustainable capitalism, leadership, and the climate crisis. He emphasizes the need for leaders to communicate a clear vision and values, and to focus on long-term goals rather than short-term gains. Gore shares an analogy about the evolution of computing to illustrate how distributed decision-making can be more effective than centralized control. He challenges business leaders to prioritize public goods and address the climate crisis.
key_highlights: True leadership empowers others to say, "We did this ourselves"
Focus on long-term value creation over short-term earnings
Prioritize vision, values, and goals
Sustainable capitalism requires accounting for externalities
The climate crisis demands immediate action
Education and empowerment of women are key to progress
episodes.quotes: Of truly great leaders, the people will say, we did this ourselves.

episodes.title: Jack Dorsey: The Future Has Already Arrived
episodes.summary: Jack Dorsey shares his journey from wanting to be a sailor and tailor to building Twitter and Square. He emphasizes the importance of having a clear vision and working backward from it, loving the work itself, and building products that fade away to allow people to focus on what's most meaningful. He highlights the power of small teams and the value of listening to users to create intuitive products that people are proud to use.
key_highlights: Never grew up wanting to be an entrepreneur
Build products that fade away to allow focus on what matters
Love the work, not just the end product
The future has already arrived, it's just not evenly distributed
Build something for yourself and then convince others to do the same
episodes.quotes: The future has already arrived. It's just not evenly distributed yet.

episodes.title: Chef Thomas Keller: Bouncing Back from Setbacks
episodes.guest_title: Chef & Restaurant Group Owner, Thomas Keller Restaurant Group
episodes.summary: Chef Thomas Keller discusses his journey into the culinary world, starting as a dishwasher in his mother's restaurant. He emphasizes the importance of organization, efficiency, and teamwork, which he learned early on. He shares the story of opening Per Se in New York and the importance of finding the right people and creating a culture of knowledge exchange. Keller also emphasizes the importance of supporting local communities and the need to be adaptable and resilient in the face of setbacks.
key_highlights: Early skills from dishwashing: organization, efficiency, teamwork
Importance of feedback for improvement
Teamwork and reliance on each other
The importance of rituals
Success breeds success
Ignorance can be an asset
Desire over passion
episodes.quotes: "Local has nothing to do with geography. It has to do with ingredients and the quality of the ingredients."

episodes.title: John Doerr: 700 Investments, 192 IPOs, 375,000+ Jobs Created
episodes.summary: John Doerr discusses the importance of a strong team and execution. He shares his experience at Intel, emphasizing the value of operating experience in a rapidly growing company. He advises students to prioritize learning and growth over compensation, build a strong foundation of experiences, and network to develop lifelong relationships. He also emphasizes the importance of culture, advocating for a missionary mindset over a mercenary one.
key_highlights: Look for opportunities to learn and grow, not just compensation
Build a strong foundation of experiences
Always network and develop personal, lifelong networks
Get in on the ground floor of high growth
Swing for the fences when your time is right
Talented, experienced leaders are always in high demand
Culture matters: Missionary vs. Mercenary
episodes.quotes: Instead of paranoia, you can have passion for what you're doing. Paranoia pushes you forward; passion draws you into an opportunity.

episodes.title: Ratan Tata: Moving the Tata Group Beyond India
episodes.guest_title: Former Chairman of the Tata Group
episodes.summary: Ratan Tata discusses his early career, returning to India after studying in the US and the culture shock of starting on the shop floor. He shares the challenges of taking over from his uncle, a titan of Indian business, and the importance of being his own person. He emphasizes the need for businesses to serve a social need and the importance of communication with employees. He also shares the story of a failed attempt to form an airline due to corruption.
key_highlights: Returning to India after 10 years was a cultural shock
Importance of being your own person as a leader
Change requires getting elderly people out of the way
The importance of serving a social need in business
Communication with employees is key
Trust in your team to drive success
Government policy can significantly impact business
episodes.quotes: "I thought of going back to the US and pursuing my career as an architect."

episodes.title: Millard Drexler: "Surround Yourself With People That Get It"
episodes.guest_title: Chairman & CEO of J Crew Group, Inc.
episodes.summary: Millard Drexler shares his journey from working in department stores to leading iconic brands like Gap and J. Crew. He emphasizes the importance of surrounding oneself with people who "get it," and the need to be direct and honest, even if it means being "explosive." Drexler stresses that success is not about test scores or degrees, but about having a vision, being consistent, and taking calculated risks. He also shares his perspective on the changing landscape of retail, particularly the impact of online shopping.
key_highlights: Hire people who understand the brand's vibe
Be direct and honest, even if explosive
Don't be afraid to take calculated risks
Follow your vision and stay consistent
Understand the consumer and their lifestyle
It's the individual, not the degree
episodes.quotes: "More than anybody else, if you look around the room, Mickey Drexler is the reason almost all of you are dressed the way you are."

episodes.title: Andrew Mason: What I've Learned at Groupon
episodes.summary: Andrew Mason shares his entrepreneurial journey, from founding The Point to pivoting to Groupon, and discusses the challenges of scaling a business. He emphasizes the importance of strong leadership and a clear understanding of the business model, especially when dealing with external pressures as a public company. He also highlights the value of a strong company culture in attracting and retaining talent, and in empowering employees to make decisions aligned with the company's long-term strategy.
key_highlights: Simplicity is key; genius of an idea is not always obvious
Culture is a way to scale desired behaviors
The best ideas are one step removed from obvious
Public companies create noise and potential distraction
Internal mechanisms needed to maintain long-term strategy
Culture attracts great people and empowers decision-making
Focus on people, the strongest asset
episodes.quotes: The ideas that are successful aren't always obvious at the time. It's important that you care about those details, especially early on, because you're in the process of establishing your values.

episodes.title: Saudi Aramco CEO: "We Have to Invest Wisely"
episodes.summary: Khalid A. Al-Falih, President and CEO of Saudi Aramco, discusses the company's history, mission, and future strategy. He emphasizes the importance of education and building capacity within the company and the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. He shares a story about the company's first chief geologist, Max Steineke, who was instrumental in discovering oil in Saudi Arabia, and how his actions continue to inspire the company's culture of innovation and risk-taking. He also discusses the importance of environmental stewardship and operational reliability.
key_highlights: Saudi Aramco's mission: Maximize income, facilitate economic expansion, enable a vibrant Saudi energy sector
Education: Develop curiosity, willingness, and ability to constantly renew knowledge
Saudi Aramco's strategic goal: Be the world's most admired energy company
To achieve full potential, Saudi Aramco must get out of the inner city
The most difficult challenge: Reinventing business procedures and systems
The importance of safety, environmental stewardship, and operational reliability
episodes.quotes: “I don't skate to where the puck is, I skate to where the puck is going to be.”

episodes.title: View From The Top: Thomas Friedman
episodes.summary: Thomas Friedman discusses his book, "That Used to Be Us," co-authored with Michael Mandelbaum, which explores America's lost way and how it can return to greatness. He emphasizes the importance of education, infrastructure, and immigration for America's success. Friedman stresses the need for a new social contract that balances capitalism with a strong social safety net, and the importance of creativity and innovation in a hyper-connected world.
key_highlights: America's fate is the biggest foreign policy issue
America needs to be a launching pad for innovation
The importance of trusted voices in a noisy world
The need for a new social contract
"Always be in beta" — lifelong learning and adaptability
Focus on education and infrastructure
The importance of creativity and innovation
episodes.quotes: "It does have a happy ending, we just don't know yet whether it is fiction or non-fiction. That really is going to depend on us."

episodes.title: Turkish President Abdullah Gül: "The age of people's empowerment"
episodes.summary: President Gül discusses the importance of a leader having a vision, and the ability to adapt to change. He shares a story about his grandfather testing his business skills as a child, and how that experience shaped his life. He also emphasizes the importance of listening to the people and responding to their aspirations, even when it means making difficult decisions.
key_highlights: Innovation and quality are synonymous with Stanford
This university is a cradle of creative destruction
The United States is a leader in innovation, science, and technology
Leaders must be able to adapt to change
Leaders must seek the better
Good leaders must be patient and prudent
episodes.quotes: Whoever we are today is the result of many choices that we made through our lives, the failures we suffered, the chances we had, the talents we obtained at birth, and the skills we developed during our life's journey.

episodes.title: View From The Top: Citigroup CEO Vikram Pandit
episodes.summary: Vikram Pandit shares his perspective on the global economy, emphasizing the tremendous uncertainty ahead. He discusses the importance of a strong financial system, the US debt issue, and the need for meaningful employment. He emphasizes the importance of trust, integrity, and responsible finance, and that the most important thing is to create meaningful employment and work for people. He also shares personal insights on the importance of having a strong support system and the value of humility.
key_highlights: Europe is a significant economic issue, costing 1-1.5% in GDP
US debt issue will be addressed after the elections
China's growth rate is slowing, creating uncertainty
Need for 200 million jobs to reach full employment
32 significant elections around the world this year
World is not at full employment
Importance of creating meaningful employment and work
episodes.quotes: The most important thing in business is you need to be sized appropriately for the task at hand. We're only as big as our clients need to be.

episodes.title: View From The Top: Ferrari Chairman Luca Cordero di Montezemolo
episodes.summary: Luca Cordero di Montezemolo shares his leadership philosophy, emphasizing the importance of passion, vision, and demanding excellence. He recounts his experience as Ferrari's racing manager in the 1970s, highlighting the value of teamwork and innovation. He advises students to be curious, open-minded, and to always prioritize the human element over technology. He also emphasizes the importance of maintaining a connection to the past while looking towards the future.
key_highlights: Passion is crucial for success, driving creativity and a forward-thinking mindset
Vision provides clear goals and priorities for the team
Demand excellence from yourself and your team
Be curious and open-minded to new ideas and perspectives
Prioritize the human element over technology
Maintain a connection to the past while looking towards the future
Teamwork and coordination are essential for success
episodes.quotes: You need fantastic people. Very easy to say, difficult to do. Behind fantastic products, whatever the products are, you need fantastic people.

episodes.title: Stanley McChrystal: Leadership is a Choice
episodes.guest_title: Partner, McChrystal Group; Former Commander, US and International Forces in Afghanistan
episodes.summary: General McChrystal discusses the challenges of leadership in a complex, constantly changing environment, drawing on his military experience. He emphasizes the importance of building trust and shared consciousness within an organization, and of fostering a culture that values individual contributions. He shares a story about his wife falling asleep during a speech he gave at Oxford, and how that experience helped him keep his ego in check. He also discusses the importance of having a clear definition of success and aligning strategy accordingly.
key_highlights: Leadership is a choice, not a talent or gift
America doesn't lead well anymore
Importance of shared consciousness and purpose
Build relationships, not just networks
Time and energy management is key
Trust one who has gone through it
episodes.quotes: Leadership is not a talent or a gift - it's a choice.

episodes.title: Ian Davis: View from the Top
episodes.summary: Ian Davis shares his perspectives on leadership, which he defines as a non-hierarchical, multifaceted concept that is context-driven. He emphasizes that leadership is about what leaders do, not what they are, and that it's best understood from the perspective of followers. He stresses the importance of setting a clear direction, initiating action, and sustaining momentum. He also shares tips on how to become a more effective personal leader, including understanding your context and being self-aware.
key_highlights: Leadership is a non-hierarchical concept, independent of position
Leadership is multifaceted; consider different types
Focus on what leaders do, not what they are
Set a clear direction, initiate action, sustain momentum
Understand the context: history, crises, people, culture, values
Don't confuse positions of leadership with the exercise of leadership
Nurture leadership in others; give leadership opportunities
episodes.quotes: Leadership is a non-hierarchical concept. People often confuse positions of leadership with the exercise of leadership.

episodes.title: Kent Thiry: View from the Top
episodes.summary: Kent Thiry emphasizes that companies can be a formidable force for good while still fulfilling their capitalist responsibilities. He shares a story about how his company had to develop a new language to communicate its values and mission to its employees. He also discusses the importance of creating a culture where people feel like they co-own the company and are empowered to make decisions.
key_highlights: Companies can be a formidable force for good without sacrificing capitalist responsibilities
Leadership is in the eyes of the led
Intentionality rules
People don't care how much you know until they know how much you care
This is not a dress rehearsal, this is your life
episodes.quotes: You are the type of leader other people experience you to be. It doesn't matter what kind of leader you think you are.

episodes.title: Bill George: Leaders Need High Emotional IQ to Succeed
episodes.summary: Bill George, former Chairman and CEO of Medtronic, shares his perspective on leadership, emphasizing the importance of authenticity and serving others. He critiques the "leader as hero" model and encourages leaders to build collaborative environments. He emphasizes the importance of self-awareness, having a group of trusted people around you, and focusing on the mission and purpose of the organization.
key_highlights: Real innovation comes from small bets, not betting the farm on one R&D program
Leaders should empower others to step up and lead
Leaders should put the organization's interests ahead of their own
Leaders should choose people for character over charisma
Leaders should have a metric of restoring people to full life and health
episodes.quotes: When you leave this world, the only thing you can take with you is what you leave behind.

episodes.title: Jeff Smisek: CEO's Role in Building Company Culture
episodes.guest_title: Former Chairman, President, and CEO, United Continental Holdings
episodes.summary: Jeff Smisek discusses the airline industry, highlighting its structural problems and the importance of culture. He emphasizes the need for a simple, clear plan that everyone understands, as well as the importance of treating people with dignity and respect. Smisek shares his personal leadership philosophy, which involves hiring talented individuals, giving them the tools to succeed, and trusting them to do their jobs effectively. He also emphasizes the need to be direct, open, and honest with both employees and customers.
key_highlights: Airlines have low barriers to entry, high barriers to exit
Low returns on invested capital since the Wright Brothers
Brutally regulated, even post-deregulation
The importance of a simple, clear plan
Hire talented people and trust them
Treat people with dignity and respect
Follow your passion, ignore the herd
episodes.quotes: If you don't know where you're going, any road will take you there. Follow your passion, ignore the herd, and be yourself.

episodes.title: Alan Mulally of Ford: Leaders Must Serve, with Courage
episodes.guest_title: Former President and CEO, Ford Motor Company
episodes.summary: Alan Mulally shares his experience turning around Ford Motor Company in 2006, emphasizing the importance of a clear vision, comprehensive strategy, and relentless implementation. He recounts how he fostered a culture of transparency and teamwork, enabling the company to overcome a dire financial situation. Mulally stresses the need for leaders to serve, make tough decisions, and maintain a laser focus on the brand, quality, and fuel efficiency.
key_highlights: Leaders must serve, not command
Transparency and teamwork are essential
Focus on the core brand
Invest in the product, even in crisis
Data sets you free
Courage to make tough decisions
Compelling vision rallies the team
episodes.quotes: "It's not about what Ford has done up through 1925, but this is all about the opportunity to serve all the people around the world."

episodes.title: Former CEO Mulcahy on Turning Xerox Around
episodes.summary: Anne Mulcahy, former CEO of Xerox, shares her experience leading the company through a crisis in the late 1990s. She emphasizes the importance of transparency, communication, and building trust with employees and customers during challenging times. Mulcahy also highlights the need for leaders to be grounded in the core values of the company and to make tough decisions based on those values, even if they are unpopular.
key_highlights: Partnership with Stanford University was key for Xerox
Focus on lessons learned, not just telling the story
Leadership at a time of crisis
Xerox stock was outperforming the market in 1998-99
Accounting irregularities in Mexico led to SEC investigation
Good leadership can move mountains over time
Crisis is a powerful motivator
episodes.quotes: "I often say we were early adopters of the corporate crisis."

episodes.title: AB InBev's Brito: 'Hire the Right People'
episodes.summary: Carlos Brito discusses building a performance culture by creating a dream, hiring great people, and fostering a culture of ownership. He emphasizes the importance of candor and constructive feedback, drawing parallels between sports and business. Brito advocates for taking people out of their comfort zones to promote growth and innovation, and for leaders to focus on the big picture while empowering their teams to execute.
key_highlights: Create a dream, not just a vision, to inspire passion
Hire great people, not just those who agree with you
Foster a culture of ownership, not just professionalism
Be candid and give constructive feedback
Take people out of their comfort zone to promote growth
Focus on the big picture, not just the details
Leaders must empower their teams to execute
episodes.quotes: The best kept secret in our company is that we have a dream, we have great people, and we have a culture of ownership. It's very simple, but it's at the same time very tough to do it on a daily basis.

episodes.title: Siemens AG's Löscher: Leading During Crisis
episodes.summary: Peter Löscher, CEO of Siemens, discusses the key traits of good leaders, including clarity of direction, the ability to break down vision into actionable steps, passion, determination, and a strong personal moral compass. He shares his experience taking over Siemens during a crisis, emphasizing the importance of differentiating between a leadership crisis and a company culture issue. He also describes the importance of building a diverse team and engaging with stakeholders at all levels of the organization to drive innovation.
key_highlights: Clarity of direction and vision is key for good leaders
Break down vision into actionable steps
Passion is essential to rally teams
Be determined in pursuing goals
Have a true north for yourself
Challenge the organization and the environment
Make the possible, possible in the fastest time
episodes.quotes: "Live your dreams and make sure that you live up to the capacity and to what you have as individuals and make a real contribution through the work what you do for others."

episodes.title: Don Valentine, Sequoia Capital: "Target Big Markets"
episodes.summary: Don Valentine shares his experience investing in companies like Apple, Google, and YouTube. He emphasizes the importance of targeting large markets, and investing in companies that are solving big problems. He also stresses the importance of storytelling and being able to articulate the value proposition of a company in a concise and compelling way. He also shares his perspective on the importance of having a strong technical team and a focus on cash flow.
key_highlights: Focus on the market, not just the technology
Target big markets to build big companies
Storytelling is crucial for attracting investment
Prioritize technology and engineering skills
Focus on cash flow, not just balance sheets
Recessions are great times to invest
episodes.quotes: We have always focused on the market… The size of the market, the dynamics of the market, the nature of the competition, because our objective always was to build big companies.

episodes.title: Jeff Immelt of GE: Leaders Must Drive Change
episodes.summary: Jeff Immelt, CEO of GE, discusses the attributes of good leaders in the current generation. He emphasizes the importance of having a point of view on global economic trends and investing around that point of view. He shares five themes he thinks about from the standpoint of running GE, including the reordering of the global economy, productivity and cost, resource scarcity, the networked world, and the state economy. He also shares the importance of analytical listening, adaptability, and perseverance.
key_highlights: Developed world generated 80% of economic growth in the 80s
Emerging markets will generate 80% of growth in the next decade
Europe will be a slower growth region
Problems have a way of getting solved
Need more products at more price points
You have to have a point of view
Good leaders have a deep understanding of themselves
episodes.quotes: If you want to do something important, people are going to hate you more days than they like you.

episodes.title: Jonathan Reckford, CEO, Habitat for Humanity
episodes.summary: Jonathan Reckford shares his journey from Goldman Sachs to leading Habitat for Humanity. He emphasizes the importance of role models and the need to define personal values to navigate career twists. Reckford reflects on the challenges of managing a large volunteer organization, balancing mission with accountability, and the importance of a "holy discontent" to drive social impact.
key_highlights: Education at Stanford opened doors to major companies
The story is never as neat as the resume
Navigating twists and turns is a collective challenge
All successful leaders identify individuals to emulate
Be a fanatic about great customer service
Don't let others define what a good life is
Lead from passion, not just position
episodes.quotes: "May God bless you with enough foolishness to believe that you can make a difference in this world, so that you can do what others say cannot be done."

episodes.title: Ted Turner: Take Care of the Planet
episodes.summary: Ted Turner shares his journey from billboard advertising to cable television, launching CNN, and his philanthropic work with the United Nations Foundation. He emphasizes the importance of courage in business, the value of integrity, and the need for a sustainable approach to resource management. Turner advocates for population planning, renewable energy, and a shift in societal values towards helping others.
key_highlights: Courage is essential for success in business
Integrity is a core value
Sustainable resource management is crucial
Population planning is necessary
Shift societal values to helping others
Government should work better
The importance of education
episodes.quotes: "My honor is my life; we live in one. Take honor from me, and my life is done."

episodes.title: Ken Chenault of AmEx: Define Reality and Give Hope
episodes.summary: Ken Chenault shares his leadership lessons from navigating crises like 9/11 and the 2008 financial crisis. He emphasizes the importance of defining reality and giving hope, drawing inspiration from Napoleon. He discusses how AmEx has evolved beyond a payments company by leveraging data to facilitate value for customers and merchants. Chenault stresses the value of constructive confrontation and the need for leaders to create an environment where people feel comfortable challenging ideas.
key_highlights: Role of a leader is to define reality and give hope
Tenure has been confronting some of the most challenging crises
Motto from Napoleon: Define reality and give hope
Grounded in reality, give strategies, ideas, and values
Importance of reputation and trust
Analyze and communicate the reality of the situation
Value of constructive confrontation
episodes.quotes: The role of a leader is to define reality and give hope. If in fact you want to have an inspired workforce, you have to make sure that people understand that you're grounded in reality, but then you have to give them the strategies, the ideas, and the values that give them reasons to be helpful.

episodes.title: Carlos Ghosn of Nissan/Renault: Look Ahead, Dont Stand Still
episodes.guest_title: Chairman & CEO, Nissan Motor Co. and Renault
episodes.summary: Carlos Ghosn discusses his experiences leading Nissan and Renault, emphasizing the importance of adapting leadership styles to different cultural contexts. He shares his approach to turning around Nissan, stressing the need for a lucid diagnosis of the situation and the importance of listening to the team. Ghosn advises newly minted MBAs to avoid over-planning their careers and to embrace flexibility, as well as to focus on working for themselves rather than seeking external recognition.
key_highlights: Enjoy applause before, rather than after, a presentation
